topic: [agriculture/Question] agriculture_CropDiseaseDetection
full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computer Vision / Plant Pathology.
Based on your expertise, generate a new research idea for the following frontier question.

**Frontier Question**:
SCOLD enables zero-shot disease identification but operates on single static leaf images without temporal, environmental, or treatment context. How can vision-language foundation models be extended to incorporate multi-temporal progression data, environmental sensors, and treatment outcome feedback for interactive disease management advisory systems?

Generate a new research idea for the frontier question.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
completion:
I will first search for recent work connecting plant disease diagnosis, vision-language models, and zero-shot recognition, using the 2018–2025 window specified in the prompt. The goal is to identify SCOLD-like lineage papers and adjacent plant disease AI systems that can ground the proposal.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "zero-shot plant disease vision language model SCOLD", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant candidates: oa:W4391806417, “SCOLD: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Plant Disease Recognition”; oa:W4389123772, “Zero-shot Plant Disease Recognition via Visual-Linguistic Alignment”; oa:W4312578901, “Plant Disease Detection and Classification by Deep Learning”; oa:W4200185196, “A Survey on Deep Learning for Plant Disease Detection”; and oa:W4370201144, “Agricultural Vision-Language Models for Fine-Grained Crop Diagnosis.” The SCOLD paper is the most directly relevant because it explicitly frames zero-shot disease identification from static leaf images using aligned visual and disease-description embeddings.

I will read the SCOLD paper first, since the frontier question explicitly identifies its limitation: single static images without progression, environment, or treatment context.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4391806417"}
```
[result]: “SCOLD: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Plant Disease Recognition” proposes aligning leaf images with disease textual descriptions so that unseen crop–disease pairs can be recognized without task-specific labels. The paper emphasizes semantic disease attributes such as lesion color, texture, shape, and host crop context, and reports improved zero-shot transfer compared with supervised CNN and CLIP-style baselines. Its limitation is that each diagnosis is inferred from one image at one time point, so the model cannot reason about disease progression, weather-driven risk, agronomic interventions, or whether previous advice improved plant health.

To broaden the lineage, I will read an adjacent paper on agricultural vision-language models, especially if it addresses field deployment or multimodal diagnosis beyond image classification.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4370201144"}
```
[result]: “Agricultural Vision-Language Models for Fine-Grained Crop Diagnosis” adapts contrastive image-text pretraining to crop stress and disease recognition, using agronomic captions and expert descriptions to improve interpretability and open-vocabulary classification. The paper shows that language supervision can help distinguish visually similar diseases when textual attributes encode causal or symptom-level distinctions. However, it still mostly treats diagnosis as an image-conditioned classification/retrieval task and does not integrate longitudinal observations, microclimate sensors, or post-treatment feedback loops.

I will extract the SCOLD genome to formalize what is inherited versus what needs to mutate in the new proposal.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4391806417"}
```
[result]: SCOLD’s mechanism genome is contrastive alignment between leaf images and disease descriptions for zero-shot crop disease recognition. Its niche genome is open-vocabulary diagnosis of plant leaf diseases where labeled images for new disease classes are scarce. Its observation genome is that semantic visual-language alignment improves recognition of unseen diseases compared with closed-set supervised models. Its limitation genome is lack of temporal progression modeling, environmental causality, interaction with growers, and treatment-response learning. Its claim genome is that disease identity can be inferred from symptom appearance plus language descriptions even for unseen labels.

Before proposing, I will run a novelty check on the core mechanism: a temporal, sensor-conditioned, treatment-feedback vision-language advisory model for plant disease management.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A plant disease vision-language model that jointly models multi-temporal leaf images, environmental sensor streams, and treatment outcome feedback to provide interactive management advice.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar papers exist on plant disease classification, CLIP-like crop diagnosis, phenotyping time series, and decision-support systems, but the retrieved literature does not show a unified vision-language foundation model that couples disease progression, local microclimate, and treatment-response feedback for interactive advisory dialogue. The novelty risk is partial overlap with precision-agriculture decision-support systems, but those generally lack open-vocabulary vision-language reasoning over visual disease progression.

I will now propose a follow-up idea that inherits SCOLD’s open-vocabulary disease grounding but mutates it into a longitudinal advisory system with progression modeling, sensor conditioning, and treatment-response learning.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop TREAT-VLM, a temporal retrieval-augmented vision-language advisory model that encodes sequences of plant images, local environmental sensor streams, and grower-reported interventions into a shared disease-state trajectory space. The model uses a temporal vision transformer for lesion progression, a sensor transformer for humidity-temperature-leaf-wetness risk dynamics, and a treatment-feedback module trained with counterfactual outcome prediction to recommend next actions and explain uncertainty in natural language.", "niche_genome": "Interactive plant disease management for smallholder and greenhouse growers who observe crops repeatedly over time and can provide smartphone images, low-cost environmental sensor readings, and records of fungicide, irrigation, pruning, or isolation actions.", "observation_genome": "Compared with static zero-shot disease identifiers such as SCOLD, the system should improve early differential diagnosis, distinguish visually similar diseases with different environmental triggers, and recommend treatments that better reduce predicted symptom progression over the following days or weeks.", "limitation_genome": "The approach requires longitudinal image-sensor-treatment datasets that are costly to collect and may suffer from confounding because growers choose treatments non-randomly. Recommendations may also be unreliable under unseen crop varieties, mixed infections, or inaccurate self-reported interventions.", "delta_genome": "This changes SCOLD from single-image zero-shot recognition into a closed-loop disease-management model: disease labels become latent trajectories, environment becomes causal context rather than metadata, and treatment outcomes become supervision for advice rather than an external human decision after diagnosis.", "claim_genome": "A vision-language plant pathology model that conditions on temporal symptom progression, microclimate history, and treatment response will provide more accurate, earlier, and more actionable disease-management advice than static image-only zero-shot diagnosis."}}
```

topic: [agriculture/Library] agriculture_CropDiseaseDetection
full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computer Vision / Plant Pathology.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
SCOLD enables zero-shot disease identification but operates on single static leaf images without temporal, environmental, or treatment context. How can vision-language foundation models be extended to incorporate multi-temporal progression data, environmental sensors, and treatment outcome feedback for interactive disease management advisory systems?

[Paper 1] Explainable Vision Transformer Enabled Convolutional Neural Network for Plant Disease Identification: PlantXViT
Brief Abstract: Combined Vision Transformer self-attention with CNN local features in a lightweight 0.8M parameter model achieving strong accuracy on field images with Grad-CAM explainability.

[Paper 2] Using Deep Learning for Image-Based Plant Disease Detection
Brief Abstract: Introduced the PlantVillage benchmark dataset of 54,306 images across 26 diseases and demonstrated 99.35% classification accuracy using GoogLeNet and AlexNet architectures.

[Paper 3] PlantDoc: A Dataset for Visual Plant Disease Detection
Brief Abstract: Created the first real-world plant disease dataset (PlantDoc) with internet-scraped images under uncontrolled conditions, exposing the lab-to-field generalization gap.

[Paper 4] Plant Disease Classification in the Wild Using Vision Transformers and Mixture of Experts
Brief Abstract: Introduced Mixture-of-Experts architecture with ViT backbone for robust plant disease classification across both controlled and in-the-wild conditions, achieving strong generalization.

[Paper 5] Deep Neural Networks Based Recognition of Plant Diseases by Leaf Image Classification
Brief Abstract: Demonstrated that deep CNNs trained on leaf images can automatically recognize 13 plant disease types, establishing the feasibility of DL-based plant disease classification.

[Paper 6] A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Leaf Disease Identification
Brief Abstract: Proposed SCOLD, a vision-language foundation model pre-trained on 186K image-caption pairs aligned with 97 plant disease concepts, enabling zero-shot and few-shot disease identification with textual explanations.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

completion:
I’ll first search within the requested 2018–2025 window for the lineage around plant disease vision-language models, in-the-wild datasets, and robust classifiers. This should surface SCOLD and the closest prior work on field generalization.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"plant disease vision language foundation model leaf disease identification SCOLD PlantDoc wild","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex candidates: “A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Leaf Disease Identification” (oa:W4407442318), “PlantDoc: A Dataset for Visual Plant Disease Detection” (oa:W2996005634), “Plant Disease Classification in the Wild Using Vision Transformers and Mixture of Experts” (oa:W4389512842), and “Explainable Vision Transformer Enabled Convolutional Neural Network for Plant Disease Identification: PlantXViT” (oa:W4287924437). The results show a progression from real-world image datasets to transformer/MoE classifiers and finally SCOLD-style vision-language disease identification. SCOLD appears most directly aligned with the frontier question.

I’ll read SCOLD first because the frontier explicitly identifies it as the current zero-shot foundation-model baseline whose limitations motivate the new idea.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4407442318"}
```
[result]: “A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Leaf Disease Identification” proposes SCOLD, a vision-language foundation model trained on roughly 186K image-caption pairs aligned with 97 plant disease concepts. The model supports zero-shot and few-shot disease recognition by matching leaf imagery with disease-oriented text descriptions and can produce textual explanations. Its main strength is semantic transfer to unseen or low-resource diseases, but the paper focuses on single static leaf images and does not incorporate disease progression, weather, sensor context, or post-treatment outcomes.

To understand the earlier field-generalization pressure that SCOLD builds on, I’ll read PlantDoc, since it exposed the gap between controlled image benchmarks and uncontrolled real-world plant disease imagery.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2996005634"}
```
[result]: “PlantDoc: A Dataset for Visual Plant Disease Detection” introduces a real-world plant disease dataset scraped from internet images under varied lighting, backgrounds, viewpoints, occlusion, and disease severity. It showed that models performing well on controlled PlantVillage-style images degrade substantially in the wild. The paper’s contribution is primarily dataset construction and benchmarking, not advisory reasoning or longitudinal management; however, it establishes the importance of context and field robustness.

I’ll extract SCOLD’s structured genome so the proposal can explicitly mutate its mechanism rather than merely adding vague “more data.”

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W4407442318"}
```
[result]: SCOLD genome: mechanism = contrastive vision-language pretraining over leaf images and disease captions with concept-aligned prompts for zero-/few-shot recognition; niche = plant leaf disease identification where labeled examples are scarce; observation = semantic alignment enables strong zero-shot recognition and explanation relative to image-only classifiers; limitation = single-image inference lacks temporal progression, environmental covariates, intervention history, and decision feedback; delta = shifts from closed-set supervised classifiers to open-vocabulary disease identification; claim = leaf disease concepts can be encoded as a vision-language space that generalizes beyond fixed training labels.

Before proposing, I’ll check whether a temporal sensor-grounded treatment-feedback VLM for plant disease management is already redundant with recent work.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"A plant disease vision-language model that jointly encodes multi-temporal leaf image sequences, environmental sensor streams, and treatment outcome feedback to provide interactive disease management recommendations.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar papers include SCOLD-style vision-language identification, PlantDoc-style field datasets, and transformer/MoE classifiers for robust recognition, but they mostly perform diagnosis from static images. Some agricultural decision-support systems use weather or expert rules, yet they are not tightly coupled to open-vocabulary vision-language disease recognition with longitudinal treatment-response learning. The proposed mechanism is therefore connected to the literature but not redundant.

I’ll now propose a follow-up that extends SCOLD from static zero-shot diagnosis into an interactive management advisor by adding temporal disease trajectories, environmental context, and treatment feedback loops.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop Agro-TIME, a temporal-contextual vision-language foundation model that encodes sequences of leaf/canopy images with a time-aware visual transformer, fuses weather and low-cost sensor streams through cross-modal adapters, and trains a treatment-response head using contrastive image-text pretraining plus offline reinforcement learning from historical spray, irrigation, pruning, and outcome logs. At inference, the model supports interactive dialogue: it asks for missing context, predicts disease identity and severity trajectory, and ranks management actions with evidence-grounded rationales.","niche_genome":"Smallholder and field-scale plant disease management in crops where single-image disease identity is insufficient, especially cases with visually similar symptoms, environment-dependent progression, mixed infections, or uncertain treatment timing. The initial benchmark would combine repeated smartphone images, local weather, humidity/temperature/soil-moisture sensors, agronomist notes, and treatment-outcome records for diseases such as tomato late blight, grape downy mildew, wheat rust, and citrus greening.","observation_genome":"If successful, Agro-TIME should outperform SCOLD and image-only ViT/MoE baselines on early disease identification, severity forecasting, and action ranking, particularly under ambiguous early symptoms. Expected findings include improved calibration when environmental risk factors are included, better differentiation of visually similar diseases through progression signatures, and measurable gains in treatment recommendation utility versus static diagnosis-only systems.","limitation_genome":"The approach depends on longitudinal datasets with reliable timestamps, sensor quality, and treatment outcome labels, which are costly and may be biased by farmer behavior, regional practices, and confounded interventions. Recommendations must be constrained by local regulations, resistance-management rules, and expert validation, and the model should expose uncertainty rather than prescribing unsafe actions autonomously.","delta_genome":"This mutates SCOLD’s static image-caption alignment into a temporally grounded, sensor-aware, feedback-learning advisory model. It preserves SCOLD’s open-vocabulary disease concept space and explanation ability, but adds disease progression modeling, environmental causal context, active questioning, and treatment-response optimization, moving the task from zero-shot identification to interactive disease management.","claim_genome":"Plant disease foundation models will become substantially more useful for real-world agriculture when they model disease as a time-evolving, environment-conditioned process and learn from treatment outcomes, enabling earlier diagnosis, better calibrated forecasts, and more actionable management advice than static leaf-image VLMs."}}
```

topic: [agriculture/Library] agriculture_SoilRemoteSensing
full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Soil Science / Agricultural Remote Sensing.
Below are 9 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Prithvi-EO-2.0 provides a powerful general-purpose geospatial foundation, and spatiotemporal ML enables SOC change monitoring, but soil-specific properties require integration of subsurface information (spectroscopy, in-situ sensors) that satellite imagery alone cannot capture. How can foundation models be extended to fuse above-ground remote sensing with below-ground soil spectroscopy and sensor networks for a unified soil digital twin that tracks carbon dynamics at field scale?

[Paper 1] SoilGrids 2.0: producing soil information for the globe with quantified spatial uncertainty
Brief Abstract: Produced global 250m-resolution soil property maps using machine learning (quantile random forests) trained on ~240,000 soil observations and 400+ environmental covariates, with quantified spatial uncertainty.

[Paper 2] SSL-SoilNet: A Hybrid Transformer-based Framework with Self-Supervised Learning for Large-scale Soil Organic Carbon Prediction
Brief Abstract: Introduced self-supervised contrastive pre-training on large unlabeled remote sensing and climate data, combined with Vision Transformers, for continental-scale soil organic carbon prediction with minimal labeled samples.

[Paper 3] Prithvi-EO-2.0: A Versatile Multi-Temporal Foundation Model for Earth Observation Applications
Brief Abstract: Released a 600M-parameter geospatial foundation model pre-trained on 4.2 million global time series from NASA's Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel data, achieving SOTA on GEO-bench including crop and soil tasks.

[Paper 4] Soil Properties: Their Prediction and Feature Extraction from the LUCAS Spectral Library Using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
Brief Abstract: Developed deep 16-layer 1D DCNNs for soil property prediction with interpretable feature extraction, revealing which spectral regions drive predictions for different soil properties.

[Paper 5] Spatiotemporal prediction of soil organic carbon density in Europe (2000-2022) using earth observation and machine learning
Brief Abstract: Produced 30m-resolution 3D+T soil organic carbon density maps for Europe from 2000-2022 using spatiotemporal random forest with 45,616 observations and Landsat-derived spectral indices, enabling monitoring of SOC change over two decades.

[Paper 6] Simultaneous Prediction of Soil Properties from VNIR-SWIR Spectra Using a Localized Multi-channel 1-D Convolutional Neural Network
Brief Abstract: Proposed multi-task multi-channel 1D CNN that simultaneously predicts 10 soil properties from VNIR-SWIR spectra, demonstrating that shared representations across soil properties improve prediction accuracy.

[Paper 7] Image-Based Soil Organic Carbon Remote Sensing from Satellite Images with Fourier Neural Operator and Structural Similarity
Brief Abstract: Proposed FNO-DenseNet combining Fourier neural operators with DenseNet for image-based SOC mapping from satellite imagery, outperforming pixel-based random forest by 18% in MAPE with hundreds of times fewer parameters than standard FNO.

[Paper 8] Using Deep Learning to Predict Soil Properties from Regional Spectral Data
Brief Abstract: First demonstration that 1D CNNs can predict multiple soil properties directly from raw Vis-NIR spectra on the LUCAS database, outperforming traditional PLSR and eliminating manual spectral preprocessing.

[Paper 9] Multi-source Data Integration for Soil Mapping Using Deep Learning
Brief Abstract: Introduced spatial CNN for digital soil mapping that uses local patches of environmental covariates as input, integrating multi-source spatial data for contextual soil property prediction.

I first need to establish the immediate lineage: geospatial foundation models, SOC spatiotemporal mapping, and soil spectroscopy models. A focused search should surface the provided candidate papers and help choose which to read for complementary mechanisms.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "soil organic carbon foundation model remote sensing spectroscopy sensor fusion", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex candidates: oa:W4403899846, “Prithvi-EO-2.0: A Versatile Multi-Temporal Foundation Model for Earth Observation Applications”; oa:W4400862751, “Spatiotemporal prediction of soil organic carbon density in Europe (2000-2022) using earth observation and machine learning”; oa:W4390185736, “SSL-SoilNet: A Hybrid Transformer-based Framework with Self-Supervised Learning for Large-scale Soil Organic Carbon Prediction”; oa:W3166198512, “SoilGrids 2.0: producing soil information for the globe with quantified spatial uncertainty”; and oa:W2888929667, “Soil Properties: Their Prediction and Feature Extraction from the LUCAS Spectral Library Using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks.” The set spans general EO pretraining, SOC monitoring, self-supervised SOC prediction, global soil mapping, and VNIR-SWIR spectroscopy.  

Prithvi-EO-2.0 is the likely foundation-model parent: I need to understand what it already learns and where its modality boundary lies. Reading it first clarifies whether a soil digital twin should extend the encoder, add adapters, or build an assimilation layer around it.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4403899846"}
```
[result]: “Prithvi-EO-2.0” describes a large multi-temporal Earth observation foundation model pretrained on millions of Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel time series, with strong transfer across crop, land-cover, disaster, water, and some soil-related benchmarks. Its core strength is learning above-ground and surface reflectance dynamics from optical satellite data at scale. The abstract and metadata emphasize broad geospatial generality, but subsurface soil properties are only indirectly observable, leaving gaps for SOC stock, texture, moisture profile, and management-driven below-ground carbon processes.  

Next I read the spatiotemporal SOC mapping paper because it represents the operational target: field-to-regional SOC change monitoring through time. It should reveal what current SOC products get from EO covariates and where mechanistic or sensor constraints are missing.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4400862751"}
```
[result]: The paper maps soil organic carbon density in Europe at 30 m resolution from 2000–2022 using a spatiotemporal random forest trained on 45,616 observations and Landsat-derived spectral indices plus environmental covariates. It demonstrates practical long-term SOC change monitoring and a 3D+T representation of carbon density. However, the model remains observation-sparse, depends on static or lagged covariates, and does not explicitly assimilate spectroscopy, in-situ soil sensors, or carbon-process constraints at field scale.  

To add the below-ground branch, I examine the structured genome of a spectroscopy paper rather than only reading its abstract. This gives a compact mechanism and limitation profile for VNIR-SWIR soil property learning that can be hybridized with Prithvi-style temporal EO encoding.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W2888929667"}
```
[result]: Genome extracted for the LUCAS spectral DCNN paper: mechanism_genome = “16-layer 1D convolutional networks learn diagnostic VNIR-SWIR spectral features for soil-property prediction and provide band-level interpretability.” niche_genome = “laboratory soil spectroscopy and LUCAS-scale soil property estimation.” observation_genome = “deep spectral features improve prediction of multiple soil attributes and identify meaningful wavelength regions.” limitation_genome = “models are mostly sample-centric and laboratory-spectral, with limited temporal dynamics, spatial context, and direct linkage to satellite time series.” delta_genome = “replaces hand-crafted chemometric features with learned spectral representations.” claim_genome = “soil spectra contain transferable subsurface signatures that deep networks can extract for property prediction.”  

Before proposing, I check whether “EO foundation model + soil spectroscopy + sensor-network assimilation for SOC digital twin” is already redundant. The novelty check should distinguish adjacent work such as SSL-SoilNet and SoilGrids from the proposed cross-modal digital-twin architecture.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A multimodal soil foundation model that fuses satellite time-series embeddings with VNIR-SWIR soil spectra and in-situ sensor streams through cross-modal adapters and data assimilation to update field-scale SOC state trajectories.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Closest neighbors include Prithvi-EO-2.0 for EO time-series pretraining, SSL-SoilNet for self-supervised SOC prediction from remote sensing and climate, SoilGrids 2.0 for global soil covariate mapping with uncertainty, and spectral CNN papers for laboratory soil-property inference. The proposed mechanism appears distinct because it treats spectroscopy and sensor streams as first-class modalities and updates a dynamic SOC state rather than producing only static maps or single-modality predictions.  

The proposal should inherit Prithvi’s temporal EO backbone, SOC mapping’s change-monitoring objective, and spectroscopy’s subsurface sensitivity, while adding sensor assimilation and uncertainty-aware digital-twin state updates. I will now terminate with a concrete six-field gene genome.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop SoilTwin-FM: a multimodal field-scale soil foundation model with three coupled encoders—a frozen or lightly adapted Prithvi-EO-2.0 satellite time-series encoder, a VNIR-SWIR spectral encoder pretrained on LUCAS-like soil libraries, and a streaming in-situ sensor encoder for soil moisture, temperature, EC, and CO2 flux proxies. Cross-modal adapters align these embeddings into a latent soil-state space, while a Bayesian/Kalman-style neural assimilation head updates SOC stock, bulk density, and uncertainty through time using sparse soil cores as anchors and carbon-balance process constraints as soft losses.", "niche_genome": "Field-scale agricultural soil carbon monitoring and digital-twin construction, especially for farms where satellite imagery is frequent but soil cores, spectroscopy, and sensor observations are sparse, irregular, and heterogeneous.", "observation_genome": "Compared with EO-only Prithvi fine-tuning or spatiotemporal random forests, the model should reduce SOC-change RMSE and calibration error at field scale, detect management-induced SOC trends earlier, and produce better uncertainty estimates in fields with changing residue, tillage, irrigation, or cover-crop regimes.", "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on harmonizing laboratory spectra, proximal spectra, sensor calibration, depth registration, and sparse destructive SOC measurements; performance may degrade where sensor networks are absent or where spectral libraries poorly represent local mineralogy and management histories.", "delta_genome": "Moves beyond static soil grids, EO-only SOC predictors, and spectroscopy-only soil property models by making subsurface observations active conditioning signals for a temporal geospatial foundation model and by updating a persistent latent SOC state rather than predicting independent map snapshots.", "claim_genome": "A soil-specific multimodal extension of geospatial foundation models can act as a unified soil digital twin: satellite time series provide spatial-temporal surface context, spectra provide chemically grounded subsurface priors, and sensor streams provide dynamic constraints, jointly enabling more reliable field-scale carbon accounting than any modality alone."}}
```

topic: [astro/Library] astro_GravitationalWaves
full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Astrophysics / Gravitational Wave Astronomy.
Below are 8 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Transformer-based parameter estimation can make gravitational-wave inference more flexible and faster, but real deployments still face non-Gaussian glitches, waveform-systematic error, detector drift, and low-latency follow-up pressure. What pipeline would jointly learn detection, denoising, simulation-based inference, and calibration so O4/O5 and next-generation detectors can trigger trustworthy multi-messenger decisions?

[Paper 1] Deep Neural Networks to Enable Real-time Multimessenger Astrophysics
Brief Abstract: This paper introduced Deep Filtering, using convolutional neural networks for direct gravitational-wave detection and coarse parameter estimation from time-series strain data. It was one of the earliest influential demonstrations that deep learning could approximate matched-filter-like sensitivity while operating much faster at inference time.

[Paper 2] Detection of gravitational-wave signals from binary neutron star mergers using machine learning
Brief Abstract: This paper developed a deep-learning architecture specifically targeting real-time detection of binary neutron star signals, which are long-duration and especially important for electromagnetic follow-up. The work emphasized low-latency inference relevant to multi-messenger astronomy in the Advanced LIGO/Virgo era.

[Paper 3] Neural Importance Sampling for Rapid and Reliable Gravitational-Wave Inference
Brief Abstract: This paper demonstrated simulation-based, likelihood-free parameter estimation for binary black holes using neural posterior estimation combined with importance sampling for verification. It showed that high-fidelity Bayesian inference can be accelerated to timescales compatible with rapid analysis of observing-run events while maintaining reliability guarantees.

[Paper 4] Flexible Gravitational-Wave Parameter Estimation with Transformers
Brief Abstract: This paper introduces a transformer-based gravitational-wave parameter estimation framework (Dingo-T1) that flexibly adapts to varying analysis settings while delivering fast, accurate inference across 48 events from the third observing run.

[Paper 5] GW170817: Observation of Gravitational Waves from a Binary Neutron Star Inspiral
Brief Abstract: This paper reported GW170817, the first binary neutron star detection, together with its broader multi-messenger context. It established the scientific premium on low-latency detection, sky localization, and rapid parameter estimation because electromagnetic counterparts could be observed within hours.

[Paper 6] Real-time gravitational-wave science with neural posterior estimation
Brief Abstract: DINGO introduced amortized Bayesian inference for gravitational-wave parameter estimation using deep generative modeling with normalizing flows, enabling orders-of-magnitude faster posterior estimation than standard stochastic samplers. It shifted ML for GW astronomy from detection alone to full rapid inference.

[Paper 7] Search for Gravitational Waves from Low Mass Compact Binary Coalescence in LIGO's Sixth Science Run and Virgo's Science Runs 2 and 3
Brief Abstract: This paper represents the mature pre-detection matched-filter search paradigm for compact binary coalescences in LIGO/Virgo data. It systematized template-bank searches, coincidence tests, data-quality vetoes, and background estimation in realistic interferometer noise.

[Paper 8] Observation of Gravitational Waves from a Binary Black Hole Merger
Brief Abstract: This paper reported GW150914, the first direct detection of gravitational waves and of a binary black hole merger. It validated the matched-filter and coherent analysis framework on real Advanced LIGO O1 data and launched gravitational-wave astronomy as an observational field.

RFdiffusion is a central parent because it introduced conditional diffusion over protein structures, including motif scaffolding and binder design. I’ll read it to understand what is optimized and where the in silico–in vivo gap remains.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4387309637"}
```
[result]: “De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion” describes a denoising diffusion model over protein backbones built on RoseTTAFold representations, enabling unconditional generation, motif scaffolding, symmetric assemblies, enzyme-like active-site scaffolds, and target-conditioned binder design. The paper reports experimentally validated designs, but its optimization is primarily structural/geometric, with downstream sequence design and filtering rather than a unified objective for affinity, selectivity, stability, expression, trafficking, and cell-context function. It establishes diffusion as a powerful generative prior but leaves biological efficacy mostly as an external experimental selection step.  

ProteinMPNN is another parent because many binder-design pipelines generate backbones first and then use ProteinMPNN-like inverse folding to assign sequences. I’ll read it to see how sequence stability and designability enter the pipeline.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4298101999"}
```
[result]: “Robust deep learning–based protein sequence design using ProteinMPNN” presents a message-passing neural network that designs amino-acid sequences for fixed protein backbones, achieving strong sequence recovery and high experimental success for stable monomers, oligomers, and nanomaterials. It is fast and broadly useful as an inverse-folding module, but it does not directly optimize target-specific binding efficacy or cell-state-dependent activity. Its strength is local structural compatibility and foldability, typically used after backbone generation rather than inside a closed-loop functional objective.  

To ground the proposal in a more complete lineage, I’ll extract a structured genome for RFdiffusion, since the new idea will likely mutate its generative mechanism by adding differentiable surrogate objectives and experimental feedback.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4387309637"}
```
[result]: RFdiffusion genome: mechanism = RoseTTAFold-conditioned denoising diffusion generates protein backbones under structural constraints; niche = de novo protein structure/function design, especially scaffolds and target-conditioned binders; observation = diverse experimentally plausible structures and some validated binders can be generated without explicit natural templates; limitation = affinity, selectivity, pharmacological stability, expression, and in vivo function are not jointly optimized in the generative loop; delta = replaces fragment/template assembly with learned structural diffusion; claim = generative structural priors can unlock programmable protein design.  

Before proposing, I’ll check whether a unified “end-to-end in vivo-aware binder design” mechanism already appears redundant with recent work. The novelty check focuses on multi-objective generative optimization coupling affinity and biological efficacy rather than only structure or sequence.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A closed-loop generative protein binder design model that jointly optimizes structure, sequence, affinity, selectivity, stability, expression, and in vivo efficacy using differentiable surrogate assays and active-learning feedback.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work includes RFdiffusion-style backbone generation, ProteinMPNN sequence design, AlphaFold/AlphaFold-Multimer filtering, and recent binder-design pipelines that combine deep learning with experimental screening, but most decompose the problem into sequential modules. The proposed mechanism is differentiated by treating cellular efficacy as a trainable latent objective coupled to binding, developability, and selectivity within the generator, rather than as a post hoc validation gate.  

The proposal should therefore inherit diffusion-based conditional generation and inverse-folding design, but mutate the objective: instead of optimizing only structural compatibility, use a multi-headed differentiable “activity world model” trained from paired in silico, biophysical, and pooled in vivo perturbation data. I’ll now terminate with a complete gene genome for the new research idea.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop ECLIPSE-Bind, an end-to-end conditional diffusion and inverse-folding framework in which protein backbones and sequences are co-generated under a differentiable multi-objective reward model. The reward model jointly predicts interface free energy, off-target cross-reactivity, conformational stability, expression/solubility, protease resistance, tissue or cell-type exposure, and pooled in vivo efficacy from paired structural simulations, deep mutational scans, yeast/display binding data, serum-stability assays, and barcoded animal or organoid perturbation readouts. Generation is optimized by Pareto-conditioned diffusion guidance and active-learning rounds that retrain the reward model on experimentally measured failures as well as successes.", "niche_genome": "Computational design of high-affinity therapeutic or perturbational protein binders where the key bottleneck is the low conversion of excellent in silico binders into selective, stable, functional molecules in living systems.", "observation_genome": "Compared with RFdiffusion plus ProteinMPNN plus AlphaFold-style filtering, the framework should produce fewer top-scoring designs but a higher fraction that bind selectively, remain folded and expressed in relevant biological matrices, and produce the intended cellular or organismal phenotype. The strongest empirical signal would be enrichment of in vivo-active binders among the top Pareto-front candidates across multiple target classes.", "limitation_genome": "The method depends on scarce, noisy, and context-specific in vivo efficacy data, so early versions may overfit to targets, delivery routes, or cell types represented in the training set. The latent efficacy model may also learn correlates of assay format rather than causal mechanisms of biological activity.", "delta_genome": "This changes the standard binder-design stack from sequential structural generation, sequence design, and post hoc experimental triage into a closed-loop generator whose search distribution is directly shaped by biological function, developability, and selectivity. It keeps RFdiffusion-like structural priors and ProteinMPNN-like sequence compatibility, but replaces single-score ranking with Pareto-guided optimization against experimentally updated surrogate phenotypes.", "claim_genome": "Generative protein design will achieve substantially higher experimental validation rates when in vivo efficacy is represented as a learned, uncertainty-aware objective inside the generative loop rather than as an external validation endpoint after affinity-optimized designs have already been selected."}}
```.TRAILING

full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computational Chemistry / Structure-Based Drug Design.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
DiffPharma generates pharmacophore-constrained molecules but optimizes only for binding geometry without explicit ADMET property optimization. What mechanism would enable joint multi-objective drug design — simultaneously satisfying pharmacophore constraints, binding affinity, ADMET properties, and synthetic accessibility within a single diffusion framework?

[Paper 1] DiffDock: Diffusion Steps, Twists, and Turns for Molecular Docking
Brief Abstract: DiffDock: diffusion over SE(3) ligand pose (translation, rotation, torsion angles); generates diverse binding pose hypotheses; surpasses all prior blind docking methods.

[Paper 2] Atomic Convolutional Networks for Predicting Protein-Ligand Binding Affinity
Brief Abstract: AtomConv: applies MPNN-style graph convolutions to 3D protein-ligand complexes; predicts binding affinity from co-crystal structures.

[Paper 3] EquiBind: Geometric Deep Learning for Drug Binding Structure Prediction
Brief Abstract: EquiBind: predicts protein-ligand binding pose directly without exhaustive docking search; SE(3)-equivariant GNN for both protein and ligand.

[Paper 4] Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3
Brief Abstract: AlphaFold3: unified diffusion-based model for structure prediction of proteins, DNA, RNA, small molecules, and their complexes; covers all biomolecular interaction types.

[Paper 5] Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry
Brief Abstract: MPNN: unified message passing framework for molecular property prediction; state-of-the-art on QM9 quantum chemical properties.

[Paper 6] Interaction-constrained 3D Molecular Generation Using a Diffusion Model Enables Structure-Based Pharmacophore Modeling
Brief Abstract: DiffPharma: conditional diffusion model with interaction-specific neural networks generates drug candidates satisfying pharmacophore constraints derived from protein-ligand structures; moves beyond AlphaFold3 structure prediction to structure-conditioned molecular generation.

Guidance weights are adapted online by a constrained Pareto-Lagrangian controller that preserves hard pharmacophore geometry while steering samples toward the non-dominated region of affinity, ADMET, and synthesis objectives.","niche_genome":"Structure-based drug design for targets with known or predicted protein pockets and pharmacophore hypotheses, especially hit-to-lead campaigns where geometric binding constraints alone produce candidates with poor permeability, toxicity risk, metabolic instability, or synthetic infeasibility.","observation_genome":"On benchmarks built from PDBBind/CrossDocked complexes annotated with QED, SA score, toxicity/CYP/hERG proxies, and docking or learned affinity labels, the method should generate a higher fraction of molecules that simultaneously satisfy pharmacophore constraints, retain plausible pocket poses, improve predicted affinity, pass ADMET filters, and remain synthetically accessible compared with DiffPharma plus post hoc filtering or single-objective guided diffusion.","limitation_genome":"The approach depends on imperfect surrogate predictors for affinity, ADMET, and synthesis, so it may exploit model artifacts or over-constrain sampling; experimental validation and uncertainty penalties will be needed to prevent reward hacking and to handle targets outside the training distribution.","delta_genome":"Compared with DiffPharma, the central change is moving multi-parameter optimization from an external filtering stage into the diffusion dynamics themselves. Compared with DiffDock/EquiBind/AtomConv/MPNN lineages, their pose, affinity, and property-prediction machinery becomes differentiable guidance inside a molecule generator rather than a separate evaluator.","claim_genome":"A pharmacophore-constrained diffusion model with uncertainty-calibrated Pareto guidance can perform joint multi-objective drug design in a single sampling framework, yielding candidates that are geometrically compatible with the binding site and simultaneously more drug-like, safer, higher-affinity, and more synthetically feasible than geometry-only constrained generation."}}
```

The paper reports faster 3D generation with competitive validity and geometry metrics, but does not solve real-time, target-specific conditioning over unseen protein pockets with additional ADMET or synthesis constraints.

The method demonstrates stronger pocket-aware molecule generation than non-target-conditioned baselines, but sampling remains iterative and does not natively enforce simultaneous ADMET and retrosynthetic constraints in real time.

full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Climate Modeling / Climate Emulators.
Below are 7 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Hybrid physics-ML climate parameterizations and learned emulators can improve speed and online skill, but they remain brittle under long rollouts and out-of-distribution forcing scenarios. What mechanism would make ML climate emulators scenario-aware and physically constrained enough to produce uncertainty-calibrated multi-decadal responses to novel greenhouse-gas, aerosol, land-use, and coupled Earth-system feedback changes for assessment-scale use?

[Paper 1] Deep learning to represent subgrid processes in climate models
Brief Abstract: This paper showed that neural networks can emulate unresolved subgrid physical processes, especially moist convection, inside climate models. It was an early demonstration that machine learning could replace expensive parameterizations while retaining useful climate statistics.

[Paper 2] ClimaX: A foundation model for weather and climate
Brief Abstract: ClimaX introduced a transformer-based foundation model pretrained across heterogeneous weather and climate datasets, tasks, and variables. It reframed climate and weather modeling as transferable representation learning rather than single-task supervised prediction.

[Paper 3] ACE: A fast, skillful learned global atmospheric model for climate prediction
Brief Abstract: ACE presented a learned global atmospheric model aimed at climate prediction with emphasis on speed, forecast skill, and stable rollout. It moved beyond generic foundation modeling toward a simulator-oriented learned atmospheric model optimized for fast and skillful climate-timescale emulation.

[Paper 4] WeatherBench: A Benchmark Data Set for Data-Driven Weather Forecasting
Brief Abstract: This paper introduced a standardized benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for data-driven weather forecasting, enabling systematic comparison of deep learning and physics-based methods. It accelerated the community's transition from component-level ML toward end-to-end atmospheric prediction.

[Paper 5] Crowdsourcing the Frontier: Advancing Hybrid Physics-ML Climate Simulation via a $50,000 Kaggle Competition
Brief Abstract: This paper advances hybrid physics-ML climate simulation by using a large-scale $50,000 Kaggle competition to crowdsource and benchmark subgrid ML parameterizations designed for stable and skillful online climate modeling.

[Paper 6] The Community Earth System Model: A Framework for Collaborative Research
Brief Abstract: This paper introduced CESM1, a major coupled general circulation / Earth system model used for climate simulation across components including atmosphere, ocean, land, and sea ice. It established the high-fidelity but computationally expensive simulation paradigm that later emulator work seeks to approximate or accelerate.

[Paper 7] Neural general circulation models for weather and climate
Brief Abstract: NeuralGCM combined neural components with differentiable dynamical cores to create a learned general circulation model capable of weather and climate simulation. It directly targeted the central frontier issues of long-term stability, physical structure, and climate-statistical fidelity.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

You are a senior researcher in Atmospheric Science / Emission Inversion.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Recent ML systems can map CO2 and CH4 from satellite observations at high spatial resolution, but they still do not cleanly separate retrieval gaps, atmospheric transport error, prior-inventory bias, and source-attribution uncertainty. What inversion architecture would couple OCO/TROPOMI-style satellite retrievals, differentiable transport and chemistry, ground sensors, and calibrated uncertainty to deliver MRV-grade city-to-facility CO2/CH4 emissions in near real time?

[Paper 1] OCO-3 early mission operations and initial (vEarly) XCO2 and SIF retrievals
Brief Abstract: This paper described OCO-3's early on-orbit performance and highlighted its snapshot area map (SAM) mode, which is especially relevant for urban and point-source emission studies. It expanded the observational regime from global track-based monitoring toward targeted mapping needed for emission inversion and carbon-neutrality MRV.

[Paper 2] The Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 early science investigations of regional carbon dioxide fluxes
Brief Abstract: This early OCO-2 science paper demonstrated that satellite XCO2 observations can constrain regional-scale carbon fluxes beyond what was possible with the sparse in situ network alone. It marked a transition from instrument validation to practical atmospheric inversion for carbon-cycle inference.

[Paper 3] Physics-informed neural networks: A deep learning framework for solving forward and inverse problems involving nonlinear partial differential equations
Brief Abstract: This foundational PINN paper introduced a framework for embedding PDE residuals into neural-network training for both forward and inverse problems. For atmospheric transport and chemistry, it provided a principled route to combine sparse observations with governing equations rather than relying on purely black-box inversion.

[Paper 4] Atmospheric Transport Modeling of CO2 with Neural Networks
Brief Abstract: This paper demonstrated that neural networks can emulate atmospheric CO2 transport, replacing expensive numerical transport models with learned surrogates. It provides a key building block toward fast, differentiable inversion systems for estimating surface carbon fluxes from satellite observations.

[Paper 5] The on-orbit performance of the Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 (OCO-2) instrument and its radiometrically calibrated products
Brief Abstract: This paper established the radiometric and spectroscopic performance basis for OCO-2 using its first 18 months of on-orbit data. It underpinned confidence in the mission's ability to retrieve precise column-averaged dry-air mole fractions of CO2 (XCO2), which are the observational foundation for later atmospheric inversions.

[Paper 6] Improved estimation of carbon dioxide and methane using machine learning with satellite observations over the Arabian Peninsula
Brief Abstract: This paper develops a machine-learning-based framework (XGBoost) to generate high-fidelity 1 km spatiotemporal maps of CO2 and CH4 over the Arabian Peninsula from satellite observations (OCO-2 and Sentinel-5P) despite data gaps and coarse model limitations.

Its key relevance is that SAM observations move beyond narrow nadir/glint tracks toward repeated spatial mapping over cities, power plants, and other localized emission features. The paper does not solve inversion itself; it mainly establishes that targeted satellite column observations can supply spatially coherent constraints for urban and point-source carbon studies, while still facing cloud gaps, retrieval biases, and variable viewing geometries.  

Next I’ll read the foundational PINN paper to anchor the differentiable inverse-problem component. The goal is to distinguish a true physics-coupled architecture from a black-box mapper from retrievals to emissions.

The framework is relevant because atmospheric transport and chemistry can be encoded as differentiable constraints rather than learned only from labeled flux fields. However, the original paper is generic and does not address satellite retrieval missingness, emission inventories, source attribution, or operational uncertainty calibration.  

I’ll now inspect the genome of the neural CO2 transport paper, since a fast differentiable transport surrogate is a central building block for near-real-time inversion. Extracting its structured limitations should clarify where a follow-up architecture can add novelty.

Its niche is speeding carbon-cycle forward modeling and enabling faster flux sensitivity experiments. Its main limitations are dependence on the training model’s meteorological/transport regime, weak treatment of observation operators and retrieval gaps, and no full Bayesian decomposition of transport error versus prior flux bias. The paper’s claim is that neural transport can materially reduce computational cost while preserving useful spatiotemporal CO2 structure.

Similar work exists on satellite CO2 inversions, TROPOMI methane plume quantification, neural transport emulation, and physics-informed inverse modeling, but the closest papers typically address only one or two uncertainty channels at a time. The novelty gap is strongest around a unified operational architecture that treats missing retrievals as informative censoring, uses differentiable transport/chemistry with calibrated error states, and reports attribution-ready posterior emissions at nested city-to-facility scales.  

The proposal should therefore hybridize OCO-3 targeted mapping, PINN/differentiable transport ideas, and neural transport emulation, but mutate them into a full MRV inversion stack. It introduces four explicit latent error channels: retrieval missingness/censoring and bias, meteorology-dependent transport discrepancy, prior-inventory bias, and source-attribution mixing; posterior inference is performed with amortized variational inference plus ensemble calibration against held-out ground and aircraft data.","niche_genome":"Operational MRV for urban airsheds, industrial clusters, landfills, oil/gas basins, and large point sources where satellite observations are high resolution but intermittent, inventories are biased or stale, and stakeholders need city-to-facility CO2/CH4 emission estimates within hours to days rather than months.","observation_genome":"Expected empirical result: compared with a conventional transport-inversion baseline and a black-box satellite-to-emission mapper, the architecture should reduce facility-scale posterior bias and improve calibrated 90% interval coverage, especially under cloud-driven retrieval gaps and wind-error episodes. It should also decompose an emission anomaly into probabilities of true source change, retrieval artifact, transport mismatch, or prior-inventory error, enabling more defensible MRV flags.","limitation_genome":"The method will still inherit errors from the meteorological analyses and satellite retrieval physics used for training/calibration, and facility-level attribution may remain weak when plumes overlap or when wind fields are poorly constrained. It also requires curated multi-sensor training windows, periodic aircraft or dense ground validation, and careful governance around reporting uncertain facility-specific estimates.","delta_genome":"Relative to OCO-2/OCO-3 observational papers, this moves from demonstrating precise column retrievals and targeted mapping to a full probabilistic emission inversion and reporting system. Relative to PINNs and neural CO2 transport emulators, it adds satellite observation operators, retrieval-gap censoring, hierarchical inventory correction, methane chemistry, multi-sensor assimilation, and calibrated attribution-aware uncertainty rather than only fast forward modeling or generic PDE-constrained inversion.","claim_genome":"A differentiable, gap-aware, multi-sensor inversion that explicitly models the four dominant uncertainty sources—retrieval gaps/bias, transport error, prior-inventory bias, and source-attribution ambiguity—will produce more reliable near-real-time MRV-grade CO2/CH4 emissions from city to facility scale than either conventional offline inversions or purely data-driven satellite emission mappers."}}

You are a senior researcher in Climate Modeling / AI Weather Prediction.
Below are 7 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Aurora-style Earth-system foundation models show broad forecasting skill, but operational centers still need calibrated ensembles, physically consistent rollouts, and dependable rare-extreme behavior under distribution shift. What architecture would combine learned forecasting, online data assimilation, physics constraints, and uncertainty calibration to produce trusted multi-hazard ensemble forecasts for atmosphere-ocean-land extremes?

[Paper 1] Skillful nowcasting of precipitation with deep generative models of radar
Brief Abstract: DeepMind introduced a deep generative radar nowcasting system that produced probabilistic precipitation forecasts with strong skill at short lead times. The paper was influential in showing that learned spatiotemporal generative models could outperform strong operational baselines in a weather forecasting setting.

[Paper 2] Pangu-Weather: A 3D High-Resolution Model for Fast and Accurate Global Weather Forecast
Brief Abstract: Pangu-Weather introduced a 3D Earth-specific transformer for global weather forecasting across multiple lead times, delivering strong skill and fast inference at operationally relevant scales. It demonstrated that transformer architectures with pressure-level-aware structure could surpass traditional NWP on several benchmarks.

[Paper 3] GraphCast: Learning skillful medium-range global weather forecasting
Brief Abstract: GraphCast recast global weather prediction as message passing on a multiscale mesh graph, achieving very strong medium-range deterministic forecast skill and outperforming a leading operational baseline on many targets. It became a landmark result for AI weather forecasting because it combined global coverage, medium-range lead times, and strong benchmark performance.

[Paper 4] FourCastNet: A Global Data-driven High-resolution Weather Model using Adaptive Fourier Neural Operators
Brief Abstract: FourCastNet was one of the first prominent global AI weather models to perform fast, high-resolution medium-range forecasting directly from reanalysis data. It used adaptive Fourier neural operators to model global atmospheric evolution and highlighted the possibility of replacing expensive numerical integration with learned dynamics.

[Paper 5] A foundation model for the Earth system
Brief Abstract: Paper B introduces Aurora, a large-scale Earth system foundation model that unifies weather and broader environmental forecasting across multiple domains and scales with strong accuracy and efficiency.

[Paper 6] Deep learning for post-processing ensemble weather forecasts
Brief Abstract: This paper showed that neural networks can improve ensemble weather forecast post-processing, learning calibrated predictive distributions from raw ensemble output. It helped establish that data-driven methods could add value to operational weather prediction workflows before full AI forecast models emerged.

[Paper 7] GenCast: Diffusion-based ensemble forecasting for medium-range weather
Brief Abstract: GenCast extended AI weather prediction from deterministic trajectories to diffusion-based ensemble generation, targeting calibrated probabilistic medium-range forecasting. It showed that generative modeling can produce skillful ensembles and improve the representation of forecast uncertainty and extremes.

You are a senior researcher in LLM Agents / Agentic AI.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
SWE-World achieves 68.2% on SWE-bench via learned environment simulation, but the simulated environment diverges from real execution for complex multi-file refactors. What mechanism would enable a continuously self-improving agent-environment co-evolution — where the agent's failures update the environment model and the improved environment produces better agent training signal?

[Paper 1] AutoGen: Enabling Next-Generation LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
Brief Abstract: AutoGen: multi-agent conversation framework where specialized agents (assistant, code executor, critic) communicate via structured conversation to solve complex tasks.

[Paper 2] Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
Brief Abstract: Reflexion: agent verbally reflects on task failures and stores reflections in episodic memory; enables improvement over multiple trials without gradient updates.

[Paper 3] SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
Brief Abstract: SWE-agent: designs Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) with specialized file editing, bash commands, and context management; resolves 12.5% of SWE-bench issues.

[Paper 4] OpenDevin: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents
Brief Abstract: OpenDevin (now OpenHands): open-source platform unifying SWE-agent's ACI with AutoGen's multi-agent framework; sandbox environment with browser + bash + file system.

[Paper 5] ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
Brief Abstract: ReAct: interleaves reasoning traces and tool-use actions in the LLM context; Thought→Action→Observation loop enables complex multi-step task completion.

[Paper 6] SWE-World: Building Software Engineering Agents in Docker-Free Environments
Brief Abstract: Replaces physical Docker execution sandbox with LLM surrogate that simulates test feedback; enables Docker-free SFT and RL training for SE agents; raises Qwen2.5-Coder-32B on SWE-bench Verified from 6.2% to 52.0% via SFT, 55.0% with RL, and 68.2% with test-time scaling.

[result]: SWE-World proposes replacing Docker-based software execution environments with an LLM-based surrogate that predicts test feedback, enabling scalable SFT and RL for software engineering agents without heavy sandbox infrastructure. {"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4398765432"}
```
[result]: SWE-agent introduces an Agent-Computer Interface with structured commands for file navigation, editing, search, bash execution, and context management. It shows that interface design strongly affects software engineering performance, resolving 12.5% of SWE-bench issues by making repository interaction more tractable for LLM agents. Unlike SWE-World, SWE-agent relies on concrete execution feedback from a real environment, which makes it accurate but expensive and less scalable. The paper’s implicit contribution is that agent learning depends not only on the model but also on the shape and fidelity of the environment interface.

I’ll extract a structured genome for SWE-World to make the inherited mechanism, niche, and limitation explicit. This will help formulate a proposal that mutates the simulator from a static surrogate into a self-correcting co-evolutionary system.

{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W4408123456"}

[result]: SWE-World genome: mechanism_genome = “train an LLM surrogate environment to simulate software execution/test feedback and use it for SFT, RL, and test-time scaling”; niche_genome = “software engineering agents on SWE-bench-like repository repair tasks where Docker execution is costly”; observation_genome = “surrogate feedback enables large-scale training and substantially improves SWE-bench Verified performance”; limitation_genome = “simulator can hallucinate or miss real execution failures, especially for complex multi-file refactors and hidden environment dependencies”; delta_genome = “replaces expensive physical sandbox feedback with learned feedback”; claim_genome = “high-fidelity learned environments can provide enough training signal to improve software agents at scale.”

Before proposing, I’ll run a novelty check on the key mechanism: closing the loop between real execution failures, simulator correction, and subsequent agent training. This checks whether the idea is simply SWE-World restated or whether the co-evolutionary update loop is a healthy extension.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"A software engineering agent and learned execution simulator co-evolve by using real-execution discrepancies from failed patches to update the simulator, then using the corrected simulator to generate harder training tasks and better RL feedback for the agent.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Closest related work includes SWE-World, which trains and uses a learned execution surrogate, Reflexion, which stores verbal failure memories for agent improvement, and AutoGen/OpenDevin, which provide multi-agent orchestration and software-development environments. However, the specific closed-loop mechanism in which real/simulated execution discrepancies become supervised counterexamples for the environment model, and the improved environment actively curates future agent training signal, is not directly covered by the nearest papers. The proposal appears connected to the lineage but not redundant.

I’ll now propose a concrete follow-up: a discrepancy-driven agent-environment co-training loop. It inherits SWE-World’s scalable learned simulation, SWE-agent’s execution-grounded interface, and Reflexion’s failure memory, but turns failures into structured environment-model updates rather than only agent-side reflections.
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Discrepancy-Driven Co-Evolution for SWE Agents: maintain a learned execution simulator paired with a real-execution audit queue. During training, the agent proposes patches and receives simulated test feedback; high-uncertainty, high-impact, or novel multi-file trajectories are periodically replayed in a real sandbox. Differences between simulated and real outcomes are converted into structured counterexamples containing patch diff, touched dependency graph, command trace, expected/actual tests, stack traces, and causal failure summaries. These counterexamples update the environment model through supervised correction and uncertainty calibration, while the corrected simulator generates adversarial follow-up tasks and denser RL rewards for the agent.","niche_genome":"SWE-bench Verified and related repository repair benchmarks where full Docker execution is expensive but purely learned feedback diverges on multi-file refactors, dependency-sensitive changes, build-system behavior, and hidden test interactions.","observation_genome":"Compared with static SWE-World-style simulation, the co-evolving simulator should reduce real-vs-simulated feedback disagreement over successive training rounds, especially on multi-file patches, and produce agents that transfer better to real execution. Expected empirical signatures include lower simulator calibration error, higher pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified under the same real-execution budget, and fewer patches that pass simulated tests but fail real tests.","limitation_genome":"The method still requires some real sandbox execution as an auditing signal, and its benefit depends on selecting the right trajectories to audit. If the real-execution budget is too small or the discrepancy classifier misses rare dependency failures, the simulator may remain overconfident in exactly the regimes where correctness matters most.","delta_genome":"SWE-World treats the learned environment mainly as a scalable replacement for Docker feedback; this proposal makes the environment model continuously falsifiable and self-improving. Reflexion-style failure information is moved from only the agent memory into the environment-training substrate, while SWE-agent/OpenDevin-style real execution becomes a sparse calibration oracle rather than the default runtime.","claim_genome":"A software engineering agent trained against a simulator that is continuously corrected by its own real-world failure discrepancies will outperform agents trained against either static learned simulators or purely real-execution-limited environments, because the environment model and the agent form a positive feedback loop: agent failures expose simulator blind spots, corrected simulation yields better training signal, and better agents discover harder environment-model errors."}}
```

Its main contribution is the issue-to-patch benchmark and execution-based scoring, which makes it a natural source of counterexamples for any learned simulator.

Its limitation genome highlights simulator drift on long-horizon multi-file edits, hidden build-system effects, and distribution shift induced by improved agents. Its claim genome is that a sufficiently accurate learned environment can produce higher-quality training signal than relying only on sparse real test execution.

topic: [cs/Library] cs_AttentionEfficiency
full_prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Systems / Efficient LLM.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
AsyncTLS achieves inference speedup via sparse attention on MLA but the sparsity pattern is fixed at inference time. What mechanism would enable dynamic, content-adaptive sparsity that selects different attention patterns per token and layer based on query semantics while preserving answer fidelity, long-context retrieval reliability, and hardware-efficient scheduling under distribution shift?

[Paper 1] GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints
Brief Abstract: GQA: group query attention — share one K/V head across G query heads; reduces KV-cache memory by G× while maintaining quality near MHA.

[Paper 2] FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
Brief Abstract: FlashAttention: IO-aware tiled kernel computation avoids materializing the N×N attention matrix; 2-4x speedup, sublinear memory in sequence length.

[Paper 3] FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning
Brief Abstract: FlashAttention-2: better parallelism over sequence dimension and improved work partitioning across thread blocks; achieves ~70% A100 MFU.

[Paper 4] DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model
Brief Abstract: MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention): compress KV cache via low-rank joint projection, 93.3% KV-cache reduction while outperforming MHA quality.

[Paper 5] Attention Is All You Need
Brief Abstract: Transformer: multi-head self-attention replacing RNNs; enables parallelizable training; defines the O(n²) KV-cache bottleneck that all subsequent work addresses.

[Paper 6] AsyncTLS: Efficient Generative LLM Inference with Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention
Brief Abstract: Asynchronous block+token sparse attention specifically optimized for MLA architectures; achieves 1.2×-10.0× operator speedup and 1.3×-4.7× end-to-end throughput improvement on MLA-based models by decoupling block-level and token-level sparsity computation with asynchronous memory transfer.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

```action
