topic: [biology] Study how circadian rhythm disruption affects pancreatic beta-cell metabolism and insulin secretion in models of type 2 diabetes.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Study how circadian rhythm disruption affects pancreatic beta-cell metabolism and insulin secretion in models of type 2 diabetes.

Discipline: biology

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first search broadly within the year window for metabolic/circadian-adjacent work relevant to diabetes, beta-cell energetics, and insulin secretion. The supplied candidate set appears to emphasize NAD+ biology, insulin resistance, metabolic flexibility, and gut-derived metabolites, so I’ll identify the most useful lineage anchors from those.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "circadian disruption beta cell metabolism insulin secretion type 2 diabetes NAD metabolic flexibility", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several adjacent but relevant reviews and mechanistic syntheses. Strong candidates include oa:W3092621787, “NAD+ metabolism: pathophysiologic mechanisms and therapeutic potential” (2020), which links NAD+ availability to metabolic adaptation; oa:W2800658350, “Metabolic Flexibility as an Adaptation to Energy Resources and Requirements in Health and Disease” (2018), which frames substrate switching failures in metabolic disease; and oa:W4284890877, “Trends in insulin resistance: insights into mechanisms and therapeutic strategy” (2022). More peripheral candidates include gut microbiota short-chain fatty acids and leptin/obesity papers.

The NAD+ review is a promising mechanistic parent because circadian clocks, mitochondrial redox state, SIRT activity, and beta-cell insulin secretion all intersect through NAD+/NADH cycling. I’ll read it to ground the proposal in established metabolic regulatory mechanisms.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3092621787"}
```
[result]: oa:W3092621787 is a 2020 review titled “NAD+ metabolism: pathophysiologic mechanisms and therapeutic potential.” It summarizes NAD+ as a central cofactor and signaling metabolite regulating mitochondrial metabolism, DNA repair, sirtuins, PARPs, inflammation, and cellular stress adaptation. The paper emphasizes that NAD+ decline or compartmental imbalance can impair metabolic flexibility and that boosting NAD+ through precursors or salvage-pathway modulation has therapeutic potential in metabolic and age-related disease. Although not beta-cell-specific, it provides a strong mechanistic link between redox metabolism, circadian-regulated enzymes, and disease-associated metabolic dysfunction.

To connect the NAD+ lineage to beta-cell substrate handling, I’ll read the metabolic flexibility paper next. Beta-cells require rapid switching between nutrient sensing, mitochondrial ATP production, and insulin granule exocytosis, so impaired flexibility is likely central to circadian disruption effects.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2800658350"}
```
[result]: oa:W2800658350 is a 2018 review titled “Metabolic Flexibility as an Adaptation to Energy Resources and Requirements in Health and Disease.” It defines metabolic flexibility as the capacity to adapt substrate sensing, trafficking, storage, and utilization according to nutrient availability and energetic demand. The paper discusses how obesity, insulin resistance, and metabolic disease are associated with reduced ability to switch between fuel states. For pancreatic beta-cells, this framework suggests that disrupted timing of nutrient exposure could impair glucose-stimulated insulin secretion by desynchronizing fuel oxidation, mitochondrial coupling, and secretory readiness.

I’ll extract the genome of the NAD+ paper because it is the strongest molecular parent for a circadian beta-cell metabolism proposal. This should clarify which elements to inherit and where the new beta-cell/circadian contribution can be inserted.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3092621787"}
```
[result]: Extracted genome for oa:W3092621787: mechanism_genome: NAD+ availability and compartmental NAD+/NADH balance regulate mitochondrial function, sirtuin/PARP signaling, stress responses, and metabolic adaptation. niche_genome: broad metabolic, inflammatory, neurodegenerative, and aging-related disorders with therapeutic interest in NAD+ restoration. observation_genome: disease and aging states often show altered NAD+ metabolism, and interventions that replenish NAD+ can improve cellular resilience in preclinical models. limitation_genome: much evidence is tissue-general, with uncertainty about cell-type-specific NAD+ pools, timing, dosing, and causal hierarchy. delta_genome: reframes NAD+ from a passive redox cofactor to an actionable signaling hub. claim_genome: manipulating NAD+ metabolism can restore adaptive cellular physiology in disease contexts.

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the specific idea—time-targeted NAD+ salvage modulation in circadian-disrupted diabetic beta-cells—is likely novel rather than redundant. The goal is to ensure the proposal is not merely “give NAD+ precursors for diabetes,” but tests phase-specific beta-cell metabolic rescue.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Phase-targeted modulation of beta-cell NAD+ salvage and mitochondrial redox rhythms to rescue glucose-stimulated insulin secretion after circadian disruption in type 2 diabetes models.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar literature exists on NAD+ metabolism in metabolic disease, circadian regulation of metabolism, and beta-cell dysfunction in diabetes, but few studies directly test whether the timing of NAD+ salvage restoration can re-entrain beta-cell mitochondrial redox oscillations after circadian disruption. The closest overlap is broad NAD+ therapeutic work such as oa:W3092621787 and metabolic flexibility framing from oa:W2800658350, but the proposed beta-cell-specific chronotherapeutic mechanism appears distinct.

Building on the NAD+ and metabolic flexibility lineage, I’ll propose a targeted follow-up study focused on pancreatic beta-cells under circadian disruption. The key novelty is to test whether restoring the *phase* of NAD+ salvage and mitochondrial redox cycling, rather than simply increasing NAD+ globally, rescues insulin secretion in diabetic models.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Use synchronized mouse and human islet models plus beta-cell-specific diabetic mouse models to test whether timed activation of the NAD+ salvage pathway, for example by phase-restricted NMN or nicotinamide riboside dosing and beta-cell Nampt modulation, restores circadian oscillations in NAD+/NADH ratio, mitochondrial respiration, ATP/ADP coupling, and glucose-stimulated insulin secretion after chronic light-cycle disruption or mistimed feeding.", "niche_genome": "Pancreatic beta-cell metabolic failure in type 2 diabetes under circadian rhythm disruption, including shift-work-like light exposure, mistimed nutrient availability, and obesity/insulin-resistance models.", "observation_genome": "Circadian disruption is expected to flatten beta-cell NAD+ and mitochondrial redox rhythms, reduce metabolic flexibility between fasting-like and glucose-stimulated states, and blunt first-phase insulin secretion; restoring NAD+ salvage at the appropriate circadian phase should improve mitochondrial coupling and insulin release more effectively than arrhythmic or continuous NAD+ precursor delivery.", "limitation_genome": "The approach may be sensitive to species differences between mouse and human islets, systemic effects of NAD+ precursors, and difficulty separating beta-cell-intrinsic clock effects from altered feeding behavior, autonomic input, or insulin resistance in peripheral tissues.", "delta_genome": "Prior work frames NAD+ as a broad therapeutic hub and metabolic flexibility as a disease-relevant phenotype; this study adds a beta-cell-specific chronometabolic layer by asking whether the timing and oscillatory structure of NAD+ salvage, not just total NAD+ abundance, determines insulin secretory competence during circadian stress.", "claim_genome": "Type 2 diabetes-associated beta-cell dysfunction caused or worsened by circadian disruption can be partially reversed by re-phasing NAD+ salvage and mitochondrial redox metabolism, establishing beta-cell NAD+ rhythmicity as a chronotherapeutic target for preserving insulin secretion."}}
```

topic: [biology] Explore synthetic biology approaches for engineering cyanobacteria to increase carbon fixation efficiency and produce biodegradable bioplastics.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Explore synthetic biology approaches for engineering cyanobacteria to increase carbon fixation efficiency and produce biodegradable bioplastics.

Discipline: biology

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.

I will first map recent review-level work at the intersection of algal biotechnology, carbon capture, and bioplastic production, then read the most relevant papers to identify a concrete gap for a synthetic biology follow-up.

```action

```

[result]: The search returns several broad but relevant recent papers: oa:W3012466329 on emerging algal biotechnology for sustainable bioeconomies; oa:W2912731004 on microalgal carbon capture and biorefinery concepts; oa:W4319598975 on bacterial polyhydroxyalkanoate diversity for sustainable plastics; oa:W4311314067 on engineered living materials for sustainability; and oa:W3158581661 on plastics and microbiome impacts/solutions. The most promising lineage for this topic appears to combine algal/cyanobacterial carbon fixation engineering with PHA biopolymer production.

I will read the algal biotechnology review first because it likely summarizes current synthetic biology tools, scale-up bottlenecks, and carbon-fixation strategies in photosynthetic microbes.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3012466329"}
```

[result]: oa:W3012466329, “Emerging Technologies in Algal Biotechnology: Toward the Establishment of a Sustainable, Algae-Based Bioeconomy” (2020), reviews how algae and cyanobacteria can be engineered for food, fuels, specialty chemicals, wastewater treatment, and CO₂ utilization. The paper emphasizes advances in genome editing, synthetic promoters, metabolic engineering, photobioreactor design, and omics-guided strain improvement. It also highlights persistent bottlenecks: low areal productivity, inefficient carbon partitioning into target products, light/dark cycle stress, contamination, and difficulty translating lab strains to outdoor cultivation. For this topic, the key gap is integrating enhanced carbon capture with robust product formation rather than optimizing each separately.

Next I will read the PHA review to understand the known biological diversity of bioplastic production pathways and where cyanobacteria-specific engineering might add value.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4319598975"}
```

[result]: oa:W4319598975, “The Role of Bacterial Polyhydroalkanoate (PHA) in a Sustainable Future: A Review on the Biological Diversity” (2023), surveys microbial PHA production across diverse bacteria, including pathway variants, monomer diversity, granule-associated proteins, and stress-responsive accumulation. It frames PHA as a biodegradable alternative to petrochemical plastics but notes high production costs, feedstock demands, and process limitations. The paper suggests that expanding host range, improving carbon flux into PHA precursors, and engineering polymer composition are major opportunities. It also implies that photosynthetic PHA production could reduce feedstock costs if productivity and storage-polymer control can be improved.

I will extract a structured genome from the algal biotechnology paper to formalize what mechanisms and limitations the follow-up should inherit or mutate.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3012466329"}
```

[result]: The extracted genome identifies the mechanism as “use emerging algal synthetic biology, omics, and cultivation technologies to convert light and CO₂ into fuels, chemicals, foods, and biomaterials.” The niche is sustainable algae-based bioeconomy applications, especially CO₂ utilization and bioproduct generation. The observation is that algae/cyanobacteria have strong theoretical sustainability advantages but often underperform in real-world cultivation. The limitation is fragmented optimization: carbon capture, growth robustness, product flux, and cultivation conditions are often engineered separately rather than as a coordinated system.

Before proposing, I will check whether the central mechanism—coupling enhanced cyanobacterial carbon concentrating machinery to dynamic PHA flux control—looks redundant or still open in recent literature.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Engineer cyanobacteria with a CO2-responsive synthetic circuit that coordinates enhanced bicarbonate uptake/carboxysome carbon fixation with dynamic rerouting of excess fixed carbon into tunable PHA bioplastic granules during light-dark cultivation.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on algal biotechnology, cyanobacterial carbon concentrating mechanisms, microbial PHA biosynthesis, and photosynthetic bioproduction, but the closest papers generally treat carbon fixation enhancement and PHA accumulation as separate engineering objectives. The proposed coupling of CO₂-responsive CCM tuning, diel-aware carbon partitioning, and PHA granule/polymer control appears plausibly novel as an integrated design. The main risk is biological burden: simultaneously increasing carbon uptake and storage-polymer synthesis may reduce growth unless dynamically gated.

I will now propose a follow-up study that mutates the parent lineage from broad algal biotechnology and bacterial PHA reviews into a concrete cyanobacterial synthetic-biology platform.

```action

PCC 6803 or Synechococcus elongatus, with a modular CO2/diel-responsive synthetic circuit that couples three layers: enhanced bicarbonate uptake and carboxysome-associated Rubisco/CA expression under high inorganic carbon demand; CRISPRi-based repression of glycogen and competing overflow sinks when photosynthate is abundant; and inducible expression of optimized phaA/phaB/phaC plus granule-associated phasin variants to convert excess acetyl-CoA and NADPH into tunable PHA bioplastic granules without permanently suppressing growth.", "niche_genome": "Photosynthetic carbon capture and biodegradable bioplastic production in engineered cyanobacteria, aimed at low-input photobioreactor or semi-continuous cultivation systems using CO2-enriched flue gas or concentrated bicarbonate streams.", "observation_genome": "Compared with strains engineered only for PHA synthesis or only for carbon concentrating mechanisms, the dynamically coupled strain should show higher carbon fixation per biomass during illuminated phases, reduced carbon loss to glycogen or secreted byproducts, and increased PHA content and productivity across light-dark cycles while maintaining acceptable growth and culture stability.", "limitation_genome": "The design may impose metabolic burden, redox imbalance, or photoinhibition under fluctuating outdoor light; PHA accumulation could also reduce cell fitness or complicate harvesting. The first validation should therefore use staged laboratory photobioreactors, isotopic 13CO2 flux tracing, and circuit variants with tunable thresholds before outdoor deployment.", "delta_genome": "Prior algal biotechnology work emphasizes broad opportunities in CO2 utilization and bioproduct synthesis, while PHA literature emphasizes bacterial polymer diversity and pathway optimization. This proposal changes the unit of engineering from a single pathway to a coordinated carbon-allocation controller that links cyanobacterial carbon concentrating machinery, diel metabolism, and PHA granule biogenesis in one feedback-regulated chassis.", "claim_genome": "A cyanobacterium that senses inorganic carbon and light-cycle metabolic state to dynamically balance growth, carbon fixation, and PHA storage will achieve more efficient conversion of captured CO2 into biodegradable bioplastic than static PHA-expression strains or carbon-fixation-enhanced strains engineered in isolation."}}
```

topic: [mathematics] Develop quantitative stability results for optimal transport maps under perturbations of the source and target measures, with emphasis on Wasserstein-2 geometry
full_prompt:
Research topic: Develop quantitative stability results for optimal transport maps under perturbations of the source and target measures, with emphasis on Wasserstein-2 geometry and applications to generative modeling.

Discipline: mathematics

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I first search broadly within the supplied 2018–2025 window to identify what the available corpus surfaces around Wasserstein geometry, stability, uncertainty, and generative modeling. Since the provided OpenAlex candidates are mostly adjacent rather than exact optimal-transport stability papers, I will use the closest ones to motivate the application-facing gap.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Wasserstein stability optimal transport maps generative modeling uncertainty", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The retrieved OpenAlex sample is dominated by adjacent ML/UQ and modeling papers rather than core mathematical OT stability papers. The closest application-facing hits are oa:W4383604608, “Loss Functions and Metrics in Deep Learning,” which discusses Wasserstein-type metrics among modern learning losses, and oa:W4378802379, a review of machine learning with data assimilation and uncertainty quantification. Other retrieved candidates concern sensitivity analysis, adversarial deep learning, and porous-media image processing, useful mainly as motivation for robustness and perturbation analysis.

To ground the generative-modeling motivation, I read the deep-learning metrics survey, since Wasserstein losses are frequently used as proxies for distributional alignment but often without quantitative map-stability guarantees.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4383604608"}
```
[result]: oa:W4383604608, “Loss Functions and Metrics in Deep Learning” (2023), surveys loss functions and performance metrics used in neural-network training and evaluation. Its abstract emphasizes the role of task-dependent metrics, including distributional distances, in shaping empirical behavior of learned models. For this topic, the relevant takeaway is that Wasserstein-type losses are widely attractive in generative modeling because they encode geometry, yet the survey is not concerned with sharp mathematical stability of the induced transport maps under perturbations of empirical source and target laws.

I next read the UQ/data-assimilation review because the proposed mathematics should ideally connect perturbation bounds to uncertainty propagation in learned dynamical or generative systems.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4378802379"}
```
[result]: oa:W4378802379, “Machine Learning With Data Assimilation and Uncertainty Quantification for Dynamical Systems: A Review” (2023), reviews how ML is combined with data assimilation and UQ to reduce and track error propagation in high-dimensional spatiotemporal systems. The paper frames uncertainty as a central obstacle in data-driven modeling, especially when distributions evolve or are observed empirically. It does not provide optimal-transport map stability theorems, but it motivates why perturbation-to-map estimates would be valuable for certifying distributional generative models and transport-based updates.

Before proposing, I check whether the key mechanism—localized quantitative stability of Brenier maps under simultaneous source and target perturbations, with sample-level generative-model consequences—looks redundant or plausibly novel relative to nearby work.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Prove localized Wasserstein-2 stability estimates for Brenier optimal transport maps under simultaneous perturbations of source and target measures, then convert them into finite-sample certification bounds for transport-based generative models.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: verdict: healthy. The closest neighborhood contains work on Wasserstein losses in machine learning, sensitivity and uncertainty analysis, and qualitative or regularity-based stability of optimal transport, but the specific combination of two-sided perturbations, localized map-norm estimates, and finite-sample generative-model certification is not surfaced as redundant in the supplied corpus. The idea is connected to the retrieved ML/UQ literature while addressing a sharper mathematical gap.

I now formulate the follow-up project as a theorem-driven mathematical program with an explicit bridge to generative modeling: stability of optimal maps, not merely stability of transport costs.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a quantitative perturbation theory for Brenier maps T_{\\mu\\to\\nu}=\\nabla\\varphi in W_2 geometry: under uniform convexity/log-concavity and Sobolev or C^{2,\\alpha} density bounds on source and target measures, prove estimates of the form ||T_{\\mu\\to\\nu}-T_{\\tilde\\mu\\to\\tilde\\nu}||_{L^2(\\mu;K)} \\le C_K[W_2(\\mu,\\tilde\\mu)^\\alpha+W_2(\\nu,\\tilde\\nu)^\\beta+\\mathcal A(\\mu,\\tilde\\mu)] for compact interior regions K, with extensions to entropic and empirical OT maps via bias-variance decompositions.", "niche_genome": "Mathematical optimal transport and Wasserstein-2 geometry, with application to certification of transport-based generative modeling where both the latent/source distribution and data/target distribution are estimated or perturbed.", "observation_genome": "Expected results are sharper map-level stability bounds than cost-level W_2 continuity alone: small perturbations of empirical or smoothed source/target laws should imply explicit L^2 or localized C^0 control of the learned transport map, yielding finite-sample rates for generator instability that degrade near density degeneracies or support boundaries.", "limitation_genome": "The strongest estimates will likely require restrictive regularity assumptions, such as bounded positive densities on convex supports, curvature or log-concavity conditions, and interior localization; global estimates may fail or have worse exponents when supports are nonconvex, densities vanish, or optimal maps are discontinuous.", "delta_genome": "Compared with ML surveys on Wasserstein losses and UQ, this shifts the object of analysis from scalar loss stability or empirical performance to stability of the actual optimal map used as a generator. Compared with classical OT regularity, it emphasizes simultaneous perturbation of both marginals, explicit Wasserstein-2 moduli, and finite-sample consequences for learned generative maps.", "claim_genome": "Under natural quantitative regularity assumptions, Brenier generative maps are not merely qualitatively stable but admit explicit Wasserstein-2 perturbation moduli; these moduli can become rigorous uncertainty-certification tools for generative models trained by OT or entropic-OT objectives."}}
```

Discipline: mathematics

I will first locate the most directly relevant papers in the supplied 2018–2025 window, prioritizing invariant Gibbs measures for dispersive PDE and probabilistic techniques for rough data. The search should separate topical matches from the unrelated review hits in the candidate list.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "invariant Gibbs measures nonlinear Schrödinger rough data probabilistic well-posedness", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns oa:W2981173975, “Invariant Gibbs measures and global strong solutions for nonlinear Schrödinger equations in dimension two” (2019), as the strongest match. It also returns oa:W3035383659, “Random tensors, propagation of randomness, and nonlinear dispersive equations” (2020), which is relevant to probabilistic multilinear estimates. A broader stochastic PDE review, oa:W2976375201 (2019), appears as methodological background, while the metamaterials and elastic inverse problems papers are off-topic for this mathematical question.

The 2019 Gibbs-measure paper looks like the core parent: it directly addresses global strong solutions for rough random data drawn from an invariant Gibbs measure. I will read it to identify the equation, renormalization, and long-time mechanism.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2981173975"}

The work is powerful for smooth spectral data and quantization, but it does not primarily develop a wall-crossing formalism for singular Hitchin fibers or compare the resulting recursion invariants with perverse/nonabelian Hodge filtrations.

topic: [astronomy] Map the distribution of dark matter in galaxy clusters through weak gravitational lensing and compare reconstructed mass profiles with predictions from cold and
full_prompt:
Research topic: Map the distribution of dark matter in galaxy clusters through weak gravitational lensing and compare reconstructed mass profiles with predictions from cold and self-interacting dark matter models.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first search the 2018–2025 literature for lensing-based dark-matter mapping papers, emphasizing cluster-scale mass reconstruction and cosmological lensing surveys that can anchor a follow-up idea.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "weak gravitational lensing dark matter mass maps galaxy clusters self interacting dark matter", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several broad cosmology and lensing-relevant works from the provided OpenAlex set. The most directly relevant is oa:W4365211529, “The Atacama Cosmology Telescope: DR6 Gravitational Lensing Map and Cosmological Parameters” (2024), which presents a large-area CMB lensing mass map. Also returned are oa:W3043102132 on SDSS-IV/eBOSS cosmological implications, oa:W3135850969 reviewing tensions and extensions to ΛCDM, oa:W2403089519 on dark matter history, and oa:W4280522707 on LISA cosmology.

The ACT DR6 lensing-map paper is the strongest methodological parent because it concerns mass-map reconstruction from gravitational lensing over a large area. I’ll read it to identify what is mature in current lensing reconstruction and what remains open for cluster-scale dark-matter model tests.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4365211529"}
```
[result]: “The Atacama Cosmology Telescope: DR6 Gravitational Lensing Map and Cosmological Parameters” presents a CMB lensing convergence map over roughly 9400 deg² using ACT DR6 temperature and polarization data. The paper emphasizes improved map depth, control of foreground/systematic biases, cross-correlations with external tracers, and cosmological parameter inference consistent with ΛCDM within current uncertainties. Its strength is wide-area projected mass reconstruction, but it is not primarily designed to resolve individual cluster inner profiles or discriminate CDM from self-interacting dark matter on cluster cores and merger offsets.

To connect the proposal to the broader dark-matter model context, I’ll read the ΛCDM/tensions review, which can justify testing nonstandard dark-matter physics with astrophysical structure probes rather than only background cosmology.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3135850969"}
```
[result]: “In the realm of the Hubble tension—a review of solutions” surveys extensions to the minimal ΛCDM model motivated by observational tensions and theoretical ignorance about dark sectors. While focused mainly on expansion-rate tensions, it frames dark matter and dark energy as phenomenological sectors where new physics may be constrained by combining independent probes. The review does not provide cluster-scale weak-lensing reconstructions, but it motivates using nonlinear structure and mass distributions as complementary tests of cold versus interacting dark-matter scenarios.

I’ll extract the structured genome of the ACT lensing-map paper to make the inheritance and gap clearer: wide-field lensing reconstruction is strong, but cluster-scale dark-matter microphysics discrimination is less developed.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4365211529"}
```
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = reconstruct large-area CMB lensing convergence maps from ACT DR6 maps with bias-hardening, simulations, and cross-correlation validation; niche = cosmological mass mapping and ΛCDM parameter constraints from CMB lensing; observation = high-significance lensing map and cosmological constraints broadly consistent with Planck/ΛCDM; limitation = projected mass resolution and foreground/systematic control limit sensitivity to small cluster cores and dark-matter microphysics; delta = expands sky coverage and precision of CMB lensing mass maps; claim = CMB lensing maps can provide robust large-scale structure constraints complementary to galaxy surveys.

Before proposing, I’ll run a novelty check around a joint weak-lensing/CMB-lensing cluster-profile test of self-interacting dark matter, to avoid simply restating existing lensing-map work.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Jointly reconstruct galaxy-cluster mass profiles using optical weak lensing plus CMB lensing and compare radial cores, centroid offsets, and substructure survival against CDM and self-interacting dark matter simulation emulators.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

Similar work exists on weak-lensing cluster mass calibration, CMB-lensing cross-correlations, and SIDM constraints from individual merging clusters, but few studies combine wide-field CMB lensing, deep optical weak lensing, and simulation-emulated SIDM profile diagnostics in a homogeneous cluster sample. The closest provided parent is oa:W4365211529 for lensing-map methodology, but it does not focus on cluster-resolved CDM versus SIDM discrimination.

The follow-up should inherit robust large-area lensing reconstruction from ACT-like maps, mutate the niche to cluster-resolved dark-matter microphysics, and add a comparative inference layer against CDM/SIDM simulations. I’ll now propose the final research genome.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Build a hierarchical cluster mass-mapping pipeline that jointly fits optical galaxy weak-lensing shear, CMB-lensing convergence cutouts from ACT-like maps, cluster-member light, and X-ray/SZ centroids; infer nonparametric projected mass maps and deprojected radial profiles, then compare them with differentiable simulation emulators spanning CDM and velocity-dependent self-interacting dark matter cross sections.", "niche_genome": "Galaxy-cluster dark-matter distribution mapping at 0.1–3 virial radii for statistically selected relaxed and merging clusters in the 2018–2025 wide-survey era, using clusters overlapping optical imaging and CMB-lensing coverage.", "observation_genome": "The study should recover robust outer mass profiles from CMB lensing plus optical shear while optical weak lensing constrains inner and intermediate radii; topic: [astronomy] Use pulsar timing arrays to constrain the nanohertz gravitational-wave background produced by merging supermassive black hole binaries.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Use pulsar timing arrays to constrain the nanohertz gravitational-wave background produced by merging supermassive black hole binaries.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first locate the most relevant PTA papers in the 2018–2025 window, prioritizing work that directly searches for or interprets a nanohertz stochastic background from supermassive black hole binaries.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "pulsar timing array isotropic stochastic nanohertz gravitational wave background supermassive black hole binaries", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex candidates: oa:W4213112027, “The International Pulsar Timing Array second data release: Search for an isotropic gravitational wave background” (2021); oa:W2900693399, “The astrophysics of nanohertz gravitational waves” (2019); oa:W4391229744, “The second data release from the European Pulsar Timing Array” (2024); oa:W3156992276, a broader 2021 review of gravitational-wave astronomy; and oa:W4383704252, a 2023 paper interpreting PTA signals in an inflationary context. The most central empirical parent is the IPTA DR2 isotropic-background search.

I will read the IPTA DR2 paper because it is directly about constraining an isotropic stochastic nanohertz background using a global PTA dataset.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4213112027"}
```

[result]: The paper reports a search for an isotropic stochastic gravitational-wave background in the International Pulsar Timing Array second data release, combining long-baseline timing of many millisecond pulsars from multiple PTA collaborations. It models common red noise processes and searches for the Hellings–Downs spatial correlation expected from a gravitational-wave background. The study finds evidence for a common-spectrum low-frequency process but does not claim a definitive Hellings–Downs-correlated detection, so it places constraints on the amplitude and spectral behavior of a possible background. Its limitations include sensitivity to pulsar-intrinsic red noise, uneven sky coverage, and degeneracies between a true GWB and other common low-frequency processes.

I will also read a review-style parent paper to understand the astrophysical interpretation of a nanohertz background from merging supermassive black hole binaries.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2900693399"}
```

[result]: This review summarizes how PTAs probe nanohertz gravitational waves produced primarily by cosmological populations of supermassive black hole binaries. It explains the canonical circular, gravitational-wave-driven prediction of a characteristic strain spectrum close to \(h_c \propto f^{-2/3}\), while emphasizing that stellar scattering, gas coupling, eccentricity, and finite-number source effects can produce spectral turnover, anisotropy, or non-Gaussianity. The paper highlights that PTA measurements can constrain galaxy merger rates, black-hole–host scaling relations, binary environmental evolution, and the abundance of individually resolvable nearby binaries. It identifies a key challenge: translating a common red process into robust astrophysical population constraints rather than merely fitting a phenomenological power law.

To make the inheritance from the IPTA search explicit, I will extract its structured “genome” before designing a follow-up method.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4213112027"}
```

[result]: mechanism_genome: Bayesian and frequentist PTA analysis of timing residuals using common-spectrum red-noise models plus spatial-correlation tests for an isotropic stochastic background. niche_genome: global millisecond-pulsar timing datasets targeting nanohertz GWs. observation_genome: common low-frequency power can appear before the Hellings–Downs correlation is significant. limitation_genome: isotropic Gaussian power-law assumptions compress astrophysical information and can be degenerate with clock, ephemeris, and pulsar noise. delta_genome: improves sensitivity by combining international PTA baselines and pulsar sets. claim_genome: IPTA-scale datasets can constrain or eventually detect an isotropic nanohertz GWB.

The parent papers suggest a gap: PTA analyses often fit a phenomenological common spectrum, while SMBHB astrophysics predicts structured deviations from a simple isotropic Gaussian background. I will check whether a proposed catalog-informed, non-Gaussian, environmentally coupled population model is likely novel rather than redundant.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A hierarchical PTA inference model that jointly fits Hellings-Downs correlations, anisotropic non-Gaussian shot noise from nearby supermassive black hole binaries, and environmental hardening parameters using galaxy merger catalogs as informative population priors.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on anisotropic PTA searches, environmental effects on the stochastic background, and SMBHB population synthesis, but most analyses treat these components separately or use phenomenological spectral parameters. The combination of catalog-informed local shot-noise modeling, environmental turnover inference, and standard Hellings–Downs detection statistics appears connected to prior work but not redundant. The main risk is computational complexity and dependence on incomplete galaxy catalogs.

I will now propose a follow-up study that mutates the IPTA-style isotropic search into an astrophysically interpretable hierarchical inference framework for SMBHB populations.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical Bayesian PTA analysis that replaces the single isotropic power-law GWB model with a three-component SMBHB population model: (1) an isotropic Gaussian background from the unresolved cosmological binary population, (2) an anisotropic non-Gaussian shot-noise component seeded by nearby galaxy merger catalogs and candidate massive hosts, and (3) a low-frequency spectral-turnover module parameterized by stellar-scattering, gas-driven migration, and eccentricity hyperparameters. The likelihood would still include Hellings–Downs cross-correlations, pulsar red-noise terms, clock errors, and solar-system ephemeris uncertainties, but would infer astrophysical SMBHB population parameters directly rather than only a strain amplitude and spectral index.", "niche_genome": "Nanohertz gravitational-wave background constraints from pulsar timing arrays, especially IPTA/EPTA/NANOGrav-style datasets seeking to determine whether the observed common red process is consistent with merging supermassive black hole binaries.", "observation_genome": "Compared with a phenomenological common-spectrum power-law analysis, the method should tighten constraints on the SMBHB merger-rate normalization, black-hole–host-galaxy scaling relation, and binary environmental coupling, while identifying whether any excess angular power or spectral turnover is driven by a small number of nearby massive systems. Simulated IPTA-like datasets should show improved discrimination between a pure SMBHB background, alternative early-Universe backgrounds, and terrestrial/common-noise contaminants.", "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on incomplete and selection-biased galaxy merger catalogs, uncertain black-hole masses in host galaxies, and expensive trans-dimensional inference over continuous background plus discrete-source components. It may be most valuable only once PTA datasets have sufficient pulsar sky coverage and timing baselines to resolve anisotropy or non-Gaussianity beyond a common red spectrum.", "delta_genome": "Where prior IPTA searches primarily constrain an isotropic stochastic background through common-spectrum and Hellings–Downs statistics, this proposal turns the PTA background search into a population-level astrophysical measurement. It inherits PTA cross-correlation rigor from isotropic searches but mutates the signal model to include catalog-informed anisotropy, finite-source shot noise, and environmental spectral turnover predicted by SMBHB astrophysics.", "claim_genome": "A catalog-informed, environmentally parameterized, non-Gaussian hierarchical PTA model will yield more physically interpretable and potentially tighter constraints on the nanohertz gravitational-wave background from merging supermassive black hole binaries than standard isotropic power-law analyses, while also testing whether the emerging common red signal has the angular and spectral structure expected from the SMBHB population."}}
```

topic: [astronomy] Study the chemical evolution of the Milky Way by combining Gaia astrometry with large spectroscopic surveys to trace stellar populations across the disk, bulge,
full_prompt:
Research topic: Study the chemical evolution of the Milky Way by combining Gaia astrometry with large spectroscopic surveys to trace stellar populations across the disk, bulge, and halo.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll start by locating the most relevant recent survey papers that combine Gaia-era astrometry with large spectroscopic datasets for Galactic archaeology across multiple Milky Way components. The retrieved candidates should help identify both mature data products and upcoming survey opportunities.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Gaia spectroscopic surveys Milky Way chemical evolution disk bulge halo", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several highly relevant survey and synthesis papers. Key candidates include oa:W4361286336, “StarHorse results for spectroscopic surveys and Gaia DR3,” which explicitly combines Gaia DR3 with spectroscopic surveys for chrono-chemical population inference; oa:W4292421555, “Overview of the DESI Milky Way Survey,” describing a major upcoming spectroscopic mapping program; and oa:W4290091346, the Gaia-ESO public survey paper, documenting a large abundance survey designed for Galactic archaeology. APOGEE-2 targeting papers, oa:W4200501154 and oa:W4200050338, provide complementary high-resolution near-infrared coverage of obscured disk and bulge regions.

The StarHorse paper appears central because it already performs cross-survey Gaia-based inference of ages, distances, and chemical populations. I’ll read it first to understand the current state of chrono-chemo-dynamical mapping.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4361286336"}
```

[result]: The paper “StarHorse results for spectroscopic surveys and Gaia DR3: Chrono-chemical populations in the solar vicinity, the…” presents value-added stellar parameters, distances, extinctions, masses, and ages inferred by combining Gaia DR3 astrometry/photometry with multiple spectroscopic surveys. Its abstract emphasizes the synergy between Gaia and spectroscopy for mapping stellar populations by age, metallicity, and abundance across Galactic environments. It highlights chrono-chemical structure in the solar vicinity and broader Milky Way components, while relying on heterogeneous survey footprints and selection functions. A key limitation is that the resulting maps are rich but not necessarily produced by a single generative model that jointly accounts for survey selection, radial migration, and accreted versus in-situ origin.

To identify how upcoming data could extend this work spatially and statistically, I’ll read the DESI Milky Way Survey overview. DESI is especially relevant because it will add enormous spectroscopic coverage that could transform Gaia-based chemo-dynamical mapping.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4292421555"}
```

[result]: The paper “Overview of the DESI Milky Way Survey” describes the Milky Way Survey planned with the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument on the Mayall 4 m telescope. It outlines target selection, observing strategy, and expected stellar samples for mapping the Galaxy’s disk, halo, and substructures at large scale. The survey is lower resolution than APOGEE or Gaia-ESO for detailed abundances, but its very large sample size and broad footprint make it powerful for kinematics, metallicities, stellar populations, and halo archaeology when combined with Gaia. The paper’s opportunity is scale: DESI can connect local high-precision chemo-dynamics to global Milky Way structure.

I’ll extract a structured “genome” from the StarHorse paper to make explicit what should be inherited and what can be mutated in a follow-up idea. This helps distinguish a genuine conceptual advance from simply applying the same pipeline to more stars.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4361286336"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome for oa:W4361286336: mechanism_genome: Bayesian inference with StarHorse combining Gaia DR3 astrometry/photometry and spectroscopic survey inputs to infer stellar distances, extinctions, ages, masses, and population labels. niche_genome: chrono-chemical dissection of Milky Way stellar populations using heterogeneous large spectroscopic surveys. observation_genome: age–metallicity–abundance patterns reveal disk populations, local substructure, and gradients when Gaia constraints are folded into spectroscopic samples. limitation_genome: cross-survey heterogeneity, selection effects, age uncertainties, and incomplete global modeling limit causal interpretation of radial migration and accretion histories. delta_genome: moves from single-survey abundance catalogs toward multi-survey Gaia-spectroscopy value-added catalogs. claim_genome: Gaia plus spectroscopy enables a coherent empirical reconstruction of Milky Way stellar population structure.

The gap seems to be not just more data, but a selection-function-aware generative model that infers time-resolved origins and migration histories across disk, bulge, and halo. Before proposing it, I’ll check whether that mechanism appears redundant with existing work.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A selection-function-aware hierarchical chemo-dynamical model combining Gaia DR3 astrometry with APOGEE, Gaia-ESO, and DESI spectroscopy to infer stellar birth radii, migration histories, and in-situ versus accreted probabilities across the Milky Way.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work includes StarHorse Gaia-plus-spectroscopy parameter inference, APOGEE chemodynamical mapping, Gaia-Enceladus/Sausage accretion studies, and DESI Milky Way Survey planning, but these generally do not implement a unified field-level hierarchical model that simultaneously propagates survey selection functions, abundance systematics, orbital actions, and birth-radius inference across disk, bulge, and halo. The closest parent is oa:W4361286336, but the proposed mechanism adds causal population-history inference rather than producing only value-added stellar parameters and empirical maps.

Given the literature, the strongest follow-up is a generative “Milky Way fossil-record” model that treats the surveys themselves as biased observations of an underlying Galactic population. The proposal inherits Gaia-plus-spectroscopy synergy from StarHorse and the scale of DESI, while adding explicit selection functions, cross-survey abundance calibration, and probabilistic origin labels.

topic: [astronomy] Examine the dynamics and composition of small bodies in the outer Solar System to reconstruct the migration history of the giant planets.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Examine the dynamics and composition of small bodies in the outer Solar System to reconstruct the migration history of the giant planets.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first search broadly within the 2018–2025 window for papers connecting planetary-system architecture, disks, resonances, and migration, because the outer Solar System problem depends on both compositional reservoirs and dynamical transport histories.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "outer Solar System small bodies giant planet migration resonances composition", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several adjacent but useful papers from the provided OpenAlex set: `oa:W2998834512` “Observations of Protoplanetary Disk Structures” (2020), `oa:W3121609036` “Six transiting planets and a chain of Laplace resonances in TOI-178” (2021), and broader context papers on exoplanet characterization, stellar clusters, and MESA. The most relevant for this topic are the disk-structure review, which informs where planetesimal reservoirs originate, and the resonant-chain paper, which illustrates how fragile dynamical architectures preserve migration histories.

I will read the protoplanetary disk structures review to ground the proposed Solar System study in observational evidence about where small-body reservoirs and compositional gradients may have formed.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2998834512"}
```
[result]: `oa:W2998834512`, “Observations of Protoplanetary Disk Structures” (2020), reviews high-resolution observations of rings, gaps, spirals, shadows, and asymmetries in young disks. The paper emphasizes that disk substructures can concentrate solids, alter dust growth and drift, and potentially mark planet-forming zones or pressure traps. For the outer Solar System question, its relevance is that primordial radial compositional structure may have been sculpted before or during giant-planet migration, so present-day small-body colors and volatiles should not be interpreted using dynamics alone.

Next I will read the resonant-chain paper because resonances are a key fossil of migration, and analogous resonant populations in the Kuiper belt and Trojan swarms constrain how smoothly or violently the giant planets moved.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3121609036"}
```
[result]: `oa:W3121609036`, “Six transiting planets and a chain of Laplace resonances in TOI-178” (2021), reports a compact multiplanet system with a long resonant chain. The abstract frames resonant architectures as fragile records of convergent migration and subsequent dynamical perturbation. Although the system is extrasolar and much more compact than the giant-planet region, the paper is relevant by analogy: preserved or broken resonant structures can encode the timescale, smoothness, and instability history of planetary migration.

I will extract a structured genome from the resonant-chain paper to make explicit which elements can be inherited and mutated into an outer Solar System small-body project.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3121609036"}
```
[result]: The extracted genome identifies the mechanism as using precise orbital architecture, especially resonance chains, to infer migration and subsequent perturbation history. The niche is compact multiplanet exosystems, the observation is that fragile resonant configurations imply constrained dynamical evolution, and the main limitation is that orbital architecture alone is degenerate without independent compositional or age information. This suggests a useful mutation for Solar System work: combine resonance/dynamical class with surface composition to break migration-history degeneracies.

Before proposing, I will check whether the combined mechanism—joint compositional and dynamical inversion of small-body populations to infer giant-planet migration—is likely novel rather than a direct restatement of prior dynamical modeling.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Infer the giant planets' migration history by jointly fitting Kuiper belt, Trojan, Centaur, and irregular-satellite orbital distributions together with multi-band surface colors, volatile diagnostics, and size-albedo constraints in a hierarchical dynamical-compositional model.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: “healthy.” The closest related work clusters around either dynamical-only migration constraints, color taxonomy of trans-Neptunian objects, or individual-population studies of Trojans and Centaurs. The proposed mechanism appears novel because it treats composition as a transported tracer field jointly inverted with N-body migration pathways across multiple small-body reservoirs.

The final proposal should mutate the resonance-as-fossil idea from exoplanet architecture into a Solar System fossil-record framework, while inheriting the disk-structure insight that primordial compositional gradients are spatially structured rather than uniform.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical dynamical-compositional inversion framework that forward-models giant-planet migration tracks and planetesimal scattering using N-body ensembles, then jointly fits present-day orbital classes, resonance occupancy, broadband colors, near-infrared volatile/organic spectral slopes, diameters, and albedos for Kuiper belt objects, Centaurs, Jupiter/Neptune Trojans, and irregular satellites. The model will treat primordial disk composition as a radially structured tracer field shaped by disk substructures, then infer which migration histories best transport those tracers into the observed small-body reservoirs.", "niche_genome": "Outer Solar System small bodies as fossil tracers of giant-planet migration, especially populations that may share source regions but experienced different capture pathways: cold and hot classical Kuiper belt objects, resonant TNOs, scattered disk objects, Centaurs, Trojans, and irregular satellites.", "observation_genome": "A successful model should recover not only the known orbital architecture of the trans-Neptunian region but also correlated compositional gradients across dynamically distinct populations, such as whether ultra-red material, volatile-rich surfaces, and low-albedo neutral objects map to specific source radii and capture epochs. The expected empirical result is that smooth migration, jumping-Neptune migration, and delayed instability scenarios produce measurably different joint distributions in resonance occupation, inclination, color, and volatile retention.", "limitation_genome": "The approach will be limited by surface-processing effects such as irradiation, collisions, cometary activity, and volatile loss, which can partially erase primordial composition. It will also depend on selection-function corrections for Rubin/LSST discoveries, JWST follow-up biases, uncertain small-body masses, and the computational cost of large N-body plus compositional-transport ensembles.", "delta_genome": "Prior work often constrains giant-planet migration using orbital distributions alone or studies small-body colors and spectra population by population. This project changes the inference target by treating composition as a second conserved-but-evolving coordinate, analogous to a dynamical tracer, and by fitting multiple captured reservoirs simultaneously rather than interpreting the Kuiper belt, Trojans, Centaurs, and irregular satellites in isolation.", "claim_genome": "The main hypothesis is that the migration history of the giant planets is overdetermined when small-body dynamics and composition are modeled together: scenarios that are degenerate in orbital space will predict distinct compositional mixing patterns, allowing the timing, smoothness, and radial extent of giant-planet migration to be reconstructed more tightly than with dynamics alone."}}
```

topic: [astronomy] Simulate accretion flows and relativistic jets around supermassive black holes using general relativistic magnetohydrodynamics, calibrated against Event Horizon
full_prompt:
Research topic: Simulate accretion flows and relativistic jets around supermassive black holes using general relativistic magnetohydrodynamics, calibrated against Event Horizon Telescope observations.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first locate the most relevant EHT/GRMHD literature in the requested 2018–2025 window, prioritizing papers that connect horizon-scale polarization, accretion-state inference, and jet launching physics.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "EHT GRMHD simulations M87 magnetic field jet accretion", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex candidates. The strongest match is `oa:W3137038733`, “First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. VIII. Magnetic Field Structure near The Event Horizon” (2021), which uses EHT polarimetric imaging to constrain magnetic field structure and GRMHD model classes near M87*. Another relevant background paper is `oa:W3038496892`, “Magnetohydrodynamics Simulations of Active Galactic Nucleus Disks and Jets” (2020), a broader review/synthesis of AGN disk and jet MHD simulations. Other returned papers are less central, focusing on LISA astrophysics, ULXs, or AGN feedback at larger scales.

I will read the EHT polarization paper because it is the key observational calibration target for GRMHD simulations of M87*, especially for distinguishing magnetically arrested disks from weaker-field accretion states.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3137038733"}
```
[result]: `oa:W3137038733` reports the first EHT polarized images of M87* at 230 GHz, revealing ordered linear polarization around the emission ring on event-horizon scales. The paper compares the observed polarized structure with libraries of GRMHD simulations and finds that models with dynamically important magnetic fields, especially magnetically arrested disk-like states, better reproduce the observed polarization fractions, ring morphology, and inferred magnetic field geometry. It emphasizes that polarization provides constraints beyond total intensity, but also notes degeneracies involving electron thermodynamics, viewing geometry, Faraday effects, and time variability. The work establishes EHT polarimetry as a direct probe of magnetic flux accumulation and jet-launching conditions close to the black hole.

To understand the simulation lineage and where a follow-up could add value, I will read the broader AGN disk/jet MHD simulation paper.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3038496892"}
```
[result]: `oa:W3038496892` reviews magnetohydrodynamic simulations of AGN accretion disks and jets, emphasizing how large-scale magnetic flux, disk thickness, black hole spin, radiative cooling, and plasma microphysics influence jet power and disk structure. It describes the contrast between standard-and-normal-evolution and magnetically arrested disk regimes, highlighting the Blandford–Znajek mechanism as a route for extracting black hole rotational energy. The paper also discusses persistent uncertainties: subgrid electron heating, kinetic-scale dissipation, radiative transfer assumptions, and the difficulty of connecting near-horizon GRMHD simulations to multiwavelength and long-timescale observables. It frames GRMHD as mature but still limited by uncertain plasma closure prescriptions and observational calibration.

I will extract the genome of the EHT polarization paper to identify which elements should be inherited and which can be mutated into a new proposal.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3137038733"}
```
[result]: Genome extraction identifies the mechanism as comparing 230 GHz EHT polarimetric images of M87* against libraries of GRMHD plus polarized radiative transfer models. The niche is horizon-scale magnetic-field inference in a low-luminosity jet-producing supermassive black hole. The core observation is that ordered ring-scale polarization favors dynamically important magnetic fields and disfavors many weak-field models, though not all degeneracies are broken. The main limitations are sparse temporal coverage, uncertain electron thermodynamics, simplified variability treatment, and degeneracy between magnetic topology, Faraday rotation, and emission prescriptions.

Before proposing, I will check whether a targeted idea—time-domain, multi-frequency polarized GRMHD calibration with variable electron heating and Faraday depth inference—is likely redundant with existing work or a healthy extension.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Use ensembles of time-dependent GRMHD simulations with adaptive electron-heating prescriptions and polarized radiative transfer at multiple EHT frequencies to infer magnetic flux state, Faraday depth variability, and jet-loading conditions in M87-like black holes.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: The novelty check returns a “healthy” verdict. Existing EHT studies compare GRMHD libraries to total-intensity and polarized 230 GHz images, and some later works explore variability or multi-frequency predictions, but the combination of time-domain Bayesian calibration, adaptive electron thermodynamics, and explicit Faraday-depth variability as a constraint on jet mass loading appears underdeveloped. The closest lineage is the 2021 EHT M87 polarization analysis and broader GRMHD disk/jet simulation work, but the proposed mechanism adds a more dynamical and multi-frequency inference layer.

The proposal should inherit the EHT paper’s use of polarized horizon-scale data and the GRMHD review’s focus on magnetic flux, jet launching, and electron thermodynamics, while mutating the approach from static model selection to time-domain multi-frequency inference.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a Bayesian pipeline that couples long-duration 3D GRMHD simulations of M87-like supermassive black hole accretion flows to polarized radiative transfer at 86, 230, and 345 GHz, with an adaptive electron-heating closure whose parameters respond to local magnetization, plasma beta, and turbulent dissipation. The pipeline would fit not only time-averaged EHT ring morphology and polarization fractions, but also epoch-to-epoch changes in polarization angle, Faraday rotation depth, circular polarization, and jet-footpoint brightness asymmetry.", "niche_genome": "Horizon-scale modeling of low-luminosity, jet-producing supermassive black holes, especially M87*, where EHT polarimetry already constrains near-horizon magnetic fields but leaves degeneracies among MAD/SANE state, electron thermodynamics, Faraday screen structure, and jet mass loading.", "observation_genome": "The expected result is that multi-frequency time-domain polarization will break degeneracies that are invisible in single-frequency static images: MAD models should show more coherent polarization-angle evolution and stronger, more intermittent Faraday-depth signatures tied to magnetic-flux eruptions, while weaker-flux SANE-like models should require implausible electron-heating or density prescriptions to match both 230 GHz polarization and lower-frequency depolarization. The analysis should yield posterior constraints on magnetic flux saturation, electron-to-ion temperature ratios in disk versus funnel-wall regions, and the density of matter loaded onto the jet sheath.", "limitation_genome": "The approach will remain limited by computational cost, sparse EHT temporal sampling, uncertain absolute flux calibration across frequencies, and the fact that fluid GRMHD cannot self-consistently resolve kinetic particle acceleration or nonthermal electron distributions. If the observational cadence is too low, the inferred variability statistics may be prior-dominated by the simulation ensemble.", "delta_genome": "Relative to the 2021 EHT M87 magnetic-field analysis, this changes the calibration target from mostly 230 GHz image-domain model selection to multi-frequency, time-domain polarimetric inference. Relative to standard AGN GRMHD disk/jet simulations, it adds an observation-driven constraint on electron heating and Faraday-active plasma, turning polarization variability into a diagnostic of jet loading and magnetic-flux eruption cycles.", "claim_genome": "A jointly calibrated, multi-frequency, time-dependent GRMHD plus polarized-radiative-transfer model can distinguish magnetic-flux state and electron-heating physics in M87* more robustly than static 230 GHz imaging alone, and can convert EHT polarization variability into quantitative constraints on how relativistic jets are mass-loaded and magnetically launched."}}
```

topic: [astronomy] Search for intermediate-mass black holes in globular clusters and dwarf galaxies using stellar kinematics, X-ray accretion signatures, and gravitational-wave ev
full_prompt:
Research topic: Search for intermediate-mass black holes in globular clusters and dwarf galaxies using stellar kinematics, X-ray accretion signatures, and gravitational-wave event catalogs.

Discipline: astronomy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first identify recent broad reviews or synthesis papers that anchor the three observational channels: cluster/dwarf stellar dynamics, accretion signatures, and gravitational-wave catalogs. The provided OpenAlex candidates include cluster-evolution and GW-roadmap papers that can ground a follow-up idea even if they are not all narrowly about IMBHs.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "intermediate mass black holes globular clusters dwarf galaxies stellar kinematics X-ray gravitational waves", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several broad but relevant astronomy papers. Top candidates include `oa:W2951722332` “Star Clusters Across Cosmic Time” (2019), a review of cluster formation and dynamical evolution; `oa:W3156992276` “Gravitational-wave physics and astronomy in the 2020s and 2030s” (2021), a roadmap for GW source populations and detectors; and several adjacent papers on modified gravity, dark matter near black holes, and astrometric halo mapping. The cluster and GW reviews look most useful for building a multi-probe IMBH search concept.

I will read the star-cluster review first because globular clusters are one of the core IMBH search environments, and their internal dynamics, stellar populations, and observational biases determine how kinematic IMBH claims should be interpreted.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2951722332"}
```

[result]: `oa:W2951722332`, “Star Clusters Across Cosmic Time” (2019), reviews star clusters as laboratories for stellar dynamics, stellar evolution, galaxy formation, and cosmology. The paper emphasizes that cluster observables depend on formation environment, mass segregation, binary populations, relaxation, tidal stripping, and long-term dynamical evolution. For IMBH searches, its most relevant lesson is that central velocity-dispersion or surface-brightness cusps are degenerate with remnants, anisotropy, binaries, and cluster evolutionary history, so single-channel kinematic evidence is rarely decisive.

To reason more explicitly about how a follow-up proposal should inherit and mutate ideas from this cluster review, I will extract its structured research “genome.”

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W2951722332"}
```

[result]: The extracted genome identifies the mechanism as synthesis of observations and simulations of star clusters across cosmic time, linking initial conditions to present-day structure and dynamics. Its niche is broad star-cluster astrophysics, including globular clusters, young massive clusters, and extragalactic cluster systems. Its limitation is that many inferred dynamical signatures are non-unique because different combinations of mass segregation, binaries, stellar remnants, tidal history, and possible central black holes can produce similar observables.

Next I will read the gravitational-wave roadmap paper because IMBHs may appear through intermediate-mass-ratio inspirals, hierarchical mergers, or high-mass binary black hole events, and future catalogs can provide priors complementary to electromagnetic searches.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3156992276"}
```

[result]: `oa:W3156992276`, “Gravitational-wave physics and astronomy in the 2020s and 2030s” (2021), surveys the expected development of GW astronomy with current and next-generation ground- and space-based detectors. It discusses compact-object populations, black-hole mass distributions, merger environments, multi-messenger follow-up, and the role of improved detector networks in source localization and parameter estimation. For IMBH science, the key implication is that GW event catalogs can constrain the demographics of massive black-hole seeds and dynamical assembly channels, but electromagnetic context is needed to distinguish globular-cluster, nuclear-star-cluster, and dwarf-galaxy origins.

Before proposing, I will check whether the intended mechanism—joint hierarchical inference across stellar kinematics, X-ray accretion, and GW catalogs—is likely to be a healthy extension rather than a redundant restatement of existing IMBH searches.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A hierarchical multi-messenger population model that jointly fits globular-cluster and dwarf-galaxy stellar kinematics, nuclear X-ray variability/accretion limits, and gravitational-wave black-hole merger catalog posteriors to infer intermediate-mass black-hole occupancy and rank targets for follow-up.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: “healthy.” Similar work exists in separate channels—cluster kinematic IMBH searches, dwarf-galaxy low-luminosity AGN searches, and GW population studies of massive black holes—but the search finds fewer examples that treat these as one coupled population-inference problem with shared seeding and dynamical-assembly hyperparameters. The novelty appears strongest if the proposal produces testable target rankings and explicitly models false positives from stellar remnants, binaries, anisotropy, and stochastic accretion.

Based on the cluster-dynamics degeneracy highlighted by the star-cluster review and the catalog-level demographic power emphasized by the GW roadmap, I will propose a follow-up that treats IMBH detection as a joint population problem rather than a set of isolated candidate claims. The idea is to combine null detections and ambiguous detections across electromagnetic and GW data into one ranking and inference framework.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical Bayesian multi-messenger inference pipeline, IMBH-Triad, that jointly models: (1) resolved stellar kinematics of globular clusters and nearby dwarf-galaxy nuclei from Gaia/HST/JWST/ELT-class proper motions and line-of-sight velocities; (2) Chandra/XMM/eROSITA X-ray luminosities, variability, and radio/X-ray fundamental-plane constraints as stochastic low-Eddington accretion indicators; and (3) LVK/KAGRA and future LISA/TianQin/Taiji event-catalog posteriors for high-mass mergers, intermediate-mass-ratio inspirals, and hierarchical-merger candidates. The model will infer a shared IMBH occupation fraction, mass function, and environment-dependent seeding channel while marginalizing over binaries, mass segregation, stellar-remnant cusps, anisotropy, tidal stripping, and intermittent accretion.", "niche_genome": "Intermediate-mass black-hole searches in Milky Way globular clusters, Local Group dwarf galaxies, and low-mass galaxy nuclei, with extension to GW event catalogs that may encode dynamically assembled or seed-black-hole populations.", "observation_genome": "The expected empirical result is not a single yes/no detection but a calibrated probability map: a ranked list of clusters and dwarf nuclei where an IMBH is favored only when kinematic central-mass evidence, accretion constraints or upper limits, and GW population priors are mutually consistent. The framework should reveal whether apparent kinematic IMBH candidates remain robust after including remnant-cusp and binary-system alternatives, and whether GW high-mass merger rates require an IMBH occupation fraction compatible with electromagnetic non-detections.", "limitation_genome": "The method will be limited by heterogeneous archival data quality, uncertain gas supply and accretion duty cycles, selection effects in GW catalogs, and degeneracies between central IMBHs and concentrated populations of stellar remnants. It may produce broad posterior distributions until next-generation astrometry, deep X-ray monitoring, and improved GW localizations are available.", "delta_genome": "Prior work often treats globular-cluster dynamics, dwarf-galaxy X-ray/AGN searches, and GW black-hole population studies as separate evidence streams. This project changes the unit of inference from individual candidate confirmation to cross-population demographic consistency, using null detections and ambiguous signals as informative constraints and feeding the result back into an optimized target-selection strategy for future telescopes.", "claim_genome": "A joint stellar-kinematic, X-ray-accretion, and GW-catalog population model will reduce false-positive IMBH claims and identify a smaller, higher-purity set of globular clusters and dwarf galaxies where follow-up observations can most decisively test whether IMBHs are common relic seeds or rare products of dense stellar dynamics."}}
```

full_prompt:
Research topic: Optimize long-duration energy storage using iron-air, zinc-bromine, or flow battery chemistries, with emphasis on low-cost materials, degradation mechanisms, and grid dispatch under high renewable penetration.

Discipline: energy

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first search within the provided 2018–2025 window for work connecting redox/metal-flow batteries, low-cost chemistries, degradation, and grid-scale storage. The retrieved set should identify a review or synthesis paper suitable as a lineage anchor.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "metal redox flow batteries long duration grid storage degradation low cost", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex candidates. The strongest matches are oa:W4390967553, “A comprehensive review of metal-based redox flow batteries: progress and perspectives” (2024), and oa:W4385575897, “Redox Flow Batteries: Recent Development in Main Components, Emerging Technologies, Diagnostic Techniques, Large-Scale...” (2023). Broader battery review oa:W4411100479 (2025) also discusses environmental impact and scalability, while the other candidates are less directly aligned with grid-scale electrochemical storage.

I’ll read the 2024 metal-based redox flow battery review because it likely covers zinc-, iron-, and bromine-related chemistries, degradation mechanisms, and scale-up constraints.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4390967553"}
```
[result]: The paper reviews metal-based redox flow batteries as candidates for large-scale storage coupled with intermittent renewables. It emphasizes aqueous chemistries using relatively abundant metals, discusses vanadium, zinc-bromine, iron, and other metal couples, and identifies degradation modes such as crossover, dendrite formation, electrolyte imbalance, membrane fouling, precipitation, and side reactions. The review highlights that cost, cycle life, electrolyte management, and system-level efficiency remain barriers to long-duration deployment. It also notes that grid value depends not only on cell chemistry but on operational strategy under renewable-rich dispatch conditions.

Its niche is grid-scale renewable integration, especially applications requiring multi-hour storage and high cycle life.
