topic: [neuroscience] Develop closed-loop deep brain stimulation strategies for Parkinson’s disease that adapt stimulation parameters in real time using local field potential biomark
full_prompt:
Research topic: Develop closed-loop deep brain stimulation strategies for Parkinson’s disease that adapt stimulation parameters in real time using local field potential biomarkers. The goal is to reduce motor fluctuations and stimulation-induced side effects through personalized neural feedback control.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I first want to identify recent literature that frames adaptive DBS, Parkinson’s phenotyping, and biomarker-driven sensing in the 2018–2025 window. The supplied OpenAlex candidates suggest one DBS-focused review and one PD phenotyping paper are especially relevant anchors.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Parkinson closed-loop deep brain stimulation local field potential biomarkers adaptive DBS", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several broad but relevant papers. The most directly relevant is oa:W4220827800, “Past, Present, and Future of Deep Brain Stimulation: Hardware, Software, Imaging, Physiology and Novel Approaches” (2022), which reviews DBS hardware, physiology, sensing, and emerging approaches. Also relevant is oa:W3026426657, “Deep Phenotyping of Parkinson’s Disease” (2020), which discusses multidimensional measurement of PD heterogeneity. Additional candidates focus on AI-enhanced sensors, biosensors, and digital speech biomarkers, but are less directly tied to DBS control.

The DBS review is the best parent paper for understanding the state of closed-loop stimulation hardware, LFP sensing, and limits of current adaptive DBS systems. I will read it first to ground the proposal in current device and physiology constraints.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4220827800"}
```

[result]: The paper reviews the evolution of DBS across hardware, software, imaging, physiology, and novel therapeutic approaches. It emphasizes that modern DBS is moving from open-loop continuous stimulation toward systems capable of sensing neural activity, including local field potentials, and adjusting stimulation more intelligently. The review highlights beta-band activity in Parkinson’s disease as a key biomarker, while noting that patient heterogeneity, biomarker instability, programming burden, and stimulation-induced side effects remain major barriers. It frames future DBS as increasingly personalized, physiology-guided, and integrated with computational methods.

To reason more explicitly about what can be inherited and mutated from this review, I will extract its structured genome. This should clarify the prior mechanism, niche, limitations, and opportunity space.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4220827800"}
```

[result]: The extracted genome identifies the mechanism as next-generation DBS combining improved electrodes, sensing, imaging, and software-guided programming. The niche is treatment of neurologic and neuropsychiatric disorders, with Parkinson’s disease as a central use case. Its key observation is that DBS efficacy is strong but limited by fixed stimulation, difficult programming, and variable symptom/side-effect tradeoffs. The main limitation is that most systems still lack robust, individualized, real-time feedback policies that generalize across behavior, medication state, and disease heterogeneity.

Because personalized adaptive control depends not only on LFPs but also on patient heterogeneity and behavioral context, I will read the Parkinson’s deep phenotyping paper next. It may suggest how to enrich DBS feedback loops with individualized symptom-state models.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3026426657"}
```

[result]: “Deep Phenotyping of Parkinson’s Disease” discusses how PD varies substantially across motor, non-motor, cognitive, imaging, genetic, molecular, and digital-behavioral dimensions. The paper argues that richer phenotyping can improve stratification, disease monitoring, and individualized therapy. It highlights the need to combine multiple data streams rather than relying on coarse clinical labels alone. For adaptive DBS, this suggests that LFP biomarkers should be interpreted in the context of each patient’s phenotype, medication cycle, and real-world behavioral state.

A likely proposal is to move beyond single-threshold beta-power adaptive DBS toward a personalized controller that jointly models LFP dynamics, motor state, side-effect risk, and phenotype context. Before proposing, I will check whether that mechanism appears redundant with existing work or whether it is a healthy extension.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A personalized closed-loop DBS controller for Parkinson’s disease that uses local field potential beta-burst dynamics plus digital phenotype context to optimize stimulation amplitude, pulse width, frequency, and contact selection under explicit side-effect constraints.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. The closest prior direction is adaptive DBS using beta-band local field potential power or beta bursts as a feedback signal, often with relatively simple threshold or state-dependent amplitude control. The proposed mechanism is distinct because it combines multi-timescale LFP features with patient-specific phenotyping and side-effect-constrained control across multiple stimulation parameters, not just amplitude. It is connected to the DBS review and PD phenotyping literature but not redundant with the papers retrieved here.

The proposal should inherit the LFP-sensing and adaptive DBS trajectory from the DBS review, while mutating it into a context-aware, safety-constrained, personalized controller. It should also inherit deep phenotyping as a way to personalize biomarker interpretation and reduce one-size-fits-all thresholds.

topic: [neuroscience] Investigate how astrocyte-neuron metabolic coupling shapes synaptic plasticity during learning in cortical circuits. This topic emphasizes lactate signaling, ca
full_prompt:
Research topic: Investigate how astrocyte-neuron metabolic coupling shapes synaptic plasticity during learning in cortical circuits. This topic emphasizes lactate signaling, calcium dynamics, and activity-dependent energy allocation.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first map the available recent literature around brain energy metabolism and astrocyte-neuron signaling, prioritizing papers that can anchor a learning/plasticity proposal within the 2018–2025 window.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "astrocyte neuron metabolic coupling lactate calcium synaptic plasticity learning cortical circuits", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant recent review and roadmap papers. Most directly useful are oa:W4390635757, “Brain energy metabolism: A roadmap for future research” (2024), which frames unresolved questions in cell-type-specific energy use, and oa:W4280585775, “Glial-neuron crosstalk in health and disease...” (2022), which emphasizes metabolic astrocyte-neuron interactions and cognition. oa:W4285088092, “Endocannabinoid signaling in astrocytes” (2022), is mechanistically relevant for astrocytic calcium dynamics and activity-dependent signaling, though not primarily metabolic.

I’ll read the 2024 roadmap first because it likely identifies current gaps in brain-energy allocation that a novel proposal can target.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4390635757"}
```

[result]: “Brain energy metabolism: A roadmap for future research” is a 2024 consensus-style review arguing that despite decades of work on glucose, lactate, oxygen use, and neurovascular coupling, the field still lacks mechanistic resolution of how energy substrates are allocated across cell types, compartments, and behavioral states. The paper highlights major open questions around astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttling, genetically encoded metabolic sensors, subcellular energy microdomains, and coupling between cellular metabolism and computation. It emphasizes that future work should connect molecular metabolism to circuit-level function during natural behavior and learning.

To complement the metabolic roadmap, I’ll read the astrocyte endocannabinoid paper because endocannabinoids are a strong candidate link between synaptic activity, astrocytic calcium elevations, and downstream metabolic support.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4285088092"}
```

[result]: “Endocannabinoid signaling in astrocytes” reviews how neuronal activity can recruit astrocytic cannabinoid receptor signaling, leading to intracellular calcium events and modulation of synaptic transmission. The paper emphasizes that astrocytic calcium signals are heterogeneous, spatially localized, and capable of influencing gliotransmission and synaptic plasticity. Although the review focuses more on signaling than metabolism, it suggests a plausible bridge whereby synaptic activity could trigger astrocytic calcium programs that coordinate local metabolic responses.

I’ll extract a structured genome from the roadmap paper to identify which conceptual components should be inherited and where a follow-up study can mutate the prior agenda into a concrete experiment.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4390635757"}
```

[result]: The extracted genome frames the paper’s mechanism as a field-level program for resolving brain energy metabolism across molecules, cell types, and circuits using modern imaging, sensors, and computational models. Its niche is broad: brain energetics across development, cognition, aging, and disease. The main limitation is that it largely articulates priorities rather than testing a specific causal mechanism linking metabolic flux to learning-related synaptic plasticity. Its delta is a call to move from bulk measurements toward dynamic, cell-type- and compartment-resolved energy mapping during behavior.

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the core mechanism—closed-loop manipulation of astrocytic calcium-gated lactate delivery during learning—is likely to be redundant with existing work.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Closed-loop, learning-phase-specific manipulation of astrocytic calcium microdomains and lactate release to test whether local lactate allocation gates cortical spine plasticity.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. The closest nearby literature includes work on astrocyte-neuron lactate transfer, genetically encoded lactate sensors, astrocytic calcium imaging, and astrocyte contributions to synaptic plasticity, but these are usually studied separately or with open-loop perturbations. The proposed combination of behavior-locked calcium/lactate imaging plus closed-loop lactate release or blockade during defined learning phases appears connected to prior work but not redundant.

The proposal should now inherit the roadmap’s emphasis on cell-type-resolved energetic dynamics, integrate astrocytic calcium signaling as a trigger, and specify a causal cortical-learning experiment rather than another broad review-style agenda.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a closed-loop in vivo cortical learning assay in which two-photon imaging simultaneously monitors astrocytic calcium microdomains, extracellular lactate with a genetically encoded lactate sensor, neuronal dendritic spine calcium, and spine structural plasticity during a whisker-dependent sensorimotor learning task; use astrocyte-specific opto/chemogenetic control of calcium microdomains and temporally precise perturbation of MCT1/MCT4-dependent lactate export or local lactate uncaging to test whether lactate pulses must coincide with spine calcium events to stabilize potentiated synapses.", "niche_genome": "Astrocyte-neuron metabolic coupling during activity-dependent synaptic plasticity in learning cortical circuits, focusing on barrel or motor cortex during acquisition of a new sensorimotor association.", "observation_genome": "The expected result is that successful learning will be marked by spatially localized astrocytic calcium events followed within seconds to minutes by lactate transients near active dendrites, and that disrupting lactate export specifically during high spine-calcium learning epochs will impair spine stabilization and behavioral acquisition more strongly than equal-duration disruption outside those epochs.", "limitation_genome": "The approach may be technically demanding because simultaneous multi-sensor imaging and closed-loop perturbation can introduce phototoxicity, sensor crosstalk, and interpretive ambiguity between lactate as an energy substrate versus lactate as a signaling molecule; initial experiments may also be limited to superficial cortical layers and head-fixed behaviors.", "delta_genome": "Where prior work and recent roadmaps call for cell-type-resolved brain-energy measurements, this project adds causal temporal precision by asking whether astrocytic lactate delivery is not merely correlated with activity but phase-locked to calcium-defined plasticity windows that determine which synapses are retained during learning.", "claim_genome": "Learning-related cortical plasticity depends on an astrocytic calcium-gated lactate allocation rule: astrocytes preferentially deliver lactate to recently active synaptic neighborhoods, and the coincidence of local lactate availability with neuronal calcium-driven plasticity signals promotes durable spine stabilization and memory-relevant circuit refinement."}}
```

topic: [neuroscience] Apply machine learning to multimodal neuroimaging data to predict treatment response in major depressive disorder. This topic focuses on biomarkers from fMRI co
full_prompt:
Research topic: Apply machine learning to multimodal neuroimaging data to predict treatment response in major depressive disorder. This topic focuses on biomarkers from fMRI connectivity, structural MRI, and clinical phenotyping.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first identify the most relevant papers in the provided 2018–2025 window, prioritizing work on machine learning for depression treatment prediction and multimodal neuroimaging biomarkers.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "machine learning major depression treatment outcome prediction fMRI structural MRI biomarkers", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant papers. The strongest matches are oa:W2888505821, “Machine learning in major depression: From classification to treatment outcome prediction” (2018), and oa:W2884781986, “Classification and Prediction of Brain Disorders Using Functional Connectivity: Promising but Challenging” (2018). Broader context is provided by oa:W3012009865 on ENIGMA-scale neuroimaging consortia and harmonization across sites.

The 2018 review on machine learning in major depression appears directly aligned with the topic, especially the transition from diagnostic classification to individualized treatment response prediction.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2888505821"}
```

[result]: The paper reviews machine-learning applications in major depressive disorder, emphasizing that diagnostic classifiers have been more common than clinically useful treatment-response predictors. It highlights common predictors including structural MRI, resting-state fMRI connectivity, clinical symptom scales, demographics, and cognitive measures. Major limitations include small samples, cross-site heterogeneity, overfitting, inconsistent validation, and weak interpretability. The paper argues that future work should focus on externally validated, multimodal, clinically actionable prediction models.

I will also read the functional connectivity review because fMRI connectivity is a central biomarker modality in the proposed topic and likely informs methodological limitations.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2884781986"}
```

[result]: This paper reviews the use of functional connectivity features for classification and prediction across brain disorders. It notes that resting-state fMRI connectivity can capture distributed circuit dysfunction but is vulnerable to motion artifacts, site effects, parcellation choices, and poor test-retest reliability. The paper emphasizes that many reported models lack external validation and may fail to generalize across scanners or cohorts. It recommends larger samples, harmonized preprocessing, and models that can handle high-dimensional connectomic features robustly.

To reason more structurally about what is inherited and where a new contribution could sit, I will extract a genome from the depression-focused review.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W2888505821"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = supervised ML models using neuroimaging and clinical features to classify MDD or predict treatment outcome; niche = major depressive disorder diagnosis and treatment-response prediction; observation = multimodal features can show predictive signal but performance is inconsistent; limitation = small cohorts, overfitting, limited external validation, poor clinical interpretability; delta = shifts field from group-level biomarkers toward individualized prediction; claim = ML could enable precision psychiatry if validated on larger multimodal datasets.

A possible gap is that prior work often treats modalities as static baseline predictors. I will check whether an uncertainty-aware longitudinal multimodal model combining fMRI connectivity, structural MRI, and clinical trajectories is likely to be novel rather than redundant.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "An uncertainty-aware longitudinal multimodal graph transformer that integrates baseline structural MRI, dynamic resting-state fMRI connectivity, and early clinical symptom trajectories to predict individualized antidepressant treatment response with site-invariant self-supervised pretraining.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on ML prediction of depression outcomes, functional connectivity classification, and large-scale harmonized imaging datasets, but most models use static baseline features and limited external validation. The combination of longitudinal early-treatment clinical change, dynamic fMRI connectivity, structural priors, uncertainty calibration, and site-invariant pretraining appears distinct from the reviewed papers. The main risk is feasibility, because such a model requires sufficiently large datasets with repeated symptoms and multimodal imaging.

Grounded in the reviewed limitations, I will now propose a follow-up study that emphasizes multimodal integration, temporal prediction, external generalization, and clinically usable uncertainty estimates.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a site-invariant, uncertainty-aware longitudinal multimodal graph transformer for predicting antidepressant treatment response in major depressive disorder. The model will encode structural MRI morphometry as node-level anatomical priors, resting-state fMRI dynamic connectivity as time-varying graph edges, and baseline plus early-treatment clinical phenotyping as temporal covariates. It will be pretrained with self-supervised cross-modal reconstruction and contrastive site-invariance objectives, then fine-tuned to predict 8–12 week symptom remission, response, and nonresponse with calibrated uncertainty.", "niche_genome": "Precision psychiatry for major depressive disorder, specifically treatment-response prediction using multimodal neuroimaging and clinical phenotyping across multi-site cohorts.", "observation_genome": "The expected result is that combining dynamic fMRI connectivity, structural MRI priors, and early symptom-change trajectories will outperform static baseline-only models using either imaging or clinical variables alone. The model should show better external-site generalization and better-calibrated confidence estimates, enabling identification of patients for whom predictions are reliable versus uncertain.", "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on access to multi-site datasets with harmonized structural MRI, resting-state fMRI, treatment metadata, and repeated clinical assessments. It may still be limited by scanner variability, medication heterogeneity, missing modalities, and the difficulty of interpreting transformer-derived graph representations in clinically transparent terms.", "delta_genome": "Compared with prior ML-in-MDD work that mainly used static baseline features and often lacked external validation, this proposal adds longitudinal early-treatment phenotyping, dynamic connectomic representations, structural-connectivity-informed graph modeling, explicit site-invariance training, and calibrated uncertainty estimation.", "claim_genome": "A longitudinal, uncertainty-aware multimodal graph model will produce more generalizable and clinically actionable predictions of depression treatment response than conventional static neuroimaging or clinical machine-learning models."}}
```

topic: [neuroscience] Examine the role of myelin plasticity in adult skill learning using longitudinal MRI, optogenetics, and oligodendrocyte-lineage tracing. The focus is on how exp
full_prompt:
Research topic: Examine the role of myelin plasticity in adult skill learning using longitudinal MRI, optogenetics, and oligodendrocyte-lineage tracing. The focus is on how experience-dependent changes in white matter influence neural computation.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first locate recent work tying myelin biology to adult circuit function and learning. The provided retrieval set appears broad, so I’ll prioritize candidates most directly related to myelination and neural computation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"adult myelin plasticity skill learning MRI optogenetics oligodendrocyte lineage tracing","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returns several partially relevant OpenAlex candidates. The strongest match is oa:W2911633778, “On the Myelination of GABAergic Interneurons” (2019), which focuses on how myelination of inhibitory interneurons may affect circuit timing and computation. Other retrieved papers are less directly aligned: oa:W4379058647 on Down syndrome stem-cell models, oa:W4392468403 on ischemia beyond the penumbra, and oa:W4414925439 on myelination of transplanted neurons for repair.

The interneuron paper looks most relevant because the prompt asks how experience-dependent white-matter changes influence neural computation, not only gross conduction speed. I’ll read it to extract the conceptual lineage around cell-type-specific myelination and circuit timing.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2911633778"}
```

[result]: “On the Myelination of GABAergic Interneurons” reviews evidence that subsets of GABAergic interneurons, especially fast-spiking parvalbumin-positive interneurons, can be myelinated in the CNS. The paper argues that interneuron myelination may support rapid spike propagation, metabolic stability, and precise timing of inhibition, with implications for oscillations and cortical computation. It highlights that interneuron myelination is heterogeneous across regions, species, development, and disease, and that its functional consequences remain difficult to test causally in vivo. The review points to a gap: linking cell-type-specific myelin changes to measurable behavior and circuit dynamics.

To make the proposal more grounded, I’ll extract a structured genome from this paper. This should clarify which elements can be inherited and which need to be extended toward adult skill learning, longitudinal MRI, optogenetics, and lineage tracing.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W2911633778"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome for oa:W2911633778: mechanism_genome: myelination of selected GABAergic interneuron axons, particularly fast-spiking interneurons, may tune inhibitory conduction timing and metabolic support. niche_genome: cortical and subcortical inhibitory microcircuits where precise timing of inhibition shapes oscillations and information processing. observation_genome: interneuron myelination is anatomically heterogeneous and plausibly linked to high-frequency firing, synchrony, and circuit stability. limitation_genome: primarily review/synthesis; lacks direct longitudinal causal tests of experience-dependent interneuron myelin remodeling during adult learning. delta_genome: shifts myelin biology from a neuron-agnostic white-matter support role to a cell-type-specific regulator of computation. claim_genome: inhibitory interneuron myelination is a candidate mechanism for fine-tuning circuit timing and neural computation.

The main opportunity is to connect this cell-type-specific computation idea to adult experience-dependent myelin plasticity and noninvasive longitudinal imaging. I’ll check whether a combined mechanism—MRI-visible myelin change linked to optogenetic disruption of newly formed oligodendrocyte support for interneuron timing during skill learning—appears redundant or still open.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"Longitudinal myelin-sensitive MRI during adult motor skill learning combined with oligodendrocyte-lineage tracing and closed-loop optogenetic disruption of newly generated oligodendrocytes to test whether experience-dependent myelination of inhibitory interneuron axons tunes oscillatory timing and skill consolidation.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. The closest prior work includes studies of adaptive myelination during motor learning, reviews of interneuron myelination, and methods papers using MRI or genetic lineage tracing separately. However, the combined causal chain—human-compatible longitudinal MRI signature → cell-type-resolved oligodendrocyte lineage tracing → optogenetic perturbation of newly myelinating oligodendrocytes → altered inhibitory timing and skill learning—does not appear directly redundant. The main risk is technical feasibility, especially resolving interneuron-specific myelin changes with MRI.

I’ll now propose a follow-up study that inherits the interneuron timing hypothesis but mutates it into a longitudinal, causal adult-learning experiment. The proposal will explicitly bridge scales: MRI-visible white-matter plasticity, oligodendrocyte lineage dynamics, optogenetic perturbation, and neural computation.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Combine longitudinal myelin-sensitive MRI during adult motor skill learning with transgenic oligodendrocyte-lineage tracing and closed-loop optogenetic inhibition of newly differentiated oligodendrocytes in task-relevant motor circuits; pair this with electrophysiology/calcium imaging to test whether new myelin on parvalbumin-interneuron and corticospinal axons alters inhibitory spike timing, beta/gamma phase-locking, and movement-sequence representations.","niche_genome":"Adult experience-dependent white-matter plasticity during acquisition and consolidation of a skilled motor sequence, with emphasis on how oligodendrocyte remodeling affects motor-cortical computation rather than merely conduction speed.","observation_genome":"Animals that learn a new motor sequence should show MRI-detectable increases in myelin-sensitive metrics in specific motor pathways, accompanied by lineage-traced expansion or remodeling of oligodendrocytes near active axons; disrupting newly generated oligodendrocytes during post-training consolidation should selectively impair refinement of spike timing, oscillatory synchrony, and sequence stability without abolishing basic movement execution.","limitation_genome":"MRI cannot by itself identify which axon classes are newly myelinated, and optogenetic manipulation of oligodendrocyte-lineage cells may have off-target metabolic or inflammatory effects; therefore the design requires histological validation, cell-type-specific axon labeling, and careful separation of developmental myelin effects from adult plasticity.","delta_genome":"Extends prior work on interneuron myelination from a largely anatomical/computational hypothesis into a longitudinal causal test of adult learning, linking noninvasive MRI signatures to lineage-resolved oligodendrocyte dynamics and circuit-level timing computations.","claim_genome":"Adult skill learning recruits a temporally specific wave of adaptive myelination that improves neural computation by retiming inhibition and stabilizing task-relevant oscillatory coordination; blocking this new oligodendrocyte contribution will decouple practice from durable circuit optimization and reduce skilled performance gains."}}
```

topic: [neuroscience] Model human cortical organoids with integrated microelectrode arrays to study early network formation and epileptiform activity. This topic addresses how patien
full_prompt:
Research topic: Model human cortical organoids with integrated microelectrode arrays to study early network formation and epileptiform activity. This topic addresses how patient-derived organoids can reveal developmental mechanisms of seizure susceptibility.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first identify central studies combining human cortical/brain organoids with multielectrode recordings and epilepsy-relevant phenotypes in the 2018–2025 window. The search should surface both developmental network formation papers and seizure/epileptiform organoid models.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "human cortical organoids microelectrode arrays epileptiform network development", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex-indexed candidates: **oa:W2976976479**, Trujillo et al., “Complex Oscillatory Waves Emerging from Cortical Organoids Model Early Human Brain Network Development” (Cell Stem Cell, 2019); **oa:W3179146114**, Samarasinghe et al., “Identification of neural oscillations and epileptiform changes in human brain organoids” (Nature Neuroscience, 2021); **oa:W2911359695**, Giandomenico et al., “Cerebral organoids at the air-liquid interface generate diverse nerve tracts with functional output” (Nature Neuroscience, 2019); **oa:W4200391486**, a 2022 paper on MEA-based functional maturation of cortical organoids; and **oa:W4388573046**, a 2024 patient-derived organoid study of developmental epileptic encephalopathy phenotypes. The strongest lineage appears to be Trujillo 2019 for intrinsic oscillatory maturation and Samarasinghe 2021 for epileptiform organoid activity.  

I’ll read the foundational organoid network maturation paper first, because the proposed idea needs to build on how spontaneous activity, oscillations, and developmental trajectories are measured on MEAs.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2976976479"}
```  
[result]: Trujillo et al. report that human cortical organoids develop spontaneous, increasingly complex network activity over months, measurable by multielectrode arrays and calcium imaging. Their abstract emphasizes emergence of nested oscillatory events, synchronization, and developmental trajectories that can be compared with preterm human EEG signatures. The study establishes organoids as a model for early human network formation, but it largely focuses on population-level maturation rather than patient-specific seizure susceptibility mechanisms or controlled perturbation of excitation/inhibition balance.  

Next I’ll read the epilepsy-specific organoid study to understand how epileptiform events were induced or identified and what gaps remain for patient-derived susceptibility modeling.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3179146114"}
```  
[result]: Samarasinghe et al. use human brain organoids to detect neural oscillations and epileptiform-like changes, combining electrophysiology with pharmacological or disease-relevant perturbations. The paper highlights that organoids can show seizure-like bursts, altered synchrony, and responsiveness to anti-epileptic modulation, making them useful for modeling circuit hyperexcitability. A key limitation is that epileptiform phenotypes are often summarized as bulk bursting or spike-rate metrics, with less resolution on which developmental circuit transitions create vulnerability windows in patient-derived organoids.  

I’ll extract a structured genome from the epilepsy-relevant parent paper to make the follow-up idea inherit the right core while mutating a clear limitation.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3179146114"}
```  
[result]: The extracted genome identifies the **mechanism** as long-term electrophysiological monitoring of human brain organoids to quantify oscillations, synchrony, and epileptiform bursts under disease or pharmacological perturbation. The **niche** is early human network excitability and seizure-like activity in organoid models. The **observation** is that organoids can generate measurable oscillatory and epileptiform patterns that respond to circuit-active drugs. The **limitation** is weak mapping from global MEA phenotypes to specific developmental cell-state transitions, microcircuit motifs, and patient-specific risk windows.  

Before proposing, I’ll check whether a more specific mechanism—closed-loop MEA stimulation plus longitudinal single-cell/multimodal readouts for seizure-susceptibility windows—appears redundant or plausibly novel.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Patient-derived cortical organoids grown on integrated microelectrode arrays are longitudinally monitored and closed-loop stimulated while matched single-cell and spatial transcriptomic snapshots identify developmental windows and cell-state predictors of epileptiform transitions.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```  
[result]: Verdict: **healthy**. Similar work exists on organoid MEA maturation, seizure-like bursting, and patient-derived epilepsy organoids, but few papers combine closed-loop perturbation of emerging networks with time-resolved multiomic anchoring of the same organoid lines. The novelty is strongest if the proposal focuses on predicting and causally testing “transition windows” from normal developmental synchrony to pathological hypersynchrony, rather than merely cataloging epileptiform bursts.  

I’ll now propose a follow-up that inherits long-term MEA organoid monitoring but adds closed-loop perturbation and multiomic anchoring to explain patient-specific seizure susceptibility mechanisms.

topic: [neuroscience] Study how sleep-dependent memory consolidation is altered by targeted manipulation of hippocampal sharp-wave ripples and cortical slow oscillations. The problem
full_prompt:
Research topic: Study how sleep-dependent memory consolidation is altered by targeted manipulation of hippocampal sharp-wave ripples and cortical slow oscillations. The problem involves linking specific sleep rhythms to behavioral memory outcomes.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll start by finding recent work in the provided window that frames sleep rhythms, memory consolidation, and candidate circuit oscillations. The goal is to identify which rhythms are already linked to behavior and where causal multi-rhythm manipulation remains underexplored.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sleep memory consolidation hippocampal sharp-wave ripples cortical slow oscillations spindles","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant review and synthesis papers from the supplied OpenAlex set. Top candidates include oa:W2914675220, “Primed to Sleep: The Dynamics of Synaptic Plasticity Across Brain States” (2019), which discusses how sleep states coordinate long-lasting circuit change; oa:W2915405525, “Abnormal Sleep Spindles, Memory Consolidation, and Schizophrenia” (2019), linking sleep spindles to memory outcomes; and oa:W4385607530, “Gamma oscillations and episodic memory” (2023), focused on episodic memory-related oscillations. The set also includes broader circuit-rhythm papers on basal forebrain cholinergic projections and gamma operations.

The “Primed to Sleep” paper looks like the best conceptual parent because it addresses how waking plasticity and sleep rhythms interact across brain states. I’ll read it first to extract the key lineage and likely gap.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2914675220"}
```
[result]: oa:W2914675220 is a 2019 paper titled “Primed to Sleep: The Dynamics of Synaptic Plasticity Across Brain States.” Its abstract argues that wakefulness, NREM sleep, and REM sleep impose distinct plasticity regimes, and that durable memory may require coordinated transitions among these regimes rather than a single sleep mechanism. It emphasizes slow oscillations, spindles, hippocampal replay/ripples, and neuromodulatory state as interacting components, while noting that the field still lacks precise causal tests of how their timing relationships drive later memory.

Because cortical spindles are often nested in slow oscillations and linked to memory outcomes, I’ll read the schizophrenia spindle paper as a clinically grounded parent. This should help distinguish a general rhythm-enhancement idea from a more specific cross-rhythm coordination hypothesis.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2915405525"}
```
[result]: oa:W2915405525 is a 2019 paper titled “Abnormal Sleep Spindles, Memory Consolidation, and Schizophrenia.” It summarizes evidence that patients with schizophrenia and their unaffected relatives show reduced sleep spindle activity, and that spindle deficits correlate with impaired sleep-dependent declarative memory consolidation. The paper highlights thalamocortical spindle generation as a target for intervention, but it treats spindles largely as a measurable biomarker or enhancement target rather than as one element in a precisely timed hippocampal–cortical event sequence.

I’ll extract a structured genome from the broader plasticity paper to make the inherited elements and limitations explicit. This will help formulate a follow-up that mutates “coordinated sleep rhythms” into a concrete causal manipulation.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W2914675220"}
```
[result]: Genome extraction for oa:W2914675220 identifies a mechanism centered on state-dependent synaptic plasticity across wake, NREM, and REM, with NREM slow oscillations/spindles and hippocampal replay proposed as coordinating systems consolidation. The niche is memory stabilization after learning, especially how sleep transforms labile wake-encoded traces into durable circuit changes. The main limitation is that many claims are integrative and correlational: the paper calls for causal experiments that manipulate the relative timing of sleep events rather than only enhancing or suppressing single rhythms. The claim genome is that memory consolidation depends on orchestrated, state-specific plasticity windows.

Before proposing, I’ll run a novelty check on a possible mechanism: closed-loop manipulation that tests whether hippocampal ripples must arrive at a particular cortical slow-oscillation phase to benefit behavior. I want the idea to be close enough to the lineage but not merely “boost ripples” or “boost slow oscillations.”  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"Closed-loop sleep intervention that detects hippocampal sharp-wave ripples and causally shifts or gates cortical slow-oscillation upstates to test whether ripple-to-upstate timing, rather than event count alone, predicts memory consolidation.","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```
[result]: Novelty verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on targeted memory reactivation, slow-oscillation stimulation, spindle enhancement, and hippocampal ripple disruption, but most studies manipulate one rhythm or cue memories without parametrically controlling hippocampal–cortical phase alignment. The closest conceptual parents emphasize coordination among ripples, spindles, and slow oscillations, yet few directly compare enhanced synchrony, forced desynchrony, and sham conditions while linking event-level timing to behavioral memory changes.

I’ll now propose a follow-up that inherits the multi-rhythm consolidation framework but converts it into a causal timing experiment. The design focuses on whether behavior improves only when hippocampal sharp-wave ripples are coupled to cortical slow-oscillation upstates, with explicit desynchronization as the critical control.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop a closed-loop NREM sleep perturbation protocol in rodents performing an object–place or spatial sequence memory task: real-time hippocampal CA1 LFP detects sharp-wave ripples, while frontal/parietal cortical EEG or LFP estimates slow-oscillation phase; upon each ripple, the system either reinforces a cortical slow-oscillation upstate with brief transcranial/electrical or optogenetic cortical stimulation, delays stimulation to the downstate to force misalignment, suppresses the ripple with local hippocampal inhibition, or delivers sham stimulation. Event-level models then quantify ripple–slow-oscillation–spindle nesting and relate those timing motifs to next-day memory.","niche_genome":"Sleep-dependent systems consolidation of hippocampus-dependent episodic/spatial memories, specifically the unresolved causal role of hippocampal sharp-wave ripple timing relative to cortical slow oscillations and thalamocortical spindles during NREM sleep.","observation_genome":"The central prediction is that memory retention will improve most when detected hippocampal ripples are phase-aligned with cortical slow-oscillation upstates and spindle windows, even if the total number of ripples is unchanged; forced downstate alignment or ripple suppression should weaken retention and reduce coordinated cortical reactivation. Trial-by-trial behavioral benefit should be better predicted by the proportion of ripples occurring in the optimal cortical phase than by ripple rate, slow-oscillation power, or spindle density alone.","limitation_genome":"The approach requires stable chronic recordings and very low-latency phase estimation, and stimulation itself may perturb sleep architecture or induce nonspecific arousal. Rodent invasive timing results may not translate directly to humans, so a later human version would likely need noninvasive EEG-triggered auditory or electrical stimulation plus indirect hippocampal markers.","delta_genome":"Prior work links sleep rhythms to consolidation and often enhances or disrupts single rhythms such as spindles, slow oscillations, or ripples; this study makes the timing relation itself the manipulated variable. It adds a causal desynchronization arm, allowing the field to distinguish ‘more sleep oscillations are beneficial’ from ‘correctly sequenced hippocampal–cortical communication is beneficial.’","claim_genome":"Sleep-dependent memory consolidation is governed not simply by the abundance of hippocampal sharp-wave ripples or cortical slow oscillations, but by a causal phase code in which ripples must coincide with cortical upstate/spindle excitability windows to drive durable hippocampal-to-neocortical memory transfer."}}
```渠

full_prompt:
Research topic: Develop noninvasive brain-computer interfaces that decode imagined speech from EEG or MEG signals. The research targets robust neural decoding methods for communication restoration in paralysis.

Discipline: neuroscience

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I first need to anchor the idea in the recent noninvasive BCI and silent-speech literature, especially work connecting EEG/MEG decoding with communication restoration rather than generic BCI control.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "noninvasive EEG MEG imagined speech silent speech interface BCI paralysis", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant review-level papers. The strongest match is `oa:W3083438180` — “Silent Speech Interfaces for Speech Restoration: A Review” (2020), which surveys non-acoustic biosignals for restoring communication. Related methodological context appears in `oa:W4388240351` — “Deep Learning in EEG-Based BCIs: A Comprehensive Review of Transformer Models, Advantages, Challenges, and Applications” (2023), and broader BCI state-of-the-art reviews such as `oa:W4382725844` and `oa:W4394859234`.

The silent-speech review is the most directly aligned parent because imagined or attempted speech decoding is one pathway within silent speech interfaces for patients who cannot vocalize.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3083438180"}
```
[result]: `oa:W3083438180`, “Silent Speech Interfaces for Speech Restoration: A Review” (2020), reviews systems that infer speech from non-acoustic biosignals including articulatory motion, surface EMG, ultrasound, electromagnetic articulography, EEG, ECoG, and related neural or muscular signals. It emphasizes that invasive recordings often outperform noninvasive ones, while EEG/MEG remain attractive for safety and accessibility but suffer from low signal-to-noise ratio, inter-subject variability, limited vocabularies, and calibration burden. The review frames speech restoration as requiring not just classification of a few words, but scalable decoding of phonetic or linguistic content suitable for communication.

To understand the modern decoder side, I next read the EEG-transformer review because sequence modeling and cross-subject generalization are likely central to any follow-up method.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4388240351"}
```
[result]: `oa:W4388240351`, “Deep Learning in EEG-Based BCIs: A Comprehensive Review of Transformer Models, Advantages, Challenges, and Applications” (2023), surveys transformer architectures for EEG decoding across motor imagery, emotion recognition, seizure detection, and BCI control. It highlights that attention models can capture long-range temporal dependencies and channel interactions, but are data-hungry and vulnerable to distribution shifts across subjects, sessions, electrode montages, and noise conditions. The paper identifies self-supervised pretraining, domain adaptation, and interpretable attention as promising directions for robust EEG-based BCIs.

I extract a structured genome from the silent-speech review to identify what a useful descendant idea should inherit versus mutate.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3083438180"}
```
[result]: Genome extraction for `oa:W3083438180` identifies the core mechanism as silent speech restoration by decoding non-acoustic biosignals generated during speech planning or production. Its niche is communication restoration for people unable to produce audible speech, including paralysis and laryngectomy contexts. Key observations are that multiple biosignals contain speech-related information, but noninvasive neural modalities remain less accurate than invasive or articulatory sensors. Major limitations include small datasets, poor generalization, restricted vocabularies, and weak integration between neural decoding and linguistic priors.

Before proposing, I check whether the intended mechanism—noninvasive imagined-speech decoding using self-supervised neural sequence models constrained by articulatory-phonological priors—looks redundant with existing work.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A noninvasive EEG/MEG imagined-speech decoder that combines self-supervised source-space neural pretraining with articulatory-phonological latent constraints and uncertainty-aware language-model rescoring for communication restoration.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. The closest papers are broad silent-speech and EEG-transformer reviews rather than a direct match to the proposed mechanism. Existing work discusses EEG/MEG imagined speech, transformer decoders, and silent speech interfaces separately, but there is little evidence of a unified framework combining source-space pretraining, articulatory-phonological latent supervision, cross-subject adaptation, and clinical uncertainty-aware communication output.

The proposal should therefore inherit the silent-speech restoration niche and the transformer/self-supervised direction, but mutate them toward a clinically practical noninvasive EEG/MEG architecture that explicitly bridges neural signals to speech motor and phonological representations.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop APriL-BCI: an Articulatory-Phonological latent imagined-speech decoder for noninvasive EEG/MEG. The method would first pretrain a masked autoencoding transformer on large unlabeled resting, listening, overt-speech, and attempted-speech EEG/MEG in source space; then fine-tune it with a bottleneck latent space aligned to articulatory gestures, phonological features, and syllable-level timing. Decoding would combine neural evidence with a constrained language model and an uncertainty-aware spelling interface that abstains or asks for confirmation when neural confidence is low.", "niche_genome": "Communication restoration for people with severe paralysis or anarthria who cannot use audible speech and for whom invasive BCI surgery is unavailable, contraindicated, or undesired. The initial target would be closed-set and phrase-level imagined or attempted speech from high-density EEG and/or MEG, progressing toward open-vocabulary assisted communication.", "observation_genome": "Compared with standard EEG imagined-speech classifiers or generic transformer decoders, the articulatory-phonological bottleneck should improve cross-session and cross-subject generalization, reduce calibration time, and produce more interpretable errors, such as confusions among phonologically similar syllables rather than arbitrary word labels. The expected empirical result is higher phrase decoding accuracy and lower communication error rate under session shifts, especially when paired with uncertainty-aware language-model rescoring.", "limitation_genome": "The approach may still be constrained by the low spatial resolution and low signal-to-noise ratio of EEG, the cost and limited accessibility of MEG, and the scarcity of labeled attempted-speech data from paralyzed participants. ```

[result]: The search returns several recent gut–brain-axis papers. The most directly useful include oa:W3156926435, a 2021 review on the gut-brain-microbiome axis, autism, and zebrafish models; oa:W4410588838, a 2025 systematic review linking migraine and gut microbiome alterations; and oa:W4410007351, a 2025 review on nutraceutical modulation of the gut–brain axis. Other retrieved items are broader neuromicrobiology or longevity/exposome papers and are less directly tied to neuroinflammation mechanisms.

The zebrafish autism review looks useful because zebrafish allow microbial manipulation, imaging, and neurodevelopmental immune readouts. I’ll read it first to identify model-system strengths and gaps relevant to gut-derived immune signaling.

[result]: oa:W3156926435 is titled “The Gut-Brain-Microbiome Axis and Its Link to Autism: Emerging Insights and the Potential of Zebrafish Models” and was published in 2021. The paper reviews evidence that microbiome alterations may influence neurodevelopmental and behavioral phenotypes through metabolites, immune signaling, endocrine pathways, and neural communication routes. It highlights zebrafish as a tractable model for live imaging, microbial colonization experiments, genetic manipulation, and behavioral assays. However, it largely synthesizes potential mechanisms rather than providing an integrated causal pipeline connecting specific microbial metabolites, vagal activity, cytokine dynamics, and CNS immune-cell states.

I’ll extract a structured genome from this paper because it is a plausible parent: it frames the gut-brain-microbiome axis and identifies zebrafish as an experimental bridge, but leaves room for a more mechanistic neuroinflammation study.

[result]: The extracted genome emphasizes a review-based mechanism: gut microbiota may modulate neurodevelopmental phenotypes through metabolite production, immune activation, endocrine signaling, and gut-brain neural communication. Its niche is autism spectrum disorder and zebrafish modeling of gut-brain-microbiome interactions. The main observation is that zebrafish offer optical accessibility and microbiome manipulability for testing candidate gut-brain mechanisms. The key limitation is that causal links among specific microbes, metabolites, neural routes such as vagal pathways, cytokine profiles, and CNS immune states remain under-resolved.

To broaden beyond autism and capture human disease evidence, I’ll read the migraine systematic review. Migraine is relevant because it involves neuroimmune activation, peripheral inflammatory signals, and gut comorbidities, even if it may not directly map vagal circuits.

[result]: oa:W4410588838 is titled “Unravelling the gut-brain connection: a systematic review of migraine and the gut microbiome” and was published in 2025. The review summarizes evidence that migraine patients often show altered gut microbial composition and increased gastrointestinal comorbidity, with proposed links through inflammatory mediators, microbial metabolites, intestinal permeability, and bidirectional gut-brain signaling. It notes heterogeneity across cohorts, inconsistent microbial signatures, and limited causal experiments. The paper supports the need for mechanistic studies that jointly measure microbiome state, immune mediators, and neural signaling rather than relying only on associative microbiome profiling.

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the core idea—combining microbial metabolite perturbation, vagal pathway mapping, and cytokine-resolved neuroinflammation—appears redundant with existing work. The main risk is overbreadth, so the proposal should focus on a small metabolite class and a defined neuroinflammatory challenge.

The literature suggests a gap between associative microbiome signatures and causal gut-to-CNS immune mechanisms. [result]: The most relevant hit is `oa:W3083950194` (2020), *The Condensation of Electrons, Holes and Data: Imaging Non-Equilibrium Dynamics at the Microscopic, Mesoscopic, and Stat*, which focuses on driven quantum materials and transient phases. The other retrieved candidates are weakly related: `oa:W4318998224` is a 2023 applied-physics roadmap with broad coverage, and `oa:W2993861799` is primarily about ZnO nanowire biointerfaces despite mentioning ultrafast laser history. The search therefore points to `oa:W3083950194` as the main parent.

Its abstract frames quantum materials driven out of equilibrium as systems that can transiently host phases inaccessible in equilibrium, with emphasis on imaging and spectroscopy across microscopic to statistical scales. It highlights photoinduced phase transitions, carrier relaxation, collective modes, and the need for richer data analysis to connect ultrafast observables to evolving order. The likely limitation is that many pump-probe studies detect transient responses after the fact rather than actively steering the pathway into a selected metastable ordered state.

Its niche is broad: photoinduced phase transitions and emergent order in correlated electron systems. The limitation genome notes incomplete causal control: measured dynamics are often descriptive, with limited ability to stabilize a chosen transient superconducting or charge-density-wave state.  

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the key mechanism—closed-loop, mode-selective pump shaping to steer transient order in layered correlated materials—looks redundant with obvious prior work or remains a healthy extension. topic: [physics] Develop precision tests of general relativity with next-generation pulsar timing arrays. Emphasize modeling gravitational-wave backgrounds from supermassive bla
full_prompt:
Research topic: Develop precision tests of general relativity with next-generation pulsar timing arrays. Emphasize modeling gravitational-wave backgrounds from supermassive black hole binaries and separating them from stochastic noise sources.

Discipline: physics

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first locate relevant recent work on low-frequency gravitational-wave tests of gravity, because pulsar timing arrays probe the nanohertz end of the same spectrum and share many theory-systematics issues with LISA-style tests.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "low frequency gravitational waves tests general relativity pulsar timing arrays stochastic background", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant low-frequency or precision-gravity papers. Top candidates include oa:W7155672532 / oa:W2971629893, “Testing alternative theories of gravity using low frequency gravitational waves” (2019), which focuses on non-GR signatures in low-frequency GW detectors; oa:W2948201147, “Testing modified gravity at cosmological distances with LISA standard sirens” (2019), which treats modified propagation over cosmological baselines; and oa:W4368754589, “Testing General Relativity with Black Hole Quasi-Normal Modes” (2023), a complementary strong-field test using ringdowns. The search set is not PTA-specific, but it identifies the relevant conceptual lineage: modified propagation, alternative polarizations, and precision separation of signal from noise.

The most directly relevant candidate is the low-frequency gravitational-wave paper, since PTA backgrounds from supermassive black hole binaries are also low-frequency stochastic signals. I will read it to extract which non-GR effects are usually targeted and where PTA-specific modeling may add something new.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W7155672532"}
```
[result]: oa:W7155672532, “Testing alternative theories of gravity using low frequency gravitational waves” (2019), reviews how low-frequency GW observatories can test deviations from GR, including modified dispersion, extra polarizations, dipole radiation, and changes to binary inspiral phasing. The paper emphasizes that low-frequency bands are especially powerful because long baselines and long observation times accumulate propagation and phase effects. Its limitation for the present topic is that it is framed mainly around resolvable or individually modeled sources, whereas PTA evidence for a nanohertz background requires population-level inference and careful disentangling from common red processes, clock errors, ephemeris errors, and pulsar-intrinsic noise.

I will also read the LISA standard-siren paper because it treats cosmological-distance propagation effects, which could be inherited in a PTA background model as frequency- and redshift-dependent distortions of the SMBHB signal.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2948201147"}
```
[result]: oa:W2948201147, “Testing modified gravity at cosmological distances with LISA standard sirens” (2019), develops tests of modified gravity using the difference between electromagnetic and gravitational-wave luminosity distances and the impact of non-GR propagation over cosmological distances. The paper’s core idea is that even if compact binaries emit nearly as in GR, propagation through an evolving universe can imprint measurable deviations. For PTAs, the analogous opportunity is not a single standard siren but the integrated stochastic background from a redshift-distributed SMBHB population, whose amplitude, spectral slope, and spatial correlations may encode both astrophysical evolution and gravity-sector deviations.

Before proposing, I will check whether the specific idea—jointly fitting a PTA SMBHB background, non-GR correlation/polarization structure, and stochastic noise contaminants—is merely duplicative or plausibly novel.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A hierarchical Bayesian pulsar timing array analysis that jointly infers the supermassive black hole binary population, frequency-dependent non-GR propagation or polarization deviations, and competing stochastic noise processes using spatial-correlation templates beyond Hellings-Downs.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Nearby work includes oa:W7155672532 / oa:W2971629893 on alternative-gravity signatures in low-frequency GW observations and oa:W2948201147 on modified propagation using LISA standard sirens, but those do not focus on stochastic PTA background inference with simultaneous SMBHB-population and noise-systematics modeling. The proposed mechanism is connected to the prior literature but appears differentiated by making the gravity test conditional on realistic PTA nuisance processes: intrinsic pulsar red noise, clock monopoles, ephemeris dipoles, solar-wind terms, and astrophysical SMBHB population uncertainty.

The proposal should therefore hybridize low-frequency modified-gravity observables with PTA-specific stochastic-background inference. I will formulate it as a concrete next-generation PTA analysis framework rather than as a generic GR test.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical Bayesian PTA inference framework that models the nanohertz gravitational-wave background as a redshift- and mass-distributed supermassive black hole binary population while simultaneously fitting non-GR deviations in propagation and polarization. The model would include the standard Hellings-Downs tensor correlation plus parameterized scalar, vector, monopole, dipole, and frequency-dependent correlation components; a modified-dispersion or gravitational-friction parameter would distort the background spectrum relative to the astrophysical SMBHB prediction. The likelihood would jointly marginalize over pulsar-intrinsic red noise, white-noise calibration errors, clock errors, solar-wind noise, and Solar-System-ephemeris uncertainties using trans-dimensional or reversible-jump sampling to avoid overclaiming non-GR structure.", "niche_genome": "Precision tests of general relativity with next-generation pulsar timing arrays such as NANOGrav, EPTA, PPTA, IPTA, and future SKA-era timing data, specifically in the regime where the observed signal is a stochastic nanohertz background from unresolved supermassive black hole binaries rather than individually resolved events.", "observation_genome": "If GR is correct and the background is dominated by circular SMBHB inspirals, the analysis should recover a Hellings-Downs spatial correlation and an approximately f^-2/3 characteristic-strain spectrum after marginalizing over environmental hardening and population uncertainty. If apparent deviations are caused by clock, ephemeris, or pulsar red-noise systematics, the model should allocate power to monopole, dipole, or uncorrelated red-noise components rather than to non-GR polarizations. A genuine beyond-GR effect would appear as a stable, data-set-independent residual correlation pattern or frequency-dependent spectral distortion that survives astrophysical-population and noise marginalization.", "limitation_genome": "The main limitation is degeneracy: SMBHB environmental coupling, eccentricity, cosmic variance from a finite number of loud binaries, and non-GR propagation can all bend the stochastic-background spectrum in similar ways. The method also requires long baselines, many well-timed pulsars, and robust ephemeris modeling; early PTA data may yield only upper limits on non-GR parameters rather than decisive tests.", "delta_genome": "Prior low-frequency gravity-test work such as oa:W7155672532 emphasizes alternative polarizations, modified dispersion, and phase effects, while oa:W2948201147 develops cosmological-distance modified-propagation tests for LISA standard sirens. This proposal shifts those ideas into the PTA stochastic-background setting and adds a crucial missing layer: simultaneous hierarchical inference of the SMBHB population and all major stochastic noise contaminants so that deviations from GR are not confused with astrophysical or timing-systematic effects.", "claim_genome": "A next-generation PTA can become a precision test of GR not merely by detecting a Hellings-Downs-correlated background, but by jointly fitting the astrophysical SMBHB population, non-GR spatial/spectral signatures, and realistic stochastic noise processes; this joint model will produce more reliable constraints on alternative polarizations and modified propagation than analyses that treat the background spectrum or noise budget as fixed."}}
```

topic: [physics] Model dark matter self-interactions through astrophysical observations of dwarf galaxies, galaxy clusters, and strong lensing systems. Connect particle-scale in
full_prompt:
Research topic: Model dark matter self-interactions through astrophysical observations of dwarf galaxies, galaxy clusters, and strong lensing systems. Connect particle-scale interaction cross sections to observable structure formation signatures.

Discipline: physics

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first identify which available 2018–2025 papers are closest to observational dark-matter constraints in small systems, then use the most relevant one as a methodological parent. The query targets dwarf/satellite-galaxy constraints because those are the lowest-mass anchors for connecting particle physics to structure formation.
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "dark matter properties dwarf satellite galaxy constraints self interactions", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns oa:W3046615382, “Constraints on Dark Matter Properties from Observations of Milky Way Satellite Galaxies” (2021), as the closest match: it uses Milky Way satellite populations to constrain fundamental dark-matter physics. It also returns broader-context papers including oa:W3197776693, “Wave Dark Matter” (2021), and less directly relevant reviews on cosmological tests of gravity and compact objects. The satellite-galaxy paper is the best parent for a structure-formation-to-particle-physics inference pipeline.

I’ll read the satellite-galaxy constraints paper because it likely contains the observational-inference machinery needed to map particle models into dwarf-scale abundance and spatial signatures. This will help identify what is missing for self-interacting dark matter across multiple astrophysical regimes.
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3046615382"}
```
[result]: “Constraints on Dark Matter Properties from Observations of Milky Way Satellite Galaxies” studies Milky Way satellites as probes of dark-matter microphysics, incorporating spatial inhomogeneities in survey coverage and satellite detectability. The paper uses satellite counts, luminosity functions, and radial distributions to constrain models that alter small-scale structure formation, emphasizing careful forward modeling of selection effects. Its limitation for the present topic is that the observational lever arm is mostly dwarf-scale and does not by itself break degeneracies between suppressed primordial power, tidal disruption, baryonic effects, and dark-sector interactions.

To reason more explicitly about inheritance, I’ll extract the structured genome of the satellite paper. The goal is to preserve its careful selection-function treatment while mutating the model space toward velocity-dependent self-interactions and adding cluster/lensing observables.
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3046615382"}
```
[result]: Genome extraction identifies the mechanism as a forward model linking dark-matter microphysics to Milky Way satellite observables through simulated subhalo populations and survey selection functions. The niche is small-scale structure constraints from resolved dwarf satellites. The core observation is that satellite abundance and spatial distributions can exclude regions of dark-matter parameter space when observational incompleteness is modeled. The main limitation is degeneracy: dwarf satellites alone cannot uniquely distinguish multiple dark-matter mechanisms or fully constrain velocity-dependent interactions relevant at cluster scales.

I’ll check whether a unified proposal combining dwarf satellites, cluster-scale self-interaction signatures, and strong-lensing substructure is already too close to existing work. The novelty check focuses on the proposed mechanism rather than only the observational domain.
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "hierarchical simulation-based inference of velocity-dependent dark matter self-interaction cross sections using joint dwarf-satellite counts, galaxy-cluster offsets and shapes, and strong-lensing substructure anomalies", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. The nearest retrieved themes include satellite-galaxy constraints on dark-matter properties, as in oa:W3046615382, and alternative small-scale dark-matter phenomenology such as oa:W3197776693, but the search does not find an exact joint emulator connecting velocity-dependent self-interaction cross sections simultaneously to dwarf, cluster, and strong-lensing likelihoods. The main novelty risk is overlap with separate SIDM cluster or lensing analyses, so the proposal should emphasize a calibrated cross-scale likelihood and explicit degeneracy control.

I’ll now propose a follow-up that inherits the satellite paper’s forward-modeling rigor, mutates the particle model to velocity-dependent self-interacting dark matter, and hybridizes three complementary observational regimes. The central idea is to infer a particle-scale cross-section curve, not just fit phenomenological halo cores or subhalo abundances separately.
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Build a hierarchical simulation-based inference framework for velocity-dependent self-interacting dark matter, parameterizing the transfer cross section as sigma_T/m(v) with a small set of mediator-inspired parameters, and training emulators on matched suites of cosmological zoom simulations and controlled halo-merger simulations to predict three observable classes: Milky Way dwarf-satellite abundance/radial distributions/internal density cores, galaxy-cluster halo shapes/BCG-DM offsets/merger-induced centroid separations, and strong-lensing subhalo perturbations/flux-ratio anomalies/time-delay residuals.", "niche_genome": "Astrophysical inference of dark-matter self-interaction physics across dwarf galaxies, galaxy clusters, and strong gravitational lenses, especially the problem of connecting particle-scale cross sections at dwarf-scale velocities (~10-50 km/s), Milky-Way subhalo velocities (~100-200 km/s), and cluster velocities (~1000-3000 km/s) to observable structure-formation signatures.", "observation_genome": "If dark matter has a Yukawa-like velocity-dependent self-interaction, the joint analysis should find a correlated pattern: dwarf-scale cores or altered satellite survival at low velocities, suppressed or bounded cluster ellipticity/offset signatures at high velocities, and a lensing substructure population whose central-density perturbations differ from collisionless-CDM predictions even when the total subhalo mass function is similar. The expected empirical result is a posterior on sigma_T/m(v) that is narrower and less degenerate than any dwarf-only, cluster-only, or lensing-only constraint.", "limitation_genome": "The proposal depends on accurate baryonic marginalization, robust lens-model systematics, and simulation coverage broad enough to emulate rare cluster mergers and low-mass subhalos; otherwise the inferred self-interaction signal could be confused with stellar feedback, tidal stripping, line-of-sight lensing structure, or cluster dynamical state. It also requires a pragmatic low-dimensional cross-section parameterization, so highly nonstandard dark-sector models may not be faithfully captured.", "delta_genome": "Relative to satellite-galaxy-only dark-matter constraints such as oa:W3046615382, this extends the forward-modeling and selection-function philosophy from one mass/velocity regime to a cross-scale, velocity-resolved SIDM likelihood. The key change is to treat dwarfs, clusters, and strong lenses as complementary velocity probes of the same particle interaction law, rather than as separate phenomenological tests of halo cores, offsets, or substructure.", "claim_genome": "A joint dwarf-cluster-lensing inference pipeline can convert heterogeneous astrophysical observations into a direct constraint on the velocity dependence of the dark-matter self-interaction transfer cross section, breaking degeneracies that limit single-regime studies and enabling a falsifiable particle-scale SIDM hypothesis test with near-term survey and lens samples."}}
``` charme
