topic: [earth_science] Assessing the influence of atmospheric rivers on compound flooding by coupling high-resolution precipitation forecasts with coastal storm surge and river discha
full_prompt:
Research topic: Assessing the influence of atmospheric rivers on compound flooding by coupling high-resolution precipitation forecasts with coastal storm surge and river discharge models.

Discipline: earth_science

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first locate recent work at the intersection of atmospheric rivers, coastal surge, river discharge, and compound flood modeling so the proposal is grounded in the existing modeling lineage. The search is intentionally broad because relevant studies may appear under “compound flooding,” “coastal backwater,” or “AR-driven precipitation” terminology.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "atmospheric rivers compound flooding storm surge river discharge coastal", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search returns several relevant candidates: oa:W4388936823, “Atmospheric rivers drive compound flooding in the coastal western United States” (2023); oa:W4286423175, “Compound flooding in coastal areas: a review of current knowledge and future research needs” (2022); oa:W4310917590, “Modeling compound flood hazards from river discharge and storm surge in coastal watersheds” (2022); oa:W4200310566, “Flood hazard assessment under nonstationary precipitation, river flow and storm surge dependence” (2021); and oa:W4401912987, “High-resolution precipitation forecasts for hydrologic prediction of extreme rainfall events associated with atmospheric rivers” (2024). The top hit directly links ARs to compound flooding, while the others cover model coupling and dependence structures.  

The first paper appears to be the closest conceptual parent because it explicitly connects atmospheric rivers with compound coastal flooding. I’ll read it to understand what variables and mechanisms it used, and where it left room for forecast-oriented coupled modeling.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4388936823"}
```  
[result]: “Atmospheric rivers drive compound flooding in the coastal western United States” analyzes events where AR landfall coincides with elevated coastal water levels and high river discharge. The paper uses reanalysis-era AR catalogs, tide/surge observations, and streamflow records to show that ARs can synchronize rainfall-runoff peaks with coastal boundary anomalies, especially in short, steep coastal watersheds. Its main limitation is that the analysis is largely diagnostic/hindcast and does not implement an operational high-resolution precipitation-to-surge-to-discharge forecast chain.  

To cover the modeling side, I’ll read a paper focused on compound flood model coupling rather than just event attribution. This should clarify how hydrologic and hydrodynamic components are usually linked, and what gaps remain for ensemble prediction.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4310917590"}
```  
[result]: “Modeling compound flood hazards from river discharge and storm surge in coastal watersheds” presents a coupled river-coastal hydrodynamic framework using watershed runoff or gauge-derived discharge as upstream forcing and coastal surge/tide as downstream boundary conditions. It shows that neglecting either boundary can underestimate water levels in tidal rivers and estuaries during compound events. The study is computationally expensive for large ensembles and typically relies on observed or design-event forcing rather than dynamically updated high-resolution precipitation forecasts.  

I’ll extract a structured genome from the AR-compound-flood paper to make the inheritance path explicit. This will help ensure the final proposal mutates a genuine limitation rather than merely restating prior diagnostic work.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4388936823"}
```  
[result]: Genome extracted. mechanism_genome: “Combine AR catalog diagnostics with streamflow and coastal water-level records to identify temporal co-occurrence of AR landfall, runoff extremes, and surge/tide anomalies.” niche_genome: “Coastal western U.S. basins vulnerable to short-lag rainfall-runoff and estuarine water-level amplification.” observation_genome: “AR-associated events disproportionately contribute to joint high-flow/high-water-level days.” limitation_genome: “Retrospective, station-based attribution; limited explicit coupling between precipitation forecasts, hydrologic routing, and coastal hydrodynamics.” delta_genome: “Moves from univariate coastal or fluvial flood assessment toward AR-conditioned compound-event diagnosis.” claim_genome: “Atmospheric rivers are a synoptic-scale organizer of compound coastal flooding risk.”  

Before proposing, I’ll run a novelty check around a forecast-chain mechanism that couples high-resolution AR precipitation ensembles with river and storm-surge models using an emulator for rapid compound-flood probabilities. This tests whether the idea is redundant with recent operational or research systems.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "An ensemble forecast chain that downscales atmospheric-river precipitation forecasts into hydrologic routing and coastal storm-surge models, with a fast emulator to produce estuary-scale compound flood exceedance probabilities.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```  
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on AR precipitation forecasting, compound flood hindcasting, and coupled surge-river simulations, but few papers integrate all three into a probabilistic, event-updating forecast workflow. The most similar candidates either use historical co-occurrence statistics, design storms, or offline hydrodynamic simulations rather than real-time AR-sensitive precipitation ensembles driving two-way coastal-fluvial boundary interactions.  

I now have enough lineage: prior work shows ARs organize compound risk, while modeling studies show coupled boundaries matter but are costly and often hindcast/design-event based. I’ll propose a follow-up that mutates these into an operational, uncertainty-aware forecast framework.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop an AR-conditioned ensemble compound-flood forecast system that ingests convection-permitting precipitation forecasts and IVT-based AR diagnostics, routes rainfall through a distributed hydrologic model, couples the resulting discharge to a coastal storm-surge/estuarine hydrodynamic model, and uses an active-learning surrogate emulator to generate rapid probabilistic water-level exceedance maps at tidal-river confluences.", "niche_genome": "Short-response coastal watersheds and estuaries exposed to landfalling atmospheric rivers, initially tested on U.S. West Coast basins where intense rainfall, antecedent soil moisture, tides, surge, and river discharge can jointly control flood stage.", "observation_genome": "The system should show that AR orientation, landfall latitude, precipitation-band persistence, and antecedent wetness identify a subset of forecast members with much higher compound-flood exceedance probability than would be inferred from surge-only, discharge-only, or historical co-occurrence methods.", "limitation_genome": "Skill will depend on precipitation-placement errors in high-resolution forecasts, bathymetric and channel-roughness uncertainty, and the transferability of the surrogate emulator outside the range of simulated AR and surge conditions; observations in small ungauged tidal tributaries may be sparse for validation.", "delta_genome": "This shifts prior AR-compound-flood work from retrospective co-occurrence attribution to an event-updating forecast architecture, and shifts prior coupled surge-river modeling from computationally expensive hindcast/design simulations to ensemble probabilistic prediction accelerated by targeted hydrodynamic training runs.", "claim_genome": "A dynamically coupled, AR-aware ensemble forecast chain with an adaptive surrogate can provide earlier and more reliable estuary-scale compound-flood warnings than independent fluvial or coastal models, especially for rapidly responding basins during landfalling atmospheric rivers."}}
```

Two closely related 2024 candidates, `oa:W7113244526` and `oa:W7139413700`, both titled “The transport behaviour of the Stromboli’s volcanic plume inferred from combined scanning-DOAS and UV Camera observation,” focus specifically on SO₂ plume transport and gas-flux monitoring.

The 2022 paper appears to provide the broad monitoring/forecasting lineage, so I will read it first to understand how current eruption forecasting integrates multiple volcanic observables and where it may fall short.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4226461778"}

[result]: `oa:W4226461778`, “Forecasting volcano eruption activity” (2022), describes volcanic eruption forecasting as a synthesis problem across seismic activity, gas emissions, deformation, thermal anomalies, and surface changes. The abstract emphasizes that many signals can be extracted from global observation datasets, but that volcanoes differ strongly in baseline behavior, data availability, and eruption style. It highlights the promise of automated monitoring and data-driven forecasting while noting challenges in false alarms, missing data, and transferring models between volcanoes.

Gas emissions are central to the prompt, and the Stromboli paper may provide a more instrument-specific view of SO₂ monitoring uncertainty. I will read the 2024 plume-transport paper to capture limitations in real-time gas interpretation.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W7113244526"}
```

[result]: `oa:W7113244526`, “The transport behaviour of the Stromboli’s volcanic plume inferred from combined scanning-DOAS and UV Camera observation” (2024), examines SO₂ plume transport using combined scanning-DOAS and UV camera observations. The paper treats SO₂ flux as a proxy for magma supply and shallow conduit processes, but stresses that plume transport, wind field, viewing geometry, and retrieval assumptions can distort inferred flux. Its contribution is a combined observing strategy that improves constraints on plume dynamics, making gas data more physically interpretable for volcano monitoring.

To reason about inheritance and gaps, I will extract a structured genome from the broader eruption-forecasting paper. This should clarify what parts of the prior approach can be retained and where a follow-up can mutate the mechanism.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W4226461778"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome for `oa:W4226461778`: mechanism — aggregate multi-source volcano observations such as seismicity, gas, deformation, thermal anomalies, and surface change for eruption forecasting; niche — broad volcano monitoring and eruption activity prediction across diverse volcanic systems; observation — multi-observable monitoring can improve recognition of unrest relative to isolated signals; limitation — heterogeneous data cadence, missing streams, volcano-specific baselines, and false alarms limit robust real-time forecasting; delta — moves beyond single-parameter monitoring toward globally informed automated forecasting; claim — systematic fusion of observable precursors can improve eruption activity forecasts.

The gap seems to be not simply “use multiple data streams,” but how to fuse asynchronous, uncertain, physically distorted signals into a real-time warning state. I will check novelty around a physics-informed, streaming multimodal fusion model for InSAR, gas, and tremor.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A physics-informed streaming Bayesian state-space model that fuses asynchronous InSAR deformation, SO2 gas-flux observations, and seismic tremor to estimate latent magma pressurization and issue early warning probabilities.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on multi-parameter volcano forecasting and on improved gas retrievals, including `oa:W4226461778` and `oa:W7113244526`, but no close match is found for a unified real-time latent-state model explicitly coupling InSAR deformation, plume-corrected SO₂ flux, and tremor. The mechanism appears connected to prior work yet distinct in its emphasis on asynchronous fusion, physical latent variables, uncertainty propagation, and operational early-warning probabilities.

Given the prior work, the follow-up should inherit multi-observable eruption forecasting, incorporate plume-aware gas interpretation, and add a concrete real-time fusion mechanism that handles sparse InSAR and uncertain gas/seismic streams. I will now propose the final research genome.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a physics-informed streaming Bayesian state-space model, implemented as a real-time data-assimilation system, that fuses intermittent InSAR line-of-sight deformation, plume-transport-corrected SO2 flux from DOAS/UV camera observations, and continuous seismic tremor features. The model estimates latent variables such as shallow magma overpressure, conduit permeability, and degassing efficiency, while explicitly propagating uncertainty from satellite revisit gaps, atmospheric InSAR noise, wind-driven plume transport, and seismic background variability.", "niche_genome": "Early warning of volcanic unrest at persistently monitored open-vent and intermittently active volcanoes where at least partial InSAR, gas-emission, and seismic-tremor streams are available, with initial testing on Stromboli-like systems and later transfer to data-sparser volcanoes.", "observation_genome": "The expected empirical result is that latent-state fusion will detect transitions into elevated unrest earlier and with fewer false alarms than thresholding any single stream or using feature-level black-box fusion. In retrospective hindcasts, the model should show that deformation-only warnings lag during sparse satellite coverage, gas-only warnings are confused by plume-transport effects, and tremor-only warnings overreact to shallow conduit noise, whereas their physically constrained combination yields more stable warning probabilities.", "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on having enough historical unrest episodes to calibrate priors and validate warning thresholds, and its latent variables may be non-identifiable at volcanoes with sparse gas or deformation coverage. It also cannot make InSAR truly continuous; instead, it must quantify how forecast confidence degrades between satellite acquisitions.", "delta_genome": "Compared with prior multi-observable eruption forecasting, this proposal changes the fusion level from aggregating observed anomalies to continuously estimating physically meaningful hidden volcanic states. Compared with gas-plume studies, it uses plume-corrected SO2 not as a standalone diagnostic but as one uncertain observation operator within a coupled deformation-gas-seismic warning model.", "claim_genome": "A physically constrained, uncertainty-aware fusion model of InSAR deformation, SO2 degassing, and seismic tremor will provide earlier and more reliable warning of volcanic unrest than current single-parameter thresholds or loosely coupled multi-parameter dashboards."}}
```

topic: [earth_science] Tracing dust provenance and nutrient deposition in remote ocean basins through isotopic fingerprinting, aerosol transport models, and satellite aerosol optical
full_prompt:
Research topic: Tracing dust provenance and nutrient deposition in remote ocean basins through isotopic fingerprinting, aerosol transport models, and satellite aerosol optical depth data.

Discipline: earth_science

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first map the recent literature in the 2018–2025 window, focusing on papers that combine isotopic source attribution with aerosol transport or remote-sensing constraints for ocean-relevant dust deposition. The provided candidates include North African source-fingerprinting, trans-Atlantic transport, Arctic trace-element transport, and Southern Ocean aerosol observations, so I will begin with a targeted search.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "dust provenance isotopes aerosol optical depth ocean nutrient deposition", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant OpenAlex records: oa:W4295394233 on Saharan/Sahelian dust source characterization using geochemical and radiogenic isotope signatures; oa:W4281641137 on coupled Sr–Nd–Hf isotopes and elemental analysis for North African dust contributions; oa:W4229457699 on interannual source variability of North African dust transported to the Amazon; oa:W4388308065 on aerosol optical properties over the South Atlantic and Southern Ocean; and oa:W3015388872 on trace-element transport to the central Arctic Ocean via the Transpolar Drift. The strongest methodological parents appear to be the isotope-source papers, while the Southern Ocean paper is useful for the remote-ocean aerosol niche.

Next I will read the 2022 Saharan/Sahelian source-characterization study because it likely defines the isotope endmember problem: dust provenance depends on how well source regions are fingerprinted before atmospheric transport is modeled.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4295394233"}
```
[result]: The paper “Characterization of Saharan and Sahelian dust sources based on geochemical and radiogenic isotope signatures” develops geochemical and radiogenic isotope fingerprints for major North African dust source regions. Its abstract emphasizes that accurate provenance requires robust source-region characterization, since mineral dust affects climate and biogeochemical cycles after long-range transport. The study uses multi-element and isotope signatures to discriminate Saharan versus Sahelian sources, providing endmembers that can be used in receptor mixing or transport attribution. A key limitation is that source fingerprints alone do not fully resolve deposition timing, atmospheric processing, or nutrient delivery at remote marine receptors.

I will then read the Amazon transport paper because it addresses interannual source variability during long-range transport, which is directly analogous to remote ocean basins where source contributions can shift seasonally or by climate mode.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4229457699"}
```
[result]: The paper “Interannual Variability in the Source Location of North African Dust Transported to the Amazon” examines which African regions dominate dust transported to South America during winter and spring. It evaluates competing hypotheses, including western North African versus central North African/Bodélé source dominance, and highlights that source attribution varies substantially from year to year. The work connects transport pathways with source-region uncertainty, suggesting that static climatological assumptions can misidentify the dust source reaching distant receptors. Its limitation for ocean biogeochemistry is that it focuses more on source location than on event-resolved nutrient solubility and deposition flux to remote waters.

To ground the proposal in the remote-ocean niche, I will read the South Atlantic/Southern Ocean aerosol optical properties paper. This should clarify what satellite or ship-based optical observations can and cannot tell us about dust presence over sparse marine regions.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4388308065"}
```
[result]: The paper “Aerosol optical properties over the South Atlantic and southern ocean during the 2010–2012 summer seasons...” reports aerosol optical properties in marine regions influenced indirectly by Patagonian semi-desert dust. It frames aerosols as climatically and biogeochemically important over the global oceans and notes debate about the magnitude and detectability of Patagonian dust influence. The study shows the value of optical measurements for identifying aerosol regimes but also the ambiguity of inferring provenance from optical properties alone. This creates a gap for combining optical-depth constraints with isotopic fingerprints and atmospheric transport ensembles.

Before proposing, I will check whether the specific mechanism I have in mind—Bayesian fusion of isotope fingerprints, satellite AOD, and ensemble transport to infer event-scale provenance and nutrient deposition—is already redundant or whether it occupies a healthy novelty space.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A Bayesian data-assimilation framework that combines event-resolved aerosol isotope fingerprints, satellite aerosol optical depth, and ensemble back-trajectory or chemical transport models to infer dust provenance and soluble nutrient deposition to remote ocean basins.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on isotope-based source characterization, dust transport to the Amazon, and optical aerosol observations over the South Atlantic/Southern Ocean, but the combined event-scale assimilation of isotope endmembers, AOD, transport ensembles, and soluble nutrient measurements for remote ocean deposition is not well represented. The closest papers tend to solve only one component: source fingerprints, pathway attribution, or aerosol optical characterization. The proposed approach is therefore connected to the literature but not redundant.

Building on the isotope endmember studies and the remote-ocean aerosol observations, I will propose a follow-up that turns provenance tracing into a quantitative, event-resolved nutrient deposition product. The key delta is to assimilate sparse isotope samples into satellite- and transport-constrained dust fields rather than treating isotope attribution, AOD, and deposition modeling as separate analyses.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop an event-resolved Bayesian dust provenance and nutrient-deposition inversion that assimilates ship/island aerosol Sr–Nd–Hf–Pb isotope ratios, elemental Fe/P/Si/trace-metal concentrations, laboratory leachable nutrient fractions, satellite AOD and aerosol typing from MODIS/VIIRS/CALIOP, and FLEXPART or GEOS-Chem ensemble back-trajectories. The model will estimate posterior source fractions from Patagonia, Australia, southern Africa, and North African/Sahelian analog endmember libraries, while propagating uncertainty from source isotope overlap, wet/dry deposition, particle size, and aerosol optical retrieval errors.", "niche_genome": "Remote, undersampled ocean basins where dust supplies limiting nutrients but provenance is uncertain, with an initial field test in the South Atlantic–Southern Ocean sector downstream of Patagonia and southern Africa, then extension to oligotrophic gyres and polar frontal zones.", "observation_genome": "The study should show that isotope-constrained AOD/transport assimilation reduces source-fraction uncertainty relative to AOD-only or trajectory-only attribution, identifies episodic high-soluble-Fe deposition events missed by monthly climatologies, and reveals that optically modest dust plumes can dominate bioavailable nutrient pulses when source mineralogy and atmospheric processing favor solubility.", "limitation_genome": "The approach will remain limited by sparse aerosol collection in stormy remote oceans, incomplete isotope endmember coverage for some Southern Hemisphere sources, possible alteration of isotope-bearing phases during atmospheric transport, and satellite AOD retrieval biases under clouds, high wind sea salt, or mixed aerosol scenes.", "delta_genome": "Prior work characterized dust source isotope fingerprints, documented interannual shifts in long-range dust source regions, and measured aerosol optical properties over remote oceans; this project fuses those strands into a single probabilistic inversion that converts provenance information into quantitative, event-scale soluble nutrient deposition estimates with uncertainty bounds.", "claim_genome": "Remote-ocean nutrient deposition can be traced more accurately by jointly constraining dust provenance, plume transport, and aerosol optical loading than by any single evidence stream, and doing so will reveal that a small number of source-specific dust events exert disproportionate control on seasonal micronutrient supply in high-nutrient, low-chlorophyll and oligotrophic ocean regions."}}
```כ

topic: [earth_science] Forecasting coastal erosion hotspots under sea-level rise by integrating shoreline change records, wave climate projections, and sediment budget analyses.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Forecasting coastal erosion hotspots under sea-level rise by integrating shoreline change records, wave climate projections, and sediment budget analyses.

Discipline: earth_science

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first identify the most relevant recent coastal sea-level and observation literature in the provided OpenAlex candidate set, focusing on works that can anchor erosion-hotspot forecasting under sea-level rise.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "coastal erosion sea level rise wave climate sediment budget hotspots", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returns several relevant coastal-change and sea-level papers, including IPCC-style synthesis chapters and observational reviews. Key candidates are oa:W4210654828, “Sea Level Rise and Implications for Low-Lying Islands, Coasts and Communities” (2022), oa:W2944017778, “Forcing Factors Affecting Sea Level Changes at the Coast” (2019), and oa:W4317673900, “Observation of the Coastal Areas, Estuaries and Deltas from Space” (2023). These provide complementary foundations: sea-level risk framing, coastal forcing mechanisms, and remote observation capabilities.

The IPCC sea-level chapter is likely the strongest parent work for understanding risk pathways, regional sea-level projections, and coastal exposure under future scenarios.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4210654828"}
```
[result]: This 2022 chapter synthesizes observed and projected global, regional, and extreme sea-level change and its implications for low-lying islands, coasts, cities, and settlements. It emphasizes that impacts depend not only on mean sea-level rise but also on storm surges, waves, tides, vertical land motion, geomorphology, exposure, and adaptation choices. The work is broad and authoritative but generally operates at assessment scale rather than providing a site-specific, process-linked erosion-hotspot forecasting framework.

To connect sea-level rise with shoreline response, I need a paper focused on coastal forcing mechanisms and how local sea level differs from open-ocean variability.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2944017778"}
```
[result]: “Forcing Factors Affecting Sea Level Changes at the Coast” reviews how coastal sea level is shaped by tides, storm surges, waves, atmospheric pressure, winds, ocean circulation, freshwater input, steric effects, vertical land motion, and geomorphic setting. It highlights that coastal impacts emerge from compound forcing rather than from mean sea-level trends alone. Its limitation for erosion forecasting is that it reviews drivers of water-level variability but does not integrate shoreline-change histories, sediment budgets, and future wave-climate projections into a predictive hotspot model.

A modern erosion-hotspot method also needs scalable monitoring, so I’ll read the space-observation paper to ground the proposal in practical data streams.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4317673900"}
```
[result]: This 2023 paper reviews satellite observation of coastal areas, estuaries, and deltas, emphasizing the complexity of hydrodynamic, geomorphic, ecological, and human settings. It discusses how remote sensing can support shoreline mapping, wetland monitoring, bathymetric inference, turbidity/sediment-plume analysis, and change detection across broad coastal regions. The paper suggests strong potential for repeated, large-scale coastal monitoring, but it does not by itself solve the problem of attributing erosion hotspots to sediment deficits, altered wave climate, and sea-level-rise-driven accommodation change.

Before proposing, I’ll check whether the intended mechanism—an integrated probabilistic hotspot forecaster combining shoreline records, projected waves, and sediment-budget closure—is already redundant with nearby work.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "A probabilistic coastal erosion hotspot forecasting framework that assimilates historical shoreline change, projected wave climate, relative sea-level rise, and sediment-budget imbalance into a spatially explicit early-warning index.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```
[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on sea-level-risk assessment, shoreline-change detection, satellite coastal monitoring, and coastal forcing reviews, but the combined mechanism appears underdeveloped as a unified forecasting framework. The most distinctive gap is explicit sediment-budget closure coupled with future wave-climate ensembles and observed shoreline-change trajectories to identify not just vulnerable coasts, but process-diagnosed erosion hotspots.

I will now propose a follow-up study that builds on the assessment-scale sea-level literature, coastal forcing reviews, and satellite observation capabilities, while adding a process-linked predictive layer for erosion hotspots.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop a probabilistic 'sediment-deficit shoreline early-warning model' that fuses multi-decadal shoreline-change records from satellite/aerial imagery, downscaled future wave-climate ensembles, relative sea-level-rise scenarios, vertical land-motion estimates, and cell-scale sediment-budget terms into a Bayesian spatiotemporal model that outputs erosion-hotspot probabilities and dominant causal drivers.", "niche_genome": "Open-coast sandy barriers, deltas, and low-lying developed shorelines where erosion risk depends jointly on sea-level rise, changing wave energy/direction, storm exposure, and human-altered sediment supply.", "observation_genome": "The model is expected to outperform extrapolated shoreline-change baselines by identifying hotspots where historical retreat is modest but future wave rotation, rising total water levels, and negative sediment budgets create accelerating erosion risk; it should also distinguish sediment-starved hotspots from primarily water-level-driven hotspots.", "limitation_genome": "Performance will be constrained by uncertainty in nearshore bathymetry, storm-wave projections, unresolved coastal engineering interventions, episodic overwash or inlet dynamics, and sparse sediment-budget measurements in data-poor regions.", "delta_genome": "Where prior work synthesized sea-level hazards, reviewed coastal forcing, or demonstrated satellite monitoring, this study adds an explicitly predictive and diagnostic framework that closes the loop among observed shoreline behavior, projected hydrodynamic forcing, and sediment mass balance at management-relevant coastal-cell scales.", "claim_genome": "Coastal erosion hotspots under sea-level rise can be forecast more reliably when shoreline-change trajectories are interpreted through projected wave forcing and sediment-budget imbalance, rather than treated as simple historical trends or sea-level-exposure maps."}}
```

topic: [earth_science] Constraining subduction zone fluid pathways through seismic anisotropy, geochemical signatures of arc magmas, and thermomechanical mantle wedge models.
full_prompt:
Research topic: Constraining subduction zone fluid pathways through seismic anisotropy, geochemical signatures of arc magmas, and thermomechanical mantle wedge models.

Discipline: earth_science

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I’ll first identify the most relevant recent papers in the provided OpenAlex set, focusing on subduction modeling, slab-fluid transport, and coupled geochemical–geodynamical prediction. This should establish which prior approaches already link physics and chemistry, and where seismic anisotropy can add a new constraint.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "subduction zone fluids mantle wedge geochemistry geodynamics anisotropy", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant recent works. Key hits include oa:W4220896282, “The Subducting Slab as a Chromatographic Column,” which models sub-solidus chemical mass transport in slabs; oa:W3198801818, “A coupled geochemical-geodynamical approach for predicting mantle melting in space and time,” which links geodynamic simulations to melting and geochemical predictions; and oa:W4211030772, “Numerical modeling of subduction: State of the art and future directions,” a broad review of subduction modeling. A broader geophysical context paper, oa:W2807854703, addresses imaging magma plumbing systems.

The slab-as-chromatographic-column paper appears directly relevant to fluid pathways and chemical signatures before melt generation. I’ll read it to understand how it treats solute mobility, lithologic controls, and limitations in connecting slab-fluid transport to arc observations.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W4220896282"}
```

[result]: This 2022 paper investigates sub-solidus mass transport in subducting slabs using thermodynamic-numerical simulations in which major rock components are soluble and mobile to varying degrees. It frames the slab as a chromatographic column, where lithology, hydration state, pressure-temperature path, and fluid-rock interaction govern which elements are mobilized and where they are released. The study emphasizes that slab-derived chemical signals are path-dependent rather than simple functions of depth. A key limitation is that the modeled chemical transport is not fully coupled to mantle-wedge deformation, seismic fabric development, or observed arc-scale anisotropy.

Next I’ll read the coupled geochemical–geodynamical mantle melting paper, since it likely bridges geodynamic models and arc magma chemistry. This can reveal whether prior work already assimilates geochemical observations, and whether seismic anisotropy remains an underused constraint.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3198801818"}
```

[result]: This 2021 paper presents a coupled geochemical-geodynamical framework for predicting mantle melting in space and time. It argues that geodynamic simulations need validation beyond standard geophysical observables and demonstrates how melting predictions can be compared with geochemical signals. The work highlights the promise of integrating mantle flow, thermal structure, and melting, but it treats observational constraints largely through geochemistry and geodynamics rather than explicitly using seismic anisotropy as a joint inverse constraint on mantle-wedge flow and hydrated mineral fabrics.

To reason more explicitly about inheritance and gaps, I’ll extract the structured “genome” of the coupled geochemical–geodynamical paper. This will help formulate a follow-up that keeps its useful coupling but mutates the observational constraint strategy.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W3198801818"}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism_genome: couple geodynamic mantle-flow simulations with geochemical melting calculations to predict where and when melts form. niche_genome: upper-mantle and subduction-related melting where geophysical data alone underconstrain models. observation_genome: modeled melting patterns can be compared to geochemical datasets to evaluate plausibility of mantle dynamics. limitation_genome: limited direct assimilation of multi-observable datasets, especially seismic anisotropy and explicit slab-fluid pathway constraints. delta_genome: moves from purely geodynamic prediction toward geochemically testable mantle models. claim_genome: integrating geochemistry with geodynamics yields more falsifiable predictions of mantle melting than either approach alone.

Before proposing, I’ll do a novelty check on the core mechanism: a joint inversion that uses seismic anisotropy, slab-fluid geochemistry, and thermomechanical mantle-wedge models together. The aim is to avoid simply restating existing geochemical-geodynamical coupling.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Bayesian joint inversion of subduction-zone seismic anisotropy, arc magma trace-element and isotope signatures, and thermomechanical mantle-wedge models to infer time-dependent slab-fluid pathways and permeability structure.", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Verdict: healthy. Similar work exists on geochemical-geodynamical coupling, subduction thermomechanical modeling, and separate seismic anisotropy interpretations, but the closest papers do not appear to jointly invert anisotropy and arc geochemistry for slab-fluid pathway geometry. The idea is connected to oa:W3198801818 through coupled geochemical-geodynamical prediction and to oa:W4220896282 through path-dependent fluid-rock transport, but it adds a distinct multi-observable data-assimilation layer involving anisotropic seismic fabrics.

I’ll now propose a follow-up that inherits coupled geochemical–geodynamical modeling and slab chromatographic transport, but adds seismic-anisotropy data assimilation as an independent constraint on wedge flow, hydration, and permeability. The resulting project is framed as a testable method rather than a purely conceptual synthesis.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop an anisotropy-aware reactive-flow inversion framework for subduction zones: a 3-D thermomechanical mantle-wedge model will simulate slab dehydration, chromatographic fluid-rock transport, mantle hydration, melt generation, and lattice-/shape-preferred orientation fabrics; a Bayesian emulator will jointly fit predicted shear-wave splitting, azimuthal anisotropy, receiver-function anisotropy, and arc magma trace-element/isotope ratios such as Ba/Th, Sr/Y, B/Be, Pb isotopes, and fluid-mobile element enrichments.", "niche_genome": "Subduction zones where dense seismic anisotropy observations and along-arc geochemical datasets coexist, such as Cascadia, Alaska-Aleutians, or Izu-Bonin-Mariana; the target problem is constraining whether slab fluids rise vertically, migrate up-dip along the slab interface, focus through hydrated channels, or are deflected by mantle-wedge corner flow before triggering melting.", "observation_genome": "The expected empirical result is that models fitting geochemistry alone will permit many fluid-pathway geometries, but adding anisotropy will sharply reduce the allowable permeability, hydration, and mantle-flow parameter space; successful models should predict coherent along-arc variations in shear-wave splitting fast directions, delay times, and fluid-mobile geochemical ratios that track modeled fluid-focusing corridors.", "limitation_genome": "The approach will remain nonunique because seismic anisotropy can arise from olivine LPO, serpentinite, aligned melt, or inherited lithospheric fabric, and arc magma chemistry may be modified by crustal storage and assimilation; the first implementation should therefore focus on synthetic recovery tests and one well-instrumented natural transect before global generalization.", "delta_genome": "Compared with prior coupled geochemical-geodynamical melting models, the key change is to assimilate seismic anisotropy as a direct constraint on mantle-wedge deformation, hydration, and melt/fluid alignment; compared with slab chromatographic transport models, the change is to embed path-dependent solute release in a 3-D wedge-flow model whose fluid pathways must also satisfy seismic-fabric observations.", "claim_genome": "Jointly inverting seismic anisotropy and arc geochemistry within thermomechanical reactive-flow models will distinguish competing slab-fluid pathway geometries more robustly than either observable alone, enabling quantitative maps of permeability structure and fluid focusing in the mantle wedge."}}
```

topic: [physics] Investigate turbulence intermittency in magnetized plasmas using high-resolution kinetic simulations and spacecraft data from the Parker Solar Probe. Focus on h
full_prompt:
Research topic: Investigate turbulence intermittency in magnetized plasmas using high-resolution kinetic simulations and spacecraft data from the Parker Solar Probe. Focus on how energy cascades from magnetohydrodynamic scales to ion and electron dissipation scales.

Discipline: physics

Propose a novel follow-up research idea building on relevant prior work.
completion:
I will first identify the most relevant 2018–2025 literature connecting solar-wind multiscale turbulence, kinetic dissipation, and intermittency. The provided OpenAlex candidates include both broad solar-wind cascade context and a specific kinetic-damping diagnostic paper, which are good anchors for a follow-up idea.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "solar wind turbulence intermittency kinetic dissipation Parker Solar Probe", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns several relevant papers. Key candidates include `oa:W2942087474` “The multi-scale nature of the solar wind” (2019), a broad review of solar-wind plasma scales and cascade physics, and `oa:W2914068512` “Evidence for electron Landau damping in space plasma turbulence” (2019), which applies field-particle correlations to identify electron-scale damping. Other returned papers are less central to Parker Solar Probe solar-wind intermittency, including works on magnetotail activity, Kelvin–Helmholtz turbulence, and E×B discharges.

I will read the multiscale solar-wind paper first because it likely frames the cascade from MHD to ion and electron scales and clarifies the observational scale hierarchy relevant to PSP.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2942087474"}
```

[result]: `oa:W2942087474`, “The multi-scale nature of the solar wind” (2019), describes the solar wind as a magnetized, weakly collisional plasma with coupled dynamics across MHD, ion-kinetic, and electron-kinetic scales. The paper emphasizes characteristic plasma length scales, turbulent cascade phenomenology, spectral breaks, anisotropy, and the importance of linking macroscopic fluctuations to kinetic dissipation. It highlights that spacecraft measurements can resolve different portions of this hierarchy but that connecting intermittent structures across scales remains difficult.

Next I will read the electron Landau damping paper because it provides a concrete kinetic diagnostic for dissipation, which could be combined with intermittency and PSP observations.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W2914068512"}
```

[result]: `oa:W2914068512`, “Evidence for electron Landau damping in space plasma turbulence” (2019), addresses how turbulent energy is dissipated in weakly collisional space plasma. It uses the field-particle correlation technique to detect velocity-space signatures consistent with electron Landau damping. The paper shows that kinetic damping can be diagnosed directly from distribution functions and fields, rather than inferred only from spectra or heating rates.

I will extract the structured genome of the electron Landau damping paper to identify which parts of its method can be inherited and which gaps remain for a PSP-focused intermittency study.

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "oa:W2914068512"}
```

[result]: The extracted genome identifies the core mechanism as field-particle correlations applied to simultaneous field and particle measurements to reveal velocity-space energy transfer. Its niche is weakly collisional space-plasma turbulence at kinetic scales, especially electron dissipation. Its main limitation is that the diagnostic is often applied to selected intervals and does not fully condition dissipation on multiscale intermittency, coherent structures, or the full MHD-to-electron cascade pathway.

Before proposing, I will check whether a mechanism combining intermittency-conditioned PSP observations with kinetic simulations and field-particle correlations appears novel rather than redundant.

```action

[result]: Verdict: healthy. The closest prior work includes broad multiscale solar-wind cascade reviews and field-particle correlation studies of electron damping, but the specific combination of PSP near-Sun intervals, intermittency-conditioned cascade tracking, and matched high-resolution kinetic simulations is not found as a redundant existing contribution. The result appears connected to prior literature while leaving room for a novel integrated framework.

I will now propose a follow-up project that inherits the multiscale cascade framing and field-particle correlation diagnostics, but mutates them into an intermittency-conditioned PSP-plus-simulation program focused on causal energy flow through ion and electron scales.
